This is essentially the journey of Quality Management — from *finding defects* to *preventing defects* to *designing quality in* to *predicting and automating quality*. ### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics to Remember - **DPMO** — Defects Per Million Opportunities (Six Sigma benchmark). - **σ (Sigma Level)** — Standard deviation, the unit of process capability. - **COPQ** — Cost of Poor Quality (Prevention + Appraisal + Internal Failure + External Failure). - **AQL** — Acceptable Quality Level (in acceptance sampling). - **TPM** — Total Productive Maintenance (equipment-driven quality). ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Four Eras of Quality Evolution (Structured Logic) > [!IMPORTANT] > KTU examiners love framing a 7-mark question as: *"Explain the evolution of quality management with eras."* Memorize the four-era structure below. #### Era 1 — Inspection Era (Pre-1920s: Craft → Industrial Revolution) - **Why it emerged**: Mass production (after 1900, Frederick W. Taylor's *Scientific Management*) made one-by-one craftsman inspection impossible. - **How it worked**: Products were inspected *after* manufacture using gauges, sorting good from bad. - **Mindset**: **"Detect and reject."** - **Limitation**: No feedback to the process; defects kept recurring. #### Era 2 — Quality Control (QC) Era (1920s–1940s) - **Why it emerged**: Walter A. Shewhart (Bell Labs, 1924) introduced **Statistical Quality Control (SQC)** — the *Control Chart*. - **How it worked**: Sampling and statistical monitoring *during* production. - **Mindset**: **"Monitor and control the process."** - **Key tool**: **Shewhart Control Chart** ($\bar{X}, R, p, c$ charts). - **Limitation**: QC was still the responsibility of a separate department (not the operator). #### Era 3 — Quality Assurance (QA) Era (1950s–1970s) - **Why it emerged**: Post-WWII Japanese reconstruction (Deming, Juran invited to Japan in 1950); need to embed quality into the *system* rather than rely on inspection. - **How it worked**: Documented procedures, standards (e.g., **BS 5750**, later **ISO 9000** family in 1987), audits, process design. - **Mindset**: **"Build quality in."** - **Key contribution**: **Armand V. Feigenbaum** coined **Total Quality Control (TQC)** in 1956 — quality is everyone's job, not just the QC department. #### Era 4 — Total Quality Management (TQM) Era (1980s–2000s) - **Why it emerged**: Global competition (Japanese cars, electronics) forced Western firms to adopt Japanese methods. - **How it worked**: Customer-focused, continuous improvement (**Kaizen**), employee involvement (Quality Circles), supplier partnership. - **Mindset**: **"Quality is a strategic, organization-wide commitment."** - **Key contribution**: **Malcolm Baldrige National Quality Award (MBNQA)** established in USA in 1987; **Deming Prize** in Japan since 1951. #### Era 5 — Six Sigma & Lean Era (1986 onwards) - **Why it emerged**: Motorola (1986, Bill Smith) needed a metric-driven methodology to slash defect rates. - **How it worked**: **DMAIC** (Define → Measure → Analyze → Improve → Control) using statistical tools. - **Mindset**: **"Reduce variation; achieve 3.4 defects per million opportunities (DPMO)."** #### Era 6 — Digital / Industry 4.0 Quality (2010s onwards) - **Why it emerged**: IoT, AI/ML, Big Data, Cyber-Physical Systems. - **How it worked**: Real-time sensor data, predictive quality, digital twins, smart manufacturing. - **Mindset**: **"Predict and self-correct in real time."** --- ### 2.2 The Quality Gurus & Their High-Yield Contributions > [!NOTE] > This is the single most-tested sub-topic in KTU QEM papers. A 14-mark question often asks: *"Explain the contributions of any two quality gurus."* | Guru | Year / Origin | Core Contribution | Famous Phrase / Tool | |---|---|---|---| | **Frederick W. Taylor** | 1911, USA | *Scientific Management*; separated planning from execution; birth of inspection-based quality. | Time & motion studies | | **Walter A. Shewhart** | 1924, USA (Bell Labs) | Father of **Statistical Quality Control**; invented the **Control Chart** and **PDCA cycle**. | "Data are not information." | | **W. Edwards Deming** | 1950s, USA → Japan | **14 Points for Management**; **System of Profound Knowledge**; championed PDCA in Japan. | "Survival is optional. No one has to make changes to survive." | | **Joseph M. Juran** | 1950s, USA → Japan | **Juran's Quality Trilogy** — Quality Planning, Quality Control, Quality Improvement. | "Fitness for use." | | **Armand V. Feigenbaum** | 1956, USA | Coined **Total Quality Control (TQC)**. | "Quality is everyone's job." | | **Philip B. Crosby** | 1979, USA | **Zero Defects**; concept of **Quality is Free**; *Quality Vaccine*. | "Quality is free. It's not a gift, but it is free." | | **Kaoru Ishikawa** | 1960s, Japan | **Quality Circles**; **Fishbone (Ishikawa) Diagram**; company-wide quality control (CWQC). | "Quality begins and ends with education." | | **Genichi Taguchi** | 1960s, Japan | **Quality Loss Function**; **Robust Design**; concept of *loss to society*. | $L(x) = k(x-T)^2$ | | **Shigeo Shingo** | 1980s, Japan | **SMED** (Single-Minute Exchange of Dies); **Poka-Yoke** (mistake-proofing). | Zero defects via system design | --- ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Formula / Concept | Expression | Meaning | Unit / Typical Value | |---|---|---|---| | **Taguchi's Quality Loss Function** | $L(x) = k(x - T)^2$ | Monetary loss to society when quality characteristic deviates from target $T$ | Currency (e.g., ₹, \$) | | **Constant $k$ in Loss Function** | $k = \dfrac{A_0}{\Delta_0^2}$ | $A_0$ = cost of replacement at tolerance limit $\Delta_0$ | Currency / unit² | | **DPMO (Defects Per Million Opportunities)** | $\text{DPMO} = \dfrac{\text{Defects} \times 10^6}{\text{Units} \times \text{Opportunities/Unit}}$ | Six Sigma metric | defects / million | | **Process Sigma Level** | $\sigma = \dfrac{\text{USL} - \mu}{3 \cdot s}$ (or) $\sigma = \dfrac{\text{Tolerance}}{6 \cdot s}$ | Process capability in σ units | σ (dimensionless) | | **Six Sigma Target** | $\sigma \geq 6 \Rightarrow \text{DPMO} \leq 3.4$ | World-class benchmark | 3.4 DPMO | | **Cost of Poor Quality (COPQ)** | $\text{COPQ} = P_v + A_p + I_f + E_f$ | $P_v$=Prevention, $A_p$=Appraisal, $I_f$=Internal failure, $E_f$=External failure | Currency | | **Yield (First-Pass Yield)** | $\text{FPY} = \dfrac{\text{Good Units at First Pass}}{\text{Total Units Entered}}$ | % of defect-free output | 0–100 % | | **Rolled Throughput Yield (RTY)** | $\text{RTY} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} \text{FPY}_i$ | Multi-stage cumulative yield | 0–1 | | **Pareto 80/20 Principle** | $80\%$ of problems from $20\%$ of causes | Vilfredo Pareto's rule | % | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Latex Isolation Safeguard Applied:** All absolute value bars and conditions in tables use $\vert$ and superscripts to keep the markdown table parser safe. --- ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Automotive (Toyota, Ford, Tesla)**: TQM + Six Sigma + Lean + Industry 4.0 for zero-defect assembly lines. - **Aerospace (Boeing, Airbus)**: AS9100 QA standards, Six Sigma DMAIC for safety-critical parts. - **Software (Google, Microsoft)**: CMMI (Capability Maturity Model Integration), derived from Deming's quality philosophy. - **Healthcare (Hospitals)**: Six Sigma reduces surgical errors; Lean reduces patient wait time. - **Electronics (Apple, Samsung)**: Taguchi's robust design minimizes field failures of chips and batteries. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of Taguchi's Quality Loss Function > [!NOTE] > Taguchi reframed "quality" not as "meeting specifications" but as **"minimizing loss to society"**. This is a very common KTU 14-mark derivation. **Step 1 — Define the loss.** When a quality characteristic $x$ deviates from its target value $T$, the product causes loss $L(x)$ to the customer, manufacturer, and society. **Step 2 — State assumptions.** Taguchi assumed the loss function is: - Always non-negative: $L(x) \geq 0$. - Minimum (zero) at the target: $L(T) = 0$. - Continuous and differentiable. - Symmetric about the target. - Monotonically increasing as $\vert x - T \vert$ grows. These conditions force the loss to be a quadratic in $(x-T)$. **Step 3 — Assume quadratic form.** $$ L(x) = k(x - T)^2 $$ **Step 4 — Determine the constant $k$ from boundary condition.** At the *tolerance limit* $\Delta_0$, the customer incurs a loss equal to the cost of replacing the product, $A_0$. $$ A_0 = L(T \pm \Delta_0) = k(\Delta_0)^2 $$ **Step 5 — Solve for $k$.** $$ k = \dfrac{A_0}{(\Delta_0)^2} $$ **Step 6 — Final loss function.** $$ \boxed{L(x) = \dfrac{A_0}{(\Delta_0)^2}\,(x - T)^2} $$ **Interpretation (engineering meaning):** - Loss grows *parabolically* with deviation — small drifts still cost money, even inside the specification band. - This contradicts the classical *conformance-only* view and is the philosophical basis of **Robust Design** and **Taguchi Methods**. --- ### 3.2 Analytical Worked Example: DPMO & Sigma Level Conversion > [!IMPORTANT] > Frequently asked: *"5000 units, each with 4 opportunities, 60 defects found. Calculate DPMO and Sigma Level."* **Given:** - Units produced $N = 5000$ - Opportunities per unit $O = 4$ - Total defects $D = 60$ **Step 1 — Compute total opportunities.** $$ \text{Total Opportunities} = N \times O = 5000 \times 4 = 20{,}000 $$ **Step 2 — Compute DPMO.** $$ \text{DPMO} = \dfrac{D \times 10^6}{N \times O} = \dfrac{60 \times 10^6}{20{,}000} = 3000 $$ **Step 3 — Convert DPMO to Yield (% defective).** $$ \text{Defective Rate} = \dfrac{3000}{10^6} \times 100\% = 0.30\% $$ **Step 4 — Convert to Yield.** $$ \text{Yield} = 100\% - 0.30\% = 99.70\% $$ **Step 5 — Use Six Sigma conversion table (standard Motorola table, with 1.5σ shift).** - 99.70% yield ≈ **4.5 σ level**. **Interpretation:** A process producing 60 defects per 20,000 opportunities operates at roughly 4.5 sigma — good but not yet Six Sigma world-class. --- ### 3.3 Comparison Matrix: QC vs. QA vs. TQM vs. Six Sigma > [!NOTE] > This 4-column comparison is the KTU examiner's favourite 7-mark matrix question. | Dimension | Quality Control (QC) | Quality Assurance (QA) | TQM | Six Sigma | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Era** | 1920s–1940s | 1950s–1970s | 1980s–2000s | 1986 onwards | | **Focus** | Product | Process | System | Variation | | **Approach** | Inspect & sort | Document & audit | Strategize & engage | Measure & analyze | | **Responsibility** | QC department | Cross-functional teams | Everyone in organization | Trained Black/Green Belts + leadership | | **Key Driver** | Shewhart | Feigenbaum | Deming, Juran, Ishikawa | Bill Smith (Motorola), Jack Welch (GE) | | **Tools** | Control charts, sampling | Standards (ISO 9000), audits | PDCA, QCC, Fishbone, 7 QC tools | DMAIC, DOE, regression, hypothesis testing | | **Metric** | % defective | Conformance to standards | Customer satisfaction, TQCost | DPMO, σ level, COPQ | | **Mindset** | Reactive → preventive | Preventive → systemic | Strategic & cultural | Data-driven & statistical | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Evolution Timeline of Quality Management ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Inspection Era
Pre-1920s
Taylor: Scientific Mgmt] --> B[Quality Control Era
1920s-1940s
Shewhart: Control Charts] B --> C[Quality Assurance Era
1950s-1970s
Feigenbaum: TQC] C --> D[TQM Era
1980s-2000s
Deming Juran Ishikawa Crosby] D --> E[Six Sigma and Lean Era
1986 onwards
Motorola Bill Smith] E --> F[Industry 4.0 Quality
2010s onwards
IoT AI ML Digital Twin] G[Craftsman Skill
One-by-one Check] -.-> A H[Mass Production
Interchangeable Parts] -.-> A I[WW2 Quality Needs
Statistical Methods] -.-> B J[Japan Reconstruction
Deming Juran Lectures] -.-> C K[Japanese Auto Threat
to US Firms] -.-> D L[Data Driven
Decision Making] -.-> E M[Sensors Cloud
Predictive Quality] -.-> F ``` ### 4.2 Block Diagram: Deming's PDCA Cycle (Shewhart Cycle) ```mermaid flowchart TD P1[Plan
Identify problem and plan change] --> D1 D1[Do
Implement the change on small scale] --> C1 C1[Check
Study results from test] --> A1 A1[Act
Adopt or abandon based on results] --> P1 ``` ### 4.3 Block Diagram: Juran's Quality Trilogy ```mermaid flowchart LR QP[Quality Planning
Identify customers and needs] --> QC QC[Quality Control
Evaluate actual vs goals and act] --> QI QI[Quality Improvement
Establish infrastructure for improvement] --> QP ``` ### 4.4 Block Diagram: Six Sigma DMAIC Framework ```mermaid flowchart LR D2[Define
Problem and customer CTQs] --> M2 M2[Measure
Current process capability] --> A2 A2[Analyze
Root cause analysis] --> I2 I2[Improve
Implement solutions] --> C2 C2[Control
Sustain improvements] --> D2 ``` ### 4.5 Block Diagram: Cost of Poor Quality (COPQ) Structure ```mermaid flowchart TB COQ[Cost of Quality] --> CON[Conformance Cost] COQ --> NCO[Non-Conformance Cost] CON --> PREV[Prevention Cost] CON --> APPR[Appraisal Cost] NCO --> INTF[Internal Failure Cost] NCO --> EXTF[External Failure Cost] ``` ### 4.6 Block Diagram: Taguchi Quality Loss Curve ```mermaid flowchart TD LMIN[L equals 0 at Target T] --> INSIDE[Inside spec limits still has loss] INSIDE --> OUTSIDE[Outside spec limits loss grows faster] OUTSIDE --> MAXL[Maximum acceptable loss A0 at tolerance Delta0] ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** *Define Total Quality Management (TQM). List any four of its key elements.* **Model Answer:** TQM is a management philosophy that seeks to integrate all organizational functions — marketing, design, production, service — to focus on meeting customer needs and organizational objectives. It views quality as a strategic, organization-wide responsibility. Four key elements: 1. **Customer focus** — understand and exceed customer expectations. 2. **Total employee involvement / participation** (e.g., Quality Circles). 3. **Continuous improvement** (Kaizen / PDCA). 4. **Fact-based decision making** (statistical tools, data analysis). 5. **Supplier partnership** (long-term, mutually beneficial). *(Any 4 — 3 marks; 1 for definition + 2 for elements)* --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *State the difference between Quality Control (QC) and Quality Assurance (QA).* **Model Answer:** | Aspect | Quality Control | Quality Assurance | |---|---|---| | Focus | Product (find defects) | Process (prevent defects) | | Nature | Reactive | Preventive | | Tools | Inspection, control charts | Standards, audits, documentation | | Era | 1920s–40s (Shewhart) | 1950s–70s (Feigenbaum) | | Responsibility | QC department | Entire organization | *(Any 3 differences — 3 marks)* --- ### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions --- **Q3. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]** **(a)** *Explain the evolution of quality management highlighting the four major eras. (7 marks)* **(b)** *Discuss the contributions of Deming and Juran to the field of quality management. (7 marks)* **Model Solution:** **(a) Four major eras of quality evolution (7 marks):** 1. **Inspection Era (Pre-1920s):** Emerged with mass production; Frederick Taylor's *Scientific Management* separated planning from execution. Quality meant end-of-line inspection. Reactive — defects were detected, not prevented. 2. **Quality Control Era (1920s–1940s):** Walter Shewhart's *Statistical Quality Control* (1924) introduced control charts and sampling. Inspection moved into the process using statistical tools. Reactive still — but now data-driven. 3. **Quality Assurance Era (1950s–1970s):** Focus shifted to the *system*. Armand Feigenbaum coined **Total Quality Control** (1956). Deming & Juran lectured in Japan (1950). Standards like **BS 5750** / **ISO 9000** institutionalized process documentation and audits. Preventive. 4. **Total Quality Management Era (1980s–2000s):** Customer focus, continuous improvement (Kaizen), employee involvement (Quality Circles by Ishikawa), strategic leadership commitment. Malcolm Baldrige Award (1987) institutionalized TQM in the US. TQM integrated quality into corporate strategy. *(Each era ~1.5 marks; ~1 mark for conclusion.)* **(b) Contributions of Deming and Juran (7 marks):** **W. Edwards Deming (1900–1993):** - **14 Points for Management** — philosophy emphasizing systems thinking, continuous improvement, elimination of slogans, training, leadership. - **System of Profound Knowledge** — 4 interrelated parts: Appreciation for a System, Knowledge of Variation, Theory of Knowledge, Psychology. - **PDCA (Plan–Do–Check–Act)** cycle, popularized from Shewhart, became the backbone of modern quality improvement. - **Deming Prize (1951)** in Japan institutionalized the philosophy. - Statistical process control tools (control charts) applied to industrial processes. **Joseph M. Juran (1904–2008):** - **Juran's Quality Trilogy** — Quality Planning (identify customers, set goals), Quality Control (evaluate performance, act on gaps), Quality Improvement (establish infrastructure for ongoing improvement). - **"Fitness for Use"** definition of quality — broadened quality beyond defect counts to customer satisfaction. - **Pareto Principle applied to quality** (the "vital few" defects). - **Juran's Quality Handbook** became the global reference text. *(3.5 marks each guru — 1 mark for biographical context, 2 marks for core contributions, 0.5 mark for impact/legacy.)* --- ### 5.3 Part B — Alternative Question Choice (Internal Choice) --- **Q4. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Alternate]** **(a)** *Explain in detail Philip Crosby's "Quality is Free" philosophy and the concept of Zero Defects. (7 marks)* **(b)** *Derive Taguchi's Quality Loss Function and explain its engineering significance. (7 marks)* **Model Solution:** **(a) Crosby's "Quality is Free" and Zero Defects (7 marks):** Philip Crosby, in his 1979 book *Quality is Free*, argued that the cost of *preventing* defects is always lower than the cost of *correcting* them. His core ideas: 1. **The Five Absolutes of Quality:** - Quality = **Conformance to requirements** (not "goodness"). - Prevention, not inspection. - Zero defects performance standard. - Measure quality by **price of non-conformance** (PONC). - Quality is a **system**, not a department. 2. **Cost of Quality (Conformance vs Non-Conformance):** - Price of conformance (POC) = Prevention + Appraisal. - Price of non-conformance (PONC) = Failure costs. - Doing things right the first time *frees* money otherwise lost to rework, scrap, warranty. 3. **Zero Defects (ZD):** - Not a motivational slogan but a *standard of performance*. - Means *doing it right the first time*, every time. - Implemented via: management commitment, education, correction, recognition, measurement. 4. **"Quality Vaccine":** Crosby's 5-step immunization — determination, education, implementation, recognition, communication. **Engineering Impact:** Adopted by Martin Marietta, IBM, and many defence/aerospace firms in the 1980s; influenced modern zero-defect programs in automotive (Toyota, Ford Q1). --- **(b) Taguchi's Quality Loss Function — Full Derivation (7 marks):** **Step 1 — Definition of Quality:** Taguchi defined quality as *"the loss a product causes to society after being shipped, other than any losses caused by its intrinsic functions."* **Step 2 — Assumptions:** - Loss $L(x)$ is a function of deviation from target $T$. - $L(T) = 0$ (no loss at target). - $L(x) \geq 0$ everywhere. - $L(x)$ is continuous, symmetric, and increases monotonically as $\vert x - T \vert$ increases. **Step 3 — Quadratic form:** The simplest function satisfying these constraints is: $$ L(x) = k(x - T)^2 $$ **Step 4 — Boundary condition:** At the customer-defined tolerance limit $\Delta_0$, the loss equals the replacement cost $A_0$: $$ A_0 = k(\Delta_0)^2 $$ **Step 5 — Solve for $k$:** $$ k = \dfrac{A_0}{(\Delta_0)^2} $$ **Step 6 — Final Loss Function:** $$ \boxed{L(x) = \dfrac{A_0}{(\Delta_0)^2}\,(x - T)^2} $$ **Engineering significance:** - Inside the specification band, *any* deviation still incurs loss — contradicts the classical "inside-spec-is-OK" view. - Loss grows **parabolically**; even 50% deviation costs 25% of $A_0$, not zero. - Foundation of **Robust Design** — design products whose performance is insensitive to noise factors. - Used to compute *quality cost* in automotive, electronics, and semiconductor industries. --- ### 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Where students lose marks on Evolution of Quality Management:** > > 1. **Mixing gurus with eras** — Crosby is *not* part of TQM era founders (he bridges QA→TQM). Mixing timelines costs 1–2 marks. > 2. **Writing only definitions, no examples** — A 14-mark question on Deming MUST include both 14 Points *and* System of Profound Knowledge. Mentioning only PDCA fetches only 2 of 7 marks. > 3. **Skipping the derivation of $k$** — In Taguchi's loss function, students often jump to $L(x)=k(x-T)^2$ without deriving the constant. Always show $k = A_0/\Delta_0^2$. > 4. **Confusing Quality Control with Quality Assurance** — the most common 3-mark question; examiners specifically test if students know QC = *product*, QA = *process*. > 5. **Forgetting Shewhart** — Shewhart is the *father* of SQC; he invented both the control chart *and* PDCA. Deming popularized PDCA, not invented it. Confusing them loses marks. > 6. **Not mentioning the 1.5σ shift** — In Six Sigma, 3.4 DPMO comes from a 1.5σ long-term shift assumption. Without it, the number looks arbitrary. KTU examiners reward this. > 7. **Skipping Industry 4.0** — KTU 2024 scheme includes modern trends; mentioning IoT, AI, Digital Twin earns bonus marks even if not explicitly asked. --- ### 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Evolution of Quality Management** - ☐ **Four eras**: Inspection → QC → QA → TQM → Six Sigma → Industry 4.0. - ☐ **Inspection** = reactive; **QC** = statistical monitoring; **QA** = process/system; **TQM** = strategy/culture; **Six Sigma** = data/variation. - ☐ **Frederick Taylor** = Scientific Management (1911), separated planning from execution. - ☐ **Walter Shewhart** = Father of SQC; invented Control Chart (1924) and PDCA. - ☐ **W. Edwards Deming** = 14 Points, System of Profound Knowledge, PDCA popularizer; Deming Prize (1951, Japan). - ☐ **Joseph Juran** = Quality Trilogy (Planning, Control, Improvement); "Fitness for Use"; Pareto for quality. - ☐ **Armand Feigenbaum** = Total Quality Control (1956). - ☐ **Philip Crosby** = "Quality is Free"; Zero Defects; Price of Non-Conformance (PONC). - ☐ **Kaoru Ishikawa** = Quality Circles; Fishbone Diagram; Company-Wide QC (CWQC). - ☐ **Genichi Taguchi** = Loss Function $L(x) = k(x-T)^2$; Robust Design. - ☐ **Shigeo Shingo** = SMED; Poka-Yoke. - ☐ **Six Sigma** = 3.4 DPMO; DMAIC; 1.5σ shift; Motorola (1986), GE (1995). - ☐ **Cost of Quality** = Prevention + Appraisal + Internal Failure + External Failure. - ☐ **Taguchi Loss Function**: $L(x) = \dfrac{A_0}{(\Delta_0)^2}(x-T)^2$. - ☐ **DPMO Formula**: $\dfrac{D \times 10^6}{N \times O}$. - ☐ **Process sigma**: $\sigma = \dfrac{\text{Tolerance}}{6 \cdot s}$. - ☐ **RTY** = product of stage FPYs. - ☐ **Modern era** = IoT, AI/ML, Digital Twin, Predictive Quality, Industry 4.0. - ☐ **Awards**: Deming Prize (Japan), Malcolm Baldrige (USA), EFQM (Europe). --- *End of Module 1 — Evolution of Quality Management | KTU PEMET758 | 2024 Scheme*

topics.title: Quality Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET758 - QUALITY ENGINEERING AND MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Quality Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition of Quality Control

**Quality Control (QC)** is the operational arm of quality management that deals with the **fulfillment of quality requirements** through the systematic activities of **measuring, examining, testing, and inspecting** a product, process, or service against defined standards or specifications, followed by corrective action when non-conformities are detected.

As per the universally accepted ISO/ASQ vocabulary, Quality Control is defined as:

> "The operational techniques and activities that are used to fulfill requirements for quality."

In a KTU 2024 Scheme context, Quality Control sits as a **sub-system** under the broader umbrella of **Quality Management (QM)** and is distinct from **Quality Assurance (QA)**.

$$ \text{Quality Management (QM)} = \text{Quality Planning} + \text{Quality Control (QC)} + \text{Quality Assurance (QA)} $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Quality Control is the **"doing"** part of quality — it consists of day-to-day operational activities like inspection, testing, and statistical monitoring that ensure products leaving the line conform to specification.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a **head chef in a large restaurant chain**:

- **Quality Planning** = Designing the menu, recipes, and standards of taste.
- **Quality Control** = Tasting the soup *before* it goes out to the customer. You *check* every batch, and if it is too salty, you throw it out or add water. You are operating at the **operational level**, hands-on.
- **Quality Assurance** = Creating the SOP (Standard Operating Procedure) that says "every chef must add exactly 5 grams of salt per liter of soup" and training chefs to follow it. You are operating at the **system level**, preventing the salty-soup problem from ever happening.

**Quality Control is the taste-test.** It is reactive, tactical, and inspection-based.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Three Pillars of Quality Practice:**
> 1. **Quality Control (QC)** → Inspection-oriented, identifies defects.
> 2. **Quality Assurance (QA)** → Process-oriented, prevents defects.
> 3. **Quality Improvement (QI)** → Continuous improvement (Kaizen, Six Sigma).

## 1.3 Quality Control vs. Quality Assurance — The Distinction

| Parameter | Quality Control (QC) | Quality Assurance (QA) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Focus** | Product / Output | Process / System |
| **Nature** | Reactive (find & reject defects) | Preventive (build quality in) |
| **Approach** | Inspection, testing, sorting | Planning, documentation, audits |
| **Time Frame** | Short-term, operational | Long-term, strategic |
| **Responsible** | Operators, inspectors, QC engineers | All departments, top management |
| **Tool Kit** | Control charts, sampling plans, gauges | FMEA, audits, ISO 9001, 5S |
| **Cost Implication** | Cost of inspection, rework, scrap | Cost of prevention (lower long-term) |

## 1.4 Core Definitions of Standard QC Metrics (KTU High-Yield)

- **Specification Limit (SL):** The numerical boundary (upper or lower) stated in the design specification. Typically **USL** (Upper Specification Limit) and **LSL** (Lower Specification Limit).
- **Tolerance:** The total allowable variation in a measurement, given by $T = USL - LSL$.
- **Conformance:** A product is in *conformance* if it lies within the specification limits.
- **Non-conformance (Defect):** A departure of a quality characteristic from its intended level such that it fails to meet specification.
- **Variable Data:** Measurable on a continuous scale (length, weight, voltage). Symbol: **X**.
- **Attribute Data:** Binary — conforms or does not conform (go/no-go, pass/fail). Symbol: **p, c, np**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Constants / Standard Metrics in QC:**
> - **3σ Limit** → 99.73% of items within (Control Chart)
> - **6σ Spread** → 99.9999998% (Six Sigma standard)
> - **AQL (Acceptable Quality Level)** → Worst tolerable process average
> - **LTPD (Lot Tolerance Percent Defective)** → Rejectable quality level
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Pillars of Modern Quality Control

Quality Control, in its modern industrial form, is built on two fundamental pillars:

### 2.1.1 Acceptance Sampling (Lot-by-Lot Inspection)
Deciding **whether to accept or reject a lot** of incoming material based on a sample drawn from the lot. This is **economic QC** when 100% inspection is too costly, destructive, or impossible.

**Key Logic Steps (the "Why" and "How"):**
1. A lot of size **N** arrives.
2. A random sample of size **n** is drawn.
3. Each item is inspected and the number of defectives **d** is counted.
4. If $d \leq c$ (acceptance number), the lot is accepted; otherwise rejected.
5. The probability of acceptance $P_a$ is governed by the **Operating Characteristic (OC) Curve**.

### 2.1.2 Statistical Process Control (SPC)
Monitoring the **process itself** in real-time using **Control Charts** to detect *special cause* variation and signal when intervention is needed. This is the **Shewhart legacy**.

**Key Logic Steps:**
1. A subgroup of size **n** measurements is taken at regular intervals.
2. Subgroup mean $\bar{X}$ and range R are calculated.
3. Process averages $\bar{\bar{X}}$ and $\bar{R}$ are computed from k subgroups.
4. Control limits are drawn at $\pm 3\sigma$ from the central line.
5. If a sample point plots outside the control limits → the process is **out of statistical control**.

## 2.2 The Seven Tools of Quality Control (7-QC Tools)

Kaoru Ishikawa identified the **seven basic tools** used by shop-floor workers for problem-solving and QC:

1. **Cause-and-Effect Diagram** (Fishbone / Ishikawa Diagram)
2. **Check Sheet** (Structured data collection form)
3. **Control Chart** (SPC tool)
4. **Histogram** (Distribution visualization)
5. **Pareto Chart** (80/20 rule application)
6. **Scatter Diagram** (Correlation between two variables)
7. **Flowchart** (Process mapping)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "3σ"?** Walter Shewhart proved that the **3-sigma** control limits provide an **economic balance** between the risk of searching for trouble that is not present (Type I error) and the risk of not noticing trouble that is present (Type II error). At 3σ, 99.73% of in-control points will fall within the limits.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Master QC Formula Table** — Memorize the boundary conditions and units for each.

| # | Chart / Metric | Central Line (CL) | Upper Control Limit (UCL) | Lower Control Limit (LCL) | Constants Used |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $\bar{X}$ Chart (mean) | $\bar{\bar{X}}$ | $\bar{\bar{X}} + A_2 \bar{R}$ | $\bar{\bar{X}} - A_2 \bar{R}$ | $A_2$ |
| 2 | R Chart (range) | $\bar{R}$ | $D_4 \bar{R}$ | $D_3 \bar{R}$ | $D_3, D_4$ |
| 3 | $\bar{X}$ (with $\sigma$) | $\bar{\bar{X}}$ | $\bar{\bar{X}} + A \bar{R}$ or $\bar{X} + 3\sigma$ | $\bar{\bar{X}} - A \bar{R}$ | $A = 3/(\sqrt{n} \cdot d_2)$ |
| 4 | p Chart (fraction defective) | $\bar{p}$ | $\bar{p} + 3\sqrt{\bar{p}(1-\bar{p})/n}$ | $\bar{p} - 3\sqrt{\bar{p}(1-\bar{p})/n}$ | none |
| 5 | np Chart (number defective) | $n\bar{p}$ | $n\bar{p} + 3\sqrt{n\bar{p}(1-\bar{p})}$ | $n\bar{p} - 3\sqrt{n\bar{p}(1-\bar{p})}$ | none |
| 6 | c Chart (defects/unit) | $\bar{c}$ | $\bar{c} + 3\sqrt{\bar{c}}$ | $\bar{c} - 3\sqrt{\bar{c}}$ | none |
| 7 | u Chart (defects/sample) | $\bar{u}$ | $\bar{u} + 3\sqrt{\bar{u}/n}$ | $\bar{u} - 3\sqrt{\bar{u}/n}$ | none |
| 8 | Process Capability $C_p$ | — | $C_p = (USL - LSL) / (6\sigma)$ | — | $C_p \geq 1.33$ acceptable |
| 9 | Process Capability $C_{pk}$ | — | $C_{pk} = \min[(USL-\mu)/(3\sigma),\, (\mu-LSL)/(3\sigma)]$ | — | $C_{pk} \geq 1.33$ acceptable |
| 10 | Process Performance $P_p$ | — | $P_p = (USL - LSL) / (6s)$ | — | uses s, not σ |
| 11 | AQL, LTPD (Sampling) | — | $P_a$ at AQL $\geq 0.90$ (Producer's risk $\alpha=0.10$) | $P_a$ at LTPD $\leq 0.10$ (Consumer's risk $\beta=0.10$) | — |
| 12 | AOQ (Average Outgoing Quality) | — | $AOQ = P_a \cdot p \cdot (N-n)/(N)$ | — | — |

**Where the Greek & Roman letters mean:**
- $\bar{\bar{X}}$ = Grand average of subgroup means (sometimes written $\bar{X}_{grand}$)
- $\bar{R}$ = Average of subgroup ranges
- $\bar{p}$ = Average fraction defective across all subgroups
- $\bar{c}$ = Average number of defects per unit
- $A_2, D_3, D_4, d_2$ = Tabulated factors depending on sample size n

> [!WARNING]
> **Table Syntax Rule:** The table above uses `\vert` for "or" conditions in the $C_{pk}$ formula to avoid breaking markdown table parsing. Do not use the vertical bar character $\vert$ inside table rows.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility of Quality Control

| Industry | Application of QC |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Automotive (e.g., Toyota, Tata Motors)** | SPC on weld strength, paint thickness, torque |
| **Semiconductor Fabrication** | Control of line-width, defect density (defects/cm²) |
| **Pharmaceuticals** | cGMP-compliant sampling, batch release via AQL |
| **Aerospace (Boeing, HAL)** | AS9100 standards, ultra-tight Cpk > 1.67 |
| **Civil Engineering (Concrete)** | Cube compressive strength via $\bar{X}$-R chart |
| **Software (IEEE 730)** | Defect tracking, code-review acceptance |
| **Food Processing (FSSAI, Kerala Dairies)** | Microbial count via c chart, weight via $\bar{X}$ chart |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** In Kerala's spice export industry, attribute charts like the **c chart** are used to count insect fragments or foreign matter per 100g batch — a direct application of statistical QC.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the $\bar{X}$-R Control Chart Limits

The $\bar{X}$-R chart is a two-chart system: one for the **process mean** ($\bar{X}$-chart) and one for **process variability** (R-chart). The R-chart is plotted *first* because the $\bar{X}$-chart limits depend on $\bar{R}$.

### 3.1.1 Why R and not σ on the shop floor?

Computing standard deviation requires squaring deviations and taking a square root — tedious without a calculator. The **range R = X_max − X_min** of a small subgroup (typically n = 5) is a **statistically efficient** estimator of σ for small n.

**Theoretical Bridge — R to σ:**

For a subgroup of size **n** drawn from a normal distribution with standard deviation σ, the expected value of the range is:

$$ E[R] = d_2 \cdot \sigma $$

where **$d_2$** is a constant tabulated for each n. Therefore:

$$ \hat{\sigma} = \frac{\bar{R}}{d_2} $$

### 3.1.2 Derivation of the $\bar{X}$-Chart UCL and LCL

The standard error of the subgroup mean $\bar{X}$ is:

$$ \sigma_{\bar{X}} = \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}} = \frac{\bar{R}}{d_2 \sqrt{n}} $$

By Shewhart's 3σ principle, the control limits for $\bar{X}$ are:

$$ UCL_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} + 3\sigma_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} + 3 \cdot \frac{\bar{R}}{d_2 \sqrt{n}} $$

$$ LCL_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} - 3\sigma_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} - 3 \cdot \frac{\bar{R}}{d_2 \sqrt{n}} $$

The factor $3 / (d_2 \sqrt{n})$ is conventionally tabulated as $A_2$:

$$ A_2 = \frac{3}{d_2 \sqrt{n}} $$

Therefore the final control limits are:

$$ UCL_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} + A_2 \bar{R} \qquad LCL_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} - A_2 \bar{R} $$

### 3.1.3 Derivation of the R-Chart UCL and LCL

The standard deviation of the range R is:

$$ \sigma_R = d_3 \cdot \sigma $$

The 3σ control limits for R are:

$$ UCL_R = \bar{R} + 3 d_3 \sigma = \bar{R} \left(1 + \frac{3 d_3}{d_2}\right) $$

$$ LCL_R = \bar{R} - 3 d_3 \sigma = \bar{R} \left(1 - \frac{3 d_3}{d_2}\right) $$

The factors in parentheses are tabulated as $D_4$ and $D_3$:

$$ D_4 = 1 + \frac{3 d_3}{d_2} \qquad D_3 = 1 - \frac{3 d_3}{d_2} $$

Hence:

$$ UCL_R = D_4 \bar{R} \qquad LCL_R = D_3 \bar{R} $$

> [!NOTE]
> For $n \leq 6$, $D_3$ becomes zero (or negative) and is conventionally reported as **0**, meaning there is **no lower control limit** for the R chart in those cases.

### 3.1.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A process produces bolts whose diameters are measured. 25 subgroups of size n = 5 are taken. The grand mean is $\bar{\bar{X}} = 10.02$ mm and the mean range is $\bar{R} = 0.08$ mm. For n = 5, $A_2 = 0.577$, $D_3 = 0$, $D_4 = 2.114$. Determine the control limits.

**Step 1: $\bar{X}$-Chart limits**

$$ UCL_{\bar{X}} = 10.02 + (0.577)(0.08) = 10.02 + 0.04616 = 10.066 \text{ mm} $$

$$ CL_{\bar{X}} = 10.02 \text{ mm} $$

$$ LCL_{\bar{X}} = 10.02 - (0.577)(0.08) = 10.02 - 0.04616 = 9.974 \text{ mm} $$

**Step 2: R-Chart limits**

$$ UCL_R = (2.114)(0.08) = 0.169 \text{ mm} $$

$$ CL_R = 0.08 \text{ mm} $$

$$ LCL_R = 0 \text{ mm } \quad (\text{since } D_3 = 0 \text{ for } n=5) $$

**Interpretation:** Any subgroup with mean outside [9.974, 10.066] mm or range above 0.169 mm indicates the process is out of control and needs investigation.

## 3.2 Derivation of the p-Chart (Attribute Control Chart)

The **p-chart** monitors the **fraction defective** in samples of constant size n.

### 3.2.1 Statistical Foundation

If the true fraction defective is p, then the number of defectives d in a sample of size n follows the **Binomial distribution**:

$$ d \sim \text{Binomial}(n, p) $$

The mean and standard deviation of the sample fraction $\hat{p} = d/n$ are:

$$ E[\hat{p}] = p \qquad \sigma_{\hat{p}} = \sqrt{\frac{p(1-p)}{n}} $$

### 3.2.2 Construction of Control Limits

Since p is unknown, it is estimated from the average of k subgroups:

$$ \bar{p} = \frac{\text{Total defectives}}{\text{Total inspected}} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{k} d_i}{kn} $$

Applying the 3σ rule:

$$ UCL_p = \bar{p} + 3\sqrt{\frac{\bar{p}(1-\bar{p})}{n}} $$

$$ CL_p = \bar{p} $$

$$ LCL_p = \bar{p} - 3\sqrt{\frac{\bar{p}(1-\bar{p})}{n}} $$

If $LCL_p$ is computed to be **negative**, it is set to **zero** (you cannot have a negative fraction defective).

### 3.2.3 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** In 20 samples of size n = 100, the number of defectives observed are: 5, 7, 4, 6, 8, 3, 9, 5, 4, 7, 6, 2, 8, 5, 4, 6, 7, 3, 5, 4. Construct a p-chart.

**Step 1: Total defectives**

$$ \sum d_i = 5+7+4+6+8+3+9+5+4+7+6+2+8+5+4+6+7+3+5+4 = 108 $$

**Step 2: Average fraction defective**

$$ \bar{p} = \frac{108}{20 \times 100} = \frac{108}{2000} = 0.054 $$

**Step 3: Standard error**

$$ \sigma_p = \sqrt{\frac{0.054 \times 0.946}{100}} = \sqrt{0.00051084} = 0.02260 $$

**Step 4: Control limits**

$$ UCL_p = 0.054 + 3(0.02260) = 0.054 + 0.0678 = 0.1218 $$

$$ LCL_p = 0.054 - 3(0.02260) = 0.054 - 0.0678 = -0.0138 \rightarrow 0 $$

**Step 5: Compare each $\hat{p}_i = d_i/100$ against [0, 0.1218]**

The maximum observed was 9/100 = 0.09, which is below 0.1218. Hence the process is **in statistical control**.

## 3.3 Derivation of Process Capability Indices

### 3.3.1 $C_p$ — Potential Process Capability

$C_p$ measures the **ability of the process to meet two-sided specification limits**, assuming the process is perfectly centered.

The natural process spread is $6\sigma$ (covering 99.73% of variation). The specification spread is $USL - LSL$.

$$ C_p = \frac{\text{Specification Spread}}{\text{Process Spread}} = \frac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma} $$

**Interpretation Table:**

| $C_p$ Value | Process Capability |
| :--- | :--- |
| $C_p < 1.00$ | Inadequate — process spread > tolerance |
| $1.00 \leq C_p < 1.33$ | Marginally capable |
| $1.33 \leq C_p < 1.67$ | Capable |
| $C_p \geq 1.67$ | Highly capable (Six Sigma territory) |

### 3.3.2 $C_{pk}$ — Actual (Centered-Aware) Capability

$C_p$ is naive — it assumes centering. The **$C_{pk}$ index** accounts for off-center processes by computing capability on each side separately:

$$ C_{pk} = \min\left[ C_{pu},\, C_{pl} \right] = \min\left[ \frac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma},\, \frac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma} \right] $$

When the process is perfectly centered, $C_{pk} = C_p$.

### 3.3.3 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A shaft has USL = 25.10 mm, LSL = 24.80 mm. From 50 measurements, $\bar{X} = 24.97$ mm and s = 0.045 mm.

**Step 1: $C_p$ computation**

$$ C_p = \frac{25.10 - 24.80}{6 \times 0.045} = \frac{0.30}{0.27} = 1.111 $$

**Step 2: $C_{pk}$ computation**

$$ C_{pu} = \frac{25.10 - 24.97}{3 \times 0.045} = \frac{0.13}{0.135} = 0.963 $$

$$ C_{pl} = \frac{24.97 - 24.80}{3 \times 0.045} = \frac{0.17}{0.135} = 1.259 $$

$$ C_{pk} = \min(0.963,\, 1.259) = 0.963 $$

**Interpretation:** Although $C_p$ suggests "marginally capable" (1.111), the $C_{pk}$ of 0.963 reveals the process is **off-center** and is producing shafts above the USL. **Re-centering the process** is required.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Control Chart

| Data Type | Sample Size | Subgroup Size Constant? | Use This Chart |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Variable | n $\geq 2$ | Yes | $\bar{X}$-R Chart (2-chart system) |
| Variable | n = 1 (one piece) | Yes | I-MR Chart (Individuals & Moving Range) |
| Attribute | Large (n $\geq 50$) | Yes | p-chart |
| Attribute | Small | Yes | np-chart |
| Attribute | Defects (counts) | Yes | c-chart |
| Attribute | Defects, varying n | No | u-chart |

## 3.5 The Operating Characteristic (OC) Curve

For acceptance sampling, the **probability of acceptance** $P_a$ is a function of the true fraction defective p in the incoming lot.

For a single sampling plan with parameters (n, c):

$$ P_a(p) = \sum_{d=0}^{c} \binom{n}{d} p^d (1-p)^{n-d} $$

The OC curve is the plot of $P_a$ vs p. Key features:

- **Producer's Point:** $P_a$ at $p = AQL \geq 0.90$ to 0.95.
- **Consumer's Point:** $P_a$ at $p = LTPD \leq 0.10$.
- **Producer's Risk (α):** Probability of rejecting a *good* lot ($p = AQL$).
- **Consumer's Risk (β):** Probability of accepting a *bad* lot ($p = LTPD$).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Trap:** Do not confuse the *Process Capability* $\sigma$ with the *Control Chart* $\sigma$. In a control chart, $\sigma$ is the **in-control** short-term variation. In capability, it may be the **long-term** variation estimated by s. The former uses $C_p$, the latter uses $P_p$.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Quality Control Workflow — Block Diagram

The flow of activities in a typical industrial QC function is shown below.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Material Receipt] --> B[Incoming Inspection]
    B --> C{In-spec?}
    C -- Yes --> D[Move to In-process Production]
    C -- No --> E[Reject / Return to Vendor]
    D --> F[In-Process SPC Monitoring]
    F --> G[Subgroup Sampling every 1 hour]
    G --> H[Calculate subgroup mean and range]
    H --> I{Within Control Limits?}
    I -- Yes --> J[Continue Production]
    I -- No --> K[Stop and Investigate Special Cause]
    K --> L[Apply 5-Why Root Cause Analysis]
    L --> M[Corrective Action]
    M --> N[Restart Production]
    D --> O[Final Inspection]
    O --> P[Acceptance Sampling n,c plan]
    P --> Q{d <= c?}
    Q -- Yes --> R[Lot Accepted Ship to Customer]
    Q -- No --> S[Lot Rejected 100 percent Screen or Return]
    J --> O
    N --> O
```

## 4.2 Variable vs Attribute Data — Decision Tree

Selecting the correct control chart begins with identifying your data type.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start What is your data type?] --> B{Measurable on a continuous scale?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Variable Data]
    B -- No --> D[Attribute Data go no go]
    C --> E{Subgroup size n?}
    E -- n equals 1 --> F[Use I-MR Chart Individuals and Moving Range]
    E -- n between 2 and 10 --> G[Use X-bar R Chart]
    E -- n greater than 10 --> H[Use X-bar S Chart]
    D --> I{What are you counting?}
    I -- Defective items fraction --> J{Is sample size n constant?}
    J -- Yes --> K[Use p Chart]
    J -- No --> L[Use p Chart with variable n limits]
    I -- Defects per unit count --> M{Is inspection area constant?}
    M -- Yes --> N[Use c Chart]
    M -- No --> O[Use u Chart defects per unit area]
```

## 4.3 Control Chart Anatomy — Conceptual Schematic

A control chart is not just a line graph. It is a decision-making tool with three horizontal zones.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CL[Control Chart Zones]
        Z1[Zone A Plus or Minus 2 to 3 sigma Warning Zone]
        Z2[Zone B Plus or Minus 1 to 2 sigma Caution Zone]
        Z3[Zone C Within Plus or Minus 1 sigma Target Zone]
    end
    UCL[UCL Upper Control Limit at plus 3 sigma] --> Z1
    Z1 --> Z2
    Z2 --> Z3
    Z3 --> Z2
    Z2 --> LCL[LCL Lower Control Limit at minus 3 sigma]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Walter Shewhart's 3σ rule is not a statistical accident. It deliberately creates a **0.27% false-alarm rate** (1 chance in 370 of a point outside limits purely by chance), which is the *economically optimal* rate for shop-floor decision-making.

## 4.4 Seven Tools of Quality Control — Ishikawa Map

```mermaid
mindmap
  root((7 QC Tools Kaoru Ishikawa))
    Tools1 Cause and Effect Diagram
    Tools2 Check Sheet
    Tools3 Control Chart
    Tools4 Histogram
    Tools5 Pareto Chart
    Tools6 Scatter Diagram
    Tools7 Flowchart
```

## 4.5 SPC Feedback Loop — PDCA Integration

Statistical Process Control is a direct engineering application of the **Deming / Shewhart Cycle**.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Plan Design sampling plan and control limits] --> D1[Do Collect subgroup data on shop floor]
    D1 --> C1[Check Plot on control chart and interpret]
    C1 --> A1[Act Remove special cause or improve common cause]
    A1 --> P1
```

## 4.6 Acceptance Sampling Plan Anatomy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Lot of N items arrives] --> B[Draw random sample of size n]
    B --> C[Inspect each of n items]
    C --> D[Count number of defectives d]
    D --> E{d is less than or equal to c?}
    E -- Yes --> F[Accept the lot Ship it]
    E -- No --> G[Reject the lot Screen 100 percent or return]
    F --> H[Compute AOQ Average Outgoing Quality]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Track ASN Average Sample Number]
    I --> J[Plot OC Curve for p versus P of acceptance]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]

> - Part A: 2 questions × 3 marks = 6 marks
> - Part B (Module-wise): 1 question of 14 marks with internal choice (a) or (b)

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**"Distinguish between Quality Control and Quality Assurance with suitable examples."** (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

| Basis | Quality Control (QC) | Quality Assurance (QA) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Focus | Product / Output inspection | Process / System design |
| Nature | Reactive — detects defects | Preventive — eliminates causes |
| Example | Inspecting 50 bolts out of 1000 for dimensional accuracy | Training machinists on tolerance settings and creating an SOP |

QC is a **sub-function** of QA. While QC asks "Is this product good?", QA asks "Why are we sometimes making bad products and how do we prevent it?"

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**"Define the term 'Process Capability Index $C_{pk}$' and explain its significance."** (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

$C_{pk}$ is a statistical measure of the **actual ability of a process to produce output within specification limits**, taking into account the **centering** of the process.

$$ C_{pk} = \min\left[ \frac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma},\, \frac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma} \right] $$

**Significance:**
- A $C_{pk} \geq 1.33$ indicates the process is capable and the defect rate is low (≤ 63 ppm for two-sided spec).
- $C_{pk} = C_p$ only when the process mean exactly equals the midpoint of the specification.
- $C_{pk}$ penalizes off-center processes and is the preferred index in modern industry (automotive, semiconductors).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Question (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**a)** Explain the concept of Statistical Process Control (SPC). With a neat sketch, describe the construction and use of an $\bar{X}$-R control chart. (7 Marks)

**b)** The following data give the sample means ($\bar{X}$) and ranges (R) for 8 subgroups of size n = 5 drawn from a manufacturing process:

| Subgroup | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 | 7 | 8 |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| $\bar{X}$ | 51.0 | 50.8 | 50.6 | 51.2 | 50.9 | 50.7 | 51.1 | 50.5 |
| R | 4 | 5 | 3 | 6 | 4 | 5 | 4 | 3 |

For n = 5, $A_2 = 0.577$, $D_3 = 0$, $D_4 = 2.114$. Calculate the control limits for the $\bar{X}$ and R charts. Is the process in statistical control? (7 Marks)

---

#### Model Solution to Question A(a)

**Definition (2 marks):**
Statistical Process Control (SPC) is the application of statistical techniques — primarily control charts — to monitor, control, and improve a process over time by distinguishing between **common cause variation** (inherent to the process) and **special cause variation** (due to external disturbances).

**Construction Steps (3 marks):**

1. Collect k subgroups, each of size n (typically n = 5), at regular time intervals.
2. For each subgroup i, compute the mean $\bar{X}_i$ and range $R_i = X_{max} - X_{min}$.
3. Compute the grand mean $\bar{\bar{X}} = \frac{1}{k}\sum \bar{X}_i$ and mean range $\bar{R} = \frac{1}{k}\sum R_i$.
4. Construct the **R-chart first**, using:

$$ UCL_R = D_4 \bar{R}, \qquad CL_R = \bar{R}, \qquad LCL_R = D_3 \bar{R} $$

5. If the R-chart is in control, construct the **$\bar{X}$-chart** using:

$$ UCL_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} + A_2 \bar{R}, \qquad CL_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}}, \qquad LCL_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} - A_2 \bar{R} $$

6. Plot subsequent $\bar{X}_i$ and $R_i$ values; investigate any points outside limits.

**Use (2 marks):** The $\bar{X}$-chart detects *shifts in the process mean*; the R-chart detects *changes in process variability*. Together they signal when the process requires corrective action.

---

#### Model Solution to Question A(b)

**Step 1: Calculate $\bar{\bar{X}}$ and $\bar{R}$**

$$ \bar{\bar{X}} = \frac{51.0 + 50.8 + 50.6 + 51.2 + 50.9 + 50.7 + 51.1 + 50.5}{8} = \frac{406.8}{8} = 50.85 $$

$$ \bar{R} = \frac{4 + 5 + 3 + 6 + 4 + 5 + 4 + 3}{8} = \frac{34}{8} = 4.25 $$

**Step 2: Control limits for R-chart**

$$ UCL_R = D_4 \bar{R} = 2.114 \times 4.25 = 8.985 $$

$$ CL_R = 4.25 $$

$$ LCL_R = D_3 \bar{R} = 0 \times 4.25 = 0 $$

**Step 3: Control limits for $\bar{X}$-chart**

$$ UCL_{\bar{X}} = 50.85 + 0.577 \times 4.25 = 50.85 + 2.452 = 53.302 $$

$$ CL_{\bar{X}} = 50.85 $$

$$ LCL_{\bar{X}} = 50.85 - 2.452 = 48.398 $$

**Step 4: Check for out-of-control points**

| Subgroup | $\bar{X}$ | Within [48.40, 53.30]? | R | Within [0, 8.99]? |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 1 | 51.0 | ✓ | 4 | ✓ |
| 2 | 50.8 | ✓ | 5 | ✓ |
| 3 | 50.6 | ✓ | 3 | ✓ |
| 4 | 51.2 | ✓ | 6 | ✓ |
| 5 | 50.9 | ✓ | 4 | ✓ |
| 6 | 50.7 | ✓ | 5 | ✓ |
| 7 | 51.1 | ✓ | 4 | ✓ |
| 8 | 50.5 | ✓ | 3 | ✓ |

**Conclusion (1 mark):** All subgroup means and ranges lie within their respective control limits. **The process is in statistical control.**

---

### Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**a)** Define Acceptance Sampling. Explain the terms AQL, LTPD, Producer's Risk (α), and Consumer's Risk (β) with the help of an Operating Characteristic (OC) curve. (7 Marks)

**b)** A single sampling plan with n = 80 and c = 1 is to be used for inspecting lots of size N = 1000. Calculate the probability of acceptance for incoming lots with fraction defective p = 0.01 and p = 0.05. Is the plan satisfactory if AQL = 0.02 and LTPD = 0.08? (7 Marks)

---

#### Model Solution to Question B(a)

**Definition (2 marks):**
Acceptance Sampling is a statistical quality control procedure in which a **random sample** is drawn from a lot, and based on the number of defectives in the sample, a decision is made to **accept or reject the entire lot**. It is used when 100% inspection is impractical, destructive, or uneconomical.

**Key Terms (5 marks):**

- **AQL (Acceptable Quality Level):** The maximum percent defective that can be considered *acceptable* as a process average. A lot at AQL quality should be accepted most of the time ($P_a \geq 0.90$ to 0.95).
- **LTPD (Lot Tolerance Percent Defective) / RQL:** The poorest quality level that the consumer is willing to accept infrequently. A lot at LTPD should be rejected most of the time ($P_a \leq 0.10$).
- **Producer's Risk (α):** The probability of **rejecting a good lot** (at AQL). Conventionally set at 0.05 or 0.10.
- **Consumer's Risk (β):** The probability of **accepting a bad lot** (at LTPD). Conventionally set at 0.10.

**OC Curve (Diagram + explanation):**
The OC curve is a plot of $P_a$ (probability of acceptance) on the Y-axis versus **p** (true fraction defective) on the X-axis. An *ideal* OC curve would be a step function (accept all good, reject all bad), but the inherent sampling variability makes the curve a smooth S-shape. The two risk points are marked as:

$$ P_a(AQL) = 1 - \alpha \qquad P_a(LTPD) = \beta $$

---

#### Model Solution to Question B(b)

**Step 1: Use the Binomial formula**

For a single sampling plan (n, c), the probability of acceptance is:

$$ P_a(p) = \sum_{d=0}^{c} \binom{n}{d} p^d (1-p)^{n-d} $$

With n = 80 and c = 1:

$$ P_a(p) = (1-p)^{80} + 80 p (1-p)^{79} $$

**Step 2: For p = 0.01**

$$ P_a(0.01) = (0.99)^{80} + 80(0.01)(0.99)^{79} $$

Compute $(0.99)^{80}$:

$$ (0.99)^{80} \approx 0.4475 $$

$$ (0.99)^{79} = (0.99)^{80} / 0.99 \approx 0.4475 / 0.99 \approx 0.4520 $$

Substitute:

$$ P_a(0.01) = 0.4475 + 80 \times 0.01 \times 0.4520 = 0.4475 + 0.3616 = 0.8091 $$

**Step 3: For p = 0.05**

$$ P_a(0.05) = (0.95)^{80} + 80(0.05)(0.95)^{79} $$

Compute $(0.95)^{80}$:

$$ (0.95)^{80} \approx 0.01686 $$

$$ (0.95)^{79} = 0.01686 / 0.95 \approx 0.01775 $$

Substitute:

$$ P_a(0.05) = 0.01686 + 80 \times 0.05 \times 0.01775 = 0.01686 + 0.0710 = 0.0879 $$

**Step 4: Test the plan against AQL = 0.02 and LTPD = 0.08**

Compute $P_a$ at AQL = 0.02:

$$ P_a(0.02) = (0.98)^{80} + 80(0.02)(0.98)^{79} $$

$$ (0.98)^{80} \approx 0.1976, \quad (0.98)^{79} \approx 0.2016 $$

$$ P_a(0.02) = 0.1976 + 80 \times 0.02 \times 0.2016 = 0.1976 + 0.3226 = 0.5202 $$

At AQL, $P_a$ should be ≥ 0.90, but we get only 0.52. **Plan fails the AQL criterion.**

Compute $P_a$ at LTPD = 0.08:

$$ P_a(0.08) = (0.92)^{80} + 80(0.08)(0.92)^{79} $$

$$ (0.92)^{80} \approx 0.00148, \quad (0.92)^{79} \approx 0.00161 $$

$$ P_a(0.08) = 0.00148 + 80 \times 0.08 \times 0.00161 = 0.00148 + 0.01031 = 0.0118 $$

At LTPD, $P_a$ should be ≤ 0.10, and 0.0118 satisfies this. **Plan passes the LTPD criterion.**

**Conclusion (1 mark):** The plan is **not fully satisfactory** because it gives insufficient protection to the producer at AQL ($P_a = 0.52 < 0.90$). The plan needs to be redesigned with either a larger n or higher c to meet the AQL requirement.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls (2024 Scheme):**
> 1. **Confusing $C_p$ and $C_{pk}$:** Students often compute $C_p$ and call it the capability index. Examiners specifically look for $C_{pk}$ to assess if you understand centering.
> 2. **Forgetting to plot the R-chart first:** The R-chart must be plotted *before* the $\bar{X}$-chart because the $\bar{X}$-chart limits depend on $\bar{R}$. Reversing the order loses 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Setting LCL to a negative value:** For the p-chart or c-chart, if LCL < 0, write "LCL = 0" explicitly. Examiners deduct marks for ignoring this.
> 4. **Misapplying the Binomial in sampling problems:** Many students use a Normal approximation when n is large, but forget the **continuity correction** or fail to check the np ≥ 5, n(1-p) ≥ 5 rule.
> 5. **Omitting the OC curve in AQL/LTPD answers:** A neat, labelled OC curve diagram with α and β marked is worth at least 2 marks. Do not describe it in words alone.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Quality Control (Module 1)**

### Key Definitions
- **Quality Control:** Operational techniques to fulfill quality requirements (inspection, testing).
- **QC vs QA:** QC = Product-focused, reactive. QA = Process-focused, preventive.
- **Variable Data:** Continuous measurements → use $\bar{X}$-R, $\bar{X}$-S, or I-MR charts.
- **Attribute Data:** Go/No-Go → use p, np, c, or u charts.
- **Specification Limit vs Control Limit:** Spec limits are design tolerances; control limits are statistical process boundaries.

### Must-Memorize Formulas
- $\bar{X}$-chart: $\bar{\bar{X}} \pm A_2 \bar{R}$
- R-chart: $D_4 \bar{R}$ and $D_3 \bar{R}$
- p-chart: $\bar{p} \pm 3\sqrt{\bar{p}(1-\bar{p})/n}$
- c-chart: $\bar{c} \pm 3\sqrt{\bar{c}}$
- $C_p = (USL - LSL)/(6\sigma)$
- $C_{pk} = \min[(USL-\mu)/(3\sigma),\, (\mu-LSL)/(3\sigma)]$
- $P_a(p) = \sum_{d=0}^{c} \binom{n}{d} p^d (1-p)^{n-d}$

### Key Numerical Thresholds
- **$C_p = 1.00$** → Process spread exactly equals tolerance (0.27% defects).
- **$C_p = 1.33$** → Industry-acceptable minimum capability.
- **$C_p = 1.67$** → Six Sigma / world-class capability.
- **$P_a$ at AQL** → Should be ≥ 0.90.
- **$P_a$ at LTPD** → Should be ≤ 0.10.

### Common KTU Pitfalls to Avoid
- Drawing control chart in wrong order (R first, then $\bar{X}$).
- Setting negative LCL on p-chart or c-chart without correction.
- Confusing $A_2, D_3, D_4$ values — they depend on subgroup size n.
- Forgetting the OC curve diagram in sampling questions.
- Computing $C_p$ when the question asks for $C_{pk}$.

### High-Yield Vocabulary
- **SPC** — Statistical Process Control
- **AQL** — Acceptable Quality Level (Producer's protection)
- **LTPD** — Lot Tolerance Percent Defective (Consumer's protection)
- **OC Curve** — Operating Characteristic Curve
- **AOQ** — Average Outgoing Quality
- **ATI** — Average Total Inspection
- **ASN** — Average Sample Number
- **3σ Rule** — Shewhart's principle for control limits
- **PDCA** — Plan-Do-Check-Act (Deming Cycle)
- **7 QC Tools** — Ishikawa's seven basic tools

> [!TIP]
> **Final Exam Tip (KTU 2024 Scheme):** Most Module 1 questions in PEMET758 combine a *theory* part (definition + diagram) with a *numerical* part (compute control limits or OC curve probability). Practice constructing clean, labelled control charts and OC curves — they are heavily weighted and easy to score.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Quality Assurance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET758 - QUALITY ENGINEERING AND MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Quality Assurance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quality Assurance (QA)** is defined as *"all the planned and systematic activities implemented within the Quality System that can be demonstrated to provide confidence that a product or service will satisfy quality requirements."*

The term is drawn directly from the ISO 9000 family of standards, which forms the international vocabulary governing Quality Assurance.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, **Quality Assurance** is the **proactive, system-wide, process-oriented discipline** that ensures — through **documented policies, procedures, standards, and audits** — that an organization's outputs consistently meet customer, statutory, and regulatory requirements.

**Key Vocabulary (must memorize verbatim for KTU exams):**

- **Quality System**: The organizational structure, responsibilities, procedures, processes, and resources needed to implement quality management.
- **Quality Policy**: A formal statement of intent and direction issued by top management regarding quality.
- **Quality Objective**: A specific, measurable goal consistent with the quality policy.
- **Conformance**: Meeting specified requirements.
- **Non-conformance**: Failure to meet specified requirements.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Hospital Operating Theatre"

> [!NOTE]
> **Imagine a hospital's operating theatre (OT).**
>
> Before any surgery, the hospital does not just trust the surgeon's skill — it enforces a **Quality Assurance System**:
> 1. A **pre-surgery checklist** verifies the patient's identity, the surgical site, and sterilization of tools.
> 2. The OT is **ISO-certified**, meaning its sterilization temperature, humidity, and air filtration meet documented standards.
> 3. The procedure is **audited** periodically by an external body.
> 4. If an infection rate crosses a threshold, a **corrective action** is triggered and the SOP (Standard Operating Procedure) is revised.
>
> Here, **QA is the entire system** — the checklist, the certification, the audits, the SOPs. **Quality Control (QC) is only the post-surgery swab test** that checks for infection. QA prevents the problem; QC detects it.

This analogy clarifies that:
- **QA = Process-oriented, prevention-focused, system-wide.**
- **QC = Product-oriented, detection-focused, inspection-based.**

---

### 1.3 Salient Characteristics of Quality Assurance

- **Planned and Systematic** — not ad hoc, not reactive.
- **Documented** — written policies, procedures, and records.
- **Auditable** — verifiable by external parties.
- **Preventive in Nature** — focuses on eliminating root causes.
- **All-encompassing** — covers people, processes, products, and the management system.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Tip:** When asked to "define QA", always add the phrase *"planned and systematic activities"* — examiners award the differential mark for this exact wording.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Principles of Quality Assurance (Aligned with ISO 9000 / TQM)

A QA system rests on **seven foundational principles** (per ISO 9000:2015):

| # | Principle | Engineering Interpretation |
|---|-----------|---------------------------|
| 1 | **Customer Focus** | Understand and exceed customer expectations. |
| 2 | **Leadership** | Top management establishes unity of purpose and direction. |
| 3 | **Engagement of People** | Competent, empowered, motivated workforce at all levels. |
| 4 | **Process Approach** | Manage activities as interrelated processes forming a coherent system. |
| 5 | **Improvement** | Reactive (CAPA) + proactive (risk-based) continual improvement. |
| 6 | **Evidence-based Decision Making** | Decisions grounded in data, facts, and statistical analysis. |
| 7 | **Relationship Management** | Manage relationships with interested parties (suppliers, regulators). |

---

### 2.2 The Four-Tier Documentation Pyramid

Every QA system is supported by a **hierarchical documentation structure**:

$$\text{Tier 1: Quality Manual} \rightarrow \text{Tier 2: Procedures} \rightarrow \text{Tier 3: Work Instructions} \rightarrow \text{Tier 4: Records/Evidence}$$

- **Tier 1 — Quality Manual:** Top-level policy document stating scope, exclusions, and interactions of processes.
- **Tier 2 — Procedures (SOPs):** "What" and "who" — describes activities, responsibilities, and controls.
- **Tier 3 — Work Instructions:** "How" — detailed step-by-step task instructions for operators.
- **Tier 4 — Records:** Objective evidence (forms, logs, certificates) that activities were performed.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** A common 14-mark question is *"Explain the documentation hierarchy of a Quality Management System"* — always draw the pyramid in the exam.

---

### 2.3 Quality Assurance vs. Quality Control (Most Repeated KTU Distinction)

| Parameter | Quality Assurance (QA) | Quality Control (QC) |
|-----------|----------------------|----------------------|
| Orientation | **Process / System** | **Product / Output** |
| Approach | **Prevention** | **Detection** |
| Nature | **Proactive** | **Reactive** |
| Tools | SOPs, Audits, Training | Inspection, Testing, Sampling |
| Responsibility | Entire organization | Inspection / QC department |
| Stage | Throughout the process | At inspection points |
| Cost Center | Prevention + Appraisal | Failure costs |
| Standard Reference | ISO 9001 (Clause 4–10) | ISO 2859 (Sampling), Statistical QC |

---

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Concept | Formula / Relationship | Units |
|---|---------|------------------------|-------|
| 1 | **Total Cost of Quality (COQ)** | $COQ = C_P + C_A + C_{IF} + C_{EF}$ | ₹ / $ |
| 2 | **Cost of Poor Quality (COPQ)** | $COPQ = C_{IF} + C_{EF}$ | ₹ / $ |
| 3 | **Cost of Good Quality (COGQ)** | $COGQ = C_P + C_A$ | ₹ / $ |
| 4 | **Defects Per Million Opportunities (DPMO)** | $DPMO = \dfrac{D \times 10^6}{U \times O}$ | defects/million |
| 5 | **Process Yield** | $Y = \dfrac{U - D}{U} | Units |
| 6 | **Rolled Throughput Yield (RTY)** | $RTY = Y_1 \times Y_2 \times \cdots \times Y_n$ | fraction |
| 7 | **Process Capability Index (Cp)** | $C_p = \dfrac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma}$ | dimensionless |
| 8 | **Process Capability Index (Cpk)** | $C_{pk} = \min\left(\dfrac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma},\ \dfrac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma}\right)$ | dimensionless |
| 9 | **Sigma Level (Z)** | $Z = \dfrac{USL - \mu}{\sigma}$ (one-sided) | σ |
| 10 | **Six Sigma Target** | $DPMO \le 3.4$ | defects/million |

> Where:
> - $C_P$ = Prevention Cost, $C_A$ = Appraisal Cost
> - $C_{IF}$ = Internal Failure Cost, $C_{EF}$ = External Failure Cost
> - $D$ = Number of defects, $U$ = Number of units, $O$ = Opportunities per unit
> - $USL$ = Upper Spec Limit, $LSL$ = Lower Spec Limit
> - $\mu$ = Process mean, $\sigma$ = Standard deviation

---

### 2.5 Cost of Quality — Conceptual Decomposition

The **Cost of Quality (COQ)** is the single most important quantitative tool in QA:

$$\underbrace{C_P}_{\text{Training, SOP design}} + \underbrace{C_A}_{\text{Inspection, testing}} + \underbrace{C_{IF}}_{\text{Scrap, rework}} + \underbrace{C_{EF}}_{\text{Warranty, recall, lawsuits}} = COQ$$

**Behaviour with Quality Improvement:**
- As quality improves, $C_P$ **rises initially** (more prevention spend), then **falls**.
- $C_A$ remains roughly **constant**.
- $C_{IF}$ and $C_{EF}$ **fall dramatically** — this is the **optimum point**.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility:** COQ is the financial bridge between the technical QA team and top management. Every QA initiative (FMEA, SPC, Six Sigma) must be justified by COQ reduction.

---

### 2.6 Types of Quality Audits (KTU Favourite)

| Audit Type | Conducted By | Purpose | Frequency |
|------------|--------------|---------|-----------|
| **First-Party (Internal)** | The organization itself | Verify conformity to its own QMS | Quarterly / Half-yearly |
| **Second-Party** | Customer / Buyer | Verify supplier conformance | Contractual |
| **Third-Party (Certification)** | Independent certification body (e.g., BIS, TÜV) | Issue / renew ISO 9001 certificate | Annual surveillance |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Cost of Quality (COPQ) Calculation

> **Problem Statement (KTU-Style):**
> A precision machining firm reports the following annual costs from its accounting records:
>
> - Design review and training: **₹ 2,50,000**
> - Calibration of measuring equipment: **₹ 1,50,000**
> - Rework on defective parts: **₹ 3,00,000**
> - Warranty claims settled: **₹ 4,00,000**
> - Scrap of rejected components: **₹ 1,00,000**
> - Incoming inspection of raw materials: **₹ 80,000**
> - Customer returns and field service: **₹ 2,00,000**
>
> Compute: (a) Prevention Cost, (b) Appraisal Cost, (c) Internal Failure Cost, (d) External Failure Cost, (e) Total COQ, (f) COPQ, (g) COGQ, and (h) Percentage of COPQ to total revenue (assume total revenue = ₹ 50,00,000).

#### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Classify each cost head into its COQ category.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Design review \& training} &= ₹ 2{,}50{,}000 \quad \rightarrow \text{Prevention } (C_P) \\
\text{Calibration of measuring equipment} &= ₹ 1{,}50{,}000 \quad \rightarrow \text{Appraisal } (C_A) \\
\text{Rework on defective parts} &= ₹ 3{,}00{,}000 \quad \rightarrow \text{Internal Failure } (C_{IF}) \\
\text{Warranty claims settled} &= ₹ 4{,}00{,}000 \quad \rightarrow \text{External Failure } (C_{EF}) \\
\text{Scrap of rejected components} &= ₹ 1{,}00{,}000 \quad \rightarrow \text{Internal Failure } (C_{IF}) \\
\text{Incoming inspection of raw materials} &= ₹ 80{,}000 \quad \rightarrow \text{Appraisal } (C_A) \\
\text{Customer returns and field service} &= ₹ 2{,}00{,}000 \quad \rightarrow \text{External Failure } (C_{EF})
\end{aligned}$$

> *Logic:* Prevention activities are performed **before** the defect occurs. Appraisal activities are performed to **detect** defects. Internal failure is detected **inside** the factory. External failure is detected **outside** the factory (post-delivery).

**Step 2 — Sum each category.**

$$\begin{aligned}
C_P &= ₹ 2{,}50{,}000 \\
C_A &= ₹ 1{,}50{,}000 + ₹ 80{,}000 = ₹ 2{,}30{,}000 \\
C_{IF} &= ₹ 3{,}00{,}000 + ₹ 1{,}00{,}000 = ₹ 4{,}00{,}000 \\
C_{EF} &= ₹ 4{,}00{,}000 + ₹ 2{,}00{,}000 = ₹ 6{,}00{,}000
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Total Cost of Quality.**

$$COQ = C_P + C_A + C_{IF} + C_{EF}$$

$$COQ = 2{,}50{,}000 + 2{,}30{,}000 + 4{,}00{,}000 + 6{,}00{,}000$$

$$\boxed{COQ = ₹ 14{,}80{,}000}$$

**Step 4 — Cost of Poor Quality (Failure Cost).**

$$COPQ = C_{IF} + C_{EF} = 4{,}00{,}000 + 6{,}00{,}000$$

$$\boxed{COPQ = ₹ 10{,}00{,}000}$$

**Step 5 — Cost of Good Quality (Conformance Cost).**

$$COGQ = C_P + C_A = 2{,}50{,}000 + 2{,}30{,}000$$

$$\boxed{COGQ = ₹ 4{,}80{,}000}$$

**Step 6 — COPQ as a percentage of revenue.**

$$\%COPQ = \dfrac{COPQ}{\text{Revenue}} \times 100 = \dfrac{10{,}00{,}000}{50{,}00{,}000} \times 100$$

$$\boxed{\%COPQ = 20\%}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Industry Benchmark (KTU context):** World-class manufacturers maintain COPQ < **5% of revenue**. The example firm is severely underperforming and must invest in prevention (training, FMEA, SPC) to slash failure costs.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Six Sigma DPMO and Sigma Level

> **Problem Statement:**
> A printed-circuit-board (PCB) assembly line produces **5,000 boards per day**. Each board has **150 soldering opportunities**. Yesterday, the line recorded **42 defects**.
>
> Compute: (a) DPMO, (b) Process Yield, (c) Estimated Sigma Level.

#### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Compute DPMO.**

$$DPMO = \dfrac{D \times 10^6}{U \times O}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
DPMO &= \dfrac{42 \times 10^6}{5000 \times 150} \\
     &= \dfrac{42 \times 10^6}{7{,}50{,}000} \\
     &= \dfrac{42{,}00{,}00{,}000}{7{,}50{,}000}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{DPMO = 5600 \text{ defects per million opportunities}}$$

**Step 2 — Compute Process Yield (in %).**

$$Y = \dfrac{U - D}{U} \times 100$$

$$Y = \dfrac{5000 - 42}{5000} \times 100 = \dfrac{4958}{5000} \times 100$$

$$\boxed{Y = 99.16\%}$$

**Step 3 — Estimate Sigma Level using DPMO → Z conversion table.**

Since DPMO = 5600, looking up the standard Six Sigma conversion table (or using the inverse normal approximation):

$$Z \approx 4.45\,\sigma$$

$$\boxed{\text{Process Sigma Level} \approx 4.45\,\sigma}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Interpretation:** A Six Sigma process demands DPMO ≤ 3.4 (≈ 6σ). This line at 4.45σ produces ~5600 ppm defects — typical of a mature but non-world-class operation. The DPMO → Z conversion is conventionally read from Motorola's Six Sigma tables; examiners accept any standard reference.

---

### 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Rolled Throughput Yield (RTY)

> **Problem Statement:**
> A 4-stage manufacturing process has the following stage yields:
>
> | Stage | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 |
> |-------|---|---|---|---|
> | Yield | 0.98 | 0.95 | 0.97 | 0.99 |
>
> Compute: (a) RTY, (b) Defect rate, (c) Number of good units from an input of **10,000** units.

#### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Compute RTY.**

$$RTY = Y_1 \times Y_2 \times Y_3 \times Y_4$$

$$RTY = 0.98 \times 0.95 \times 0.97 \times 0.99$$

$$\begin{aligned}
RTY_2 &= 0.98 \times 0.95 = 0.9310 \\
RTY_3 &= 0.9310 \times 0.97 = 0.90307 \\
RTY_4 &= 0.90307 \times 0.99 = 0.89404
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{RTY \approx 0.8940 = 89.40\%}$$

**Step 2 — Defect Rate.**

$$\text{Defect Rate} = 1 - RTY = 1 - 0.8940 = 0.1060$$

$$\boxed{\text{Defect Rate} = 10.60\%}$$

**Step 3 — Number of good units.**

$$\text{Good Units} = RTY \times \text{Input} = 0.8940 \times 10{,}000$$

$$\boxed{\text{Good Units} = 8940}$$

> **Engineering Insight:** Although each individual stage appears to be high-yield (95–99%), the *system* only delivers ~89% good units. This **hidden factory** effect is a powerful argument for system-wide QA rather than local optimization.

---

### 3.4 Worked Example 4 — Process Capability Indices

> **Problem Statement:**
> A CNC lathe produces shafts with specifications $LSL = 24.95$ mm and $USL = 25.05$ mm. The process mean $\mu = 25.00$ mm and standard deviation $\sigma = 0.012$ mm.
>
> Compute: (a) $C_p$, (b) $C_{pk}$, and comment on whether the process is capable.

#### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Compute Cp.**

$$C_p = \dfrac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma} = \dfrac{25.05 - 24.95}{6 \times 0.012} = \dfrac{0.10}{0.072}$$

$$\boxed{C_p = 1.389}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the two components of Cpk.**

$$\dfrac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma} = \dfrac{25.05 - 25.00}{3 \times 0.012} = \dfrac{0.05}{0.036} = 1.389$$

$$\dfrac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma} = \dfrac{25.00 - 24.95}{3 \times 0.012} = \dfrac{0.05}{0.036} = 1.389$$

**Step 3 — Take the minimum.**

$$C_{pk} = \min(1.389,\ 1.389)$$

$$\boxed{C_{pk} = 1.389}$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation.**

| Value Range | Capability Class |
|-------------|------------------|
| $C_{pk} \ge 1.67$ | World-class (Six Sigma) |
| $1.33 \le C_{pk} < 1.67$ | Capable (existing process) |
| $1.00 \le C_{pk} < 1.33$ | Marginally capable |
| $C_{pk} < 1.00$ | Not capable |

> **Comment:** With $C_{pk} = 1.389$, the process is **marginally capable** (between 1.33 and 1.67). Since $C_p = C_{pk}$, the process is also **perfectly centred** on the target value (25.00 mm). The process is acceptable for current production but should be improved for Six Sigma targets.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — The Quality Assurance System Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Top Management Commitment] --> B[Quality Policy Statement]
    B --> C[Define Quality Objectives]
    C --> D[Document Quality Manual]
    D --> E[Define SOPs and Procedures]
    E --> F[Train Workforce]
    F --> G[Implement Process Controls]
    G --> H[Perform Internal Audits]
    H --> I{Audit Result}
    I -->|Conforming| J[Continue Monitoring SPC]
    I -->|Non-Conforming| K[Root Cause Analysis 5-Why Ishikawa]
    K --> L[Corrective and Preventive Action CAPA]
    L --> M[Update Procedures and Retrain]
    M --> H
    J --> N[Management Review]
    N --> O[Continual Improvement PDCA Cycle]
    O --> C
    H --> P[External Third-Party Audit]
    P --> Q{ISO 9001 Certification}
    Q -->|Granted| R[Certified QMS]
    Q -->|Refused| K
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The flow shows QA as a closed-loop system — Plan (policy/objectives) → Do (implement & train) → Check (audit & SPC) → Act (CAPA & management review) — feeding back into planning. This is the classic **PDCA (Deming) cycle** at the system level.

---

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Cost of Quality Composition and Behaviour

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph COQ[Total Cost of Quality COQ]
        CP[Prevention Cost CP]
        CA[Appraisal Cost CA]
        CIF[Internal Failure Cost CIF]
        CEF[External Failure Cost CEF]
    end

    CP --> COGT[Cost of Good Quality COGQ]
    CA --> COGT
    CIF --> COPT[Cost of Poor Quality COPQ]
    CEF --> COPT

    COGT --> TOTAL[Total COQ = COGQ + COPQ]
    COPT --> TOTAL

    QLVL[Quality Level Improvement] -->|rises then falls| CP
    QLVL -->|roughly constant| CA
    QLVL -->|falls sharply| CIF
    QLVL -->|falls sharply| CEF

    style CP fill:#cce5ff
    style CA fill:#cce5ff
    style CIF fill:#ffcccc
    style CEF fill:#ffcccc
    style COGT fill:#d4edda
    style COPT fill:#f8d7da
```

> **Interpretation:** The two halves of the pie — **green (COGQ)** vs **red (COPQ)** — capture the QA philosophy: every rupee shifted from red to green is the financial reward of a mature QA system.

---

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — QA vs QC Boundary (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph QA[QUALITY ASSURANCE QA - SYSTEM LEVEL]
        Q1[Quality Manual and Policies]
        Q2[Standard Operating Procedures]
        Q3[Training and Competency Matrix]
        Q4[Internal Audits]
        Q5[Management Review]
    end

    subgraph QC[QUALITY CONTROL QC - PRODUCT LEVEL]
        P1[Incoming Inspection]
        P2[In-Process Inspection]
        P3[Final Inspection]
        P4[Statistical Sampling ISO 2859]
        P5[Testing and Measurement]
    end

    Q1 --> P1
    Q2 --> P2
    Q3 --> P3
    Q4 --> P4
    Q5 --> P5

    style QA fill:#d4edda
    style QC fill:#fff3cd
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Every QC activity is **governed** by a QA control. The yellow block detects defects; the green block prevents them.

---

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Four-Tier Documentation Pyramid

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    T1[Tier 1 - Quality Manual - Scope and Policy] --> T2[Tier 2 - Procedures SOPs - What and Who]
    T2 --> T3[Tier 3 - Work Instructions - Detailed How]
    T3 --> T4[Tier 4 - Records and Forms - Objective Evidence]
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Top-down authority flows from policy to procedure to instruction to record. Bottom-up evidence flows from records back up to inform policy revision — the documentation cycle of any ISO 9001-compliant QMS.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]**
*Define Quality Assurance. How is it different from Quality Control?*

#### Model Answer (3 marks)

**Definition (2 marks):**
Quality Assurance (QA) is the totality of planned and systematic activities implemented within the Quality System that can be demonstrated to provide confidence that a product or service will satisfy quality requirements. It is **process-oriented, prevention-focused, and system-wide**.

**Distinction (1 mark):**
QA governs the **process** (the system that *prevents* defects) whereas QC governs the **product** (the inspection that *detects* defects). QA is proactive; QC is reactive.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model]**
*List the four categories of Cost of Quality with one example each.*

#### Model Answer (3 marks)

| Category | Example |
|----------|---------|
| **Prevention Cost** | Employee training, design review, quality planning |
| **Appraisal Cost** | Incoming inspection, calibration of gauges, testing |
| **Internal Failure Cost** | Scrap, rework, re-inspection |
| **External Failure Cost** | Warranty claims, customer returns, product recalls |

*(½ mark each for the category name + ½ mark each for a valid example = 3 marks)*

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Adapted]*

**(a)** Explain the **principles of Quality Assurance** as outlined in ISO 9000:2015. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A manufacturing company reports the following annual costs:
- Quality training: ₹ 1,80,000
- Inspection of finished goods: ₹ 1,20,000
- Rework: ₹ 2,40,000
- Warranty repairs: ₹ 3,60,000
- Scrap disposal: ₹ 90,000
- Vendor surveillance: ₹ 60,000
- Customer complaint handling: ₹ 1,50,000

Compute the **Total Cost of Quality (COQ)**, the **Cost of Poor Quality (COPQ)**, and the **Cost of Good Quality (COGQ)**. Comment on the quality posture of the company. *(7 marks)*

---

##### Model Solution — Part (a): Principles of QA per ISO 9000:2015 *(7 marks)*

The seven quality management principles form the foundation of any QA system:
