topics.title: Deming Approach
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET758 - QUALITY ENGINEERING AND MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Deming Approach

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Deming's Approach** is a holistic, philosophy-driven management framework for organizational transformation toward sustained quality and competitive advantage, developed by the American statistician and management consultant **Dr. W. Edwards Deming (1900–1993)**. It is built on the principle that **quality is a system, not a department** and that management bears the **94% responsibility** for system-induced quality problems, while workers contribute only about 6%.

The Deming Approach rests on three foundational pillars:

1. **System of Profound Knowledge (SoPK)** — A unifying theory of management.
2. **The 14 Points for Management** — Actionable obligations for top leadership.
3. **The PDSA (Plan–Do–Study–Act) Cycle** — A scientific method for continuous improvement.

> [!NOTE]
> **Historical Context:** Deming's philosophy gained worldwide fame after the **1950 Tokyo seminar** where he taught top Japanese engineers. This transformed post-war Japanese industry and led to the rise of Toyota, Sony, and the global Lean movement. The **Deming Prize (established 1951)** is one of the most prestigious quality awards in the world.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Real-World Parallel)

> **Analogy — The Garden & The Gardener:**
> Imagine a rose garden. When the roses wilt, the gardener (management) has two choices:
> - **Wrong approach (Deming calls this the "old style"):** Punish the gardener's assistant (worker) for the wilted roses.
> - **Right approach (Deming's way):** Inspect the **soil pH, sunlight, water system, and fertilizer mix** — the *system* the roses grow in.
>
> Deming argued: **A bad worker cannot damage goods as much as a bad system.** If the system is flawed, no amount of worker effort can compensate. Therefore, fix the *system* first, not the people.

### 1.3 Key Terminology

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Profound Knowledge** | The interplay of four disciplines required to manage quality |
| **Common Cause Variation** | Inherent randomness within a stable system |
| **Special Cause Variation** | External, assignable disturbances |
| **PDSA Cycle** | Iterative learning loop for improvement |
| **System** | A network of interdependent components working for a common aim |
| **Chain Reaction** | The economic consequence sequence triggered by quality improvement |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Deming's System of Profound Knowledge (SoPK)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **System of Profound Knowledge (SoPK)** is the apex of Deming's philosophy. It is a map of theory that enables prediction and optimization of systems. The four interrelated components are:

#### 2.1.1 Appreciation for a System

- A **system** is a set of components that work together to achieve a common aim.
- The aim must be clearly stated, understood, and optimized as a **whole**, not sub-optimized at department level.
- Components: *Suppliers → Inputs → Process → Outputs → Customers.*
- **Key Insight:** Every person works in two systems: the **system in which they work**, and the **system in which they live** (family, society).

#### 2.1.2 Knowledge of Variation

- Variation is **ubiquitous** in every process. The question is not *whether* variation exists, but *what causes* it.
- **Common Cause Variation** (random noise) — inherent to the system, requires management action.
- **Special Cause Variation** (signals) — assignable to specific factors, requires operator action.
- **Statisticians must work with operating people** to distinguish signals from noise.

#### 2.1.3 Theory of Knowledge

- Knowledge is built on **experience, observation, and prediction**.
- The **PDSA cycle** is the engine of theory-building.
- A theory must be **testable, falsifiable, and operational** in a real environment.
- Management decisions without theory are mere **opinions** and lead to failure.

#### 2.1.4 Psychology

- People are inherently different and motivated by **intrinsic factors** (achievement, recognition, responsibility) more than extrinsic ones.
- Fear in the workplace is **destructive** — it suppresses learning and innovation.
- **Performance evaluations**, ranking systems, and slogans ("Do it right the first time") destroy pride of workmanship.

### 2.2 Deming's 14 Points for Management

> [!NOTE]
> The 14 Points are **obligations**, not suggestions. They apply to top management first.

| # | Point (Short Title) | Core Concept |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Create Constancy of Purpose** | Long-term commitment to innovation, R&D, improvement — not short-term profits |
| 2 | **Adopt the New Philosophy** | Reject defective products and poor service; tolerate zero faults |
| 3 | **Cease Dependence on Inspection** | Build quality *into* the product, not by inspecting it in |
| 4 | **End Lowest-Tender Contracts** | Minimize total cost, not initial cost; build long-term relationships |
| 5 | **Improve Constantly** | Identify problems, fix root causes, never stop improving |
| 6 | **Institute Training on the Job** | Workers need clear standards, methods, and proper materials |
| 7 | **Institute Leadership** | Help people do a better job; supervise *for* quality, not by numbers |
| 8 | **Drive Out Fear** | No one is afraid to ask questions or report problems |
| 9 | **Break Down Barriers** | Tear down departmental silos; team work across functions |
| 10 | **Eliminate Slogans & Exhortations** | Slogans don't help workers do a better job if the system is wrong |
| 11 | **Eliminate Numerical Quotas** | Replace with leadership; quotas breed poor quality |
| 12 | **Remove Barriers to Pride of Workmanship** | Eliminate annual rating systems that penalize workers |
| 13 | **Institute a Vigorous Programme of Education & Self-Improvement** | Continuous learning for all employees |
| 14 | **Put Everybody to Work** | Transformation is everyone's job; the 14 Points become a religion |

### 2.3 The PDSA (Deming) Cycle

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Plan–Do–Study–Act** cycle, originally known as the Shewhart Cycle, was popularized by Deming. It is the **scientific method of management**.

| Step | Action | Output |
|---|---|---|
| **PLAN** | Identify the problem, hypothesize a solution, design the experiment | Plan document, hypothesis statement |
| **DO** | Implement the plan on a small scale, collect data | Raw data, observations |
| **STUDY** | Analyze the data, compare predicted vs actual results | Validated/invalidated hypothesis |
| **ACT** | Standardize the change if successful; abandon and revise if not | New standard operating procedure, new cycle |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The **Taguchi Loss Function**, which complements Deming's philosophy, is mathematically written as:

$$
L(x) = k(x - T)^2
$$

where:
- $L(x)$ = Loss incurred in monetary terms
- $k$ = Cost coefficient (proportionality constant, $k = A / \Delta^2$)
- $x$ = Actual measured value of the quality characteristic
- $T$ = Target value of the quality characteristic
- $A$ = Loss at functional tolerance limit $\Delta$

**Deming's Chain Reaction — Sequential Economic Consequences:**

$$
\text{Quality Up} \rightarrow \text{Cost Down} \rightarrow \text{Productivity Up} \rightarrow \text{Market Share Up} \rightarrow \text{Stay in Business} \rightarrow \text{Provide Jobs}
$$

**7 Deadly Diseases (Table):**

| # | Disease | Description |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Lack of Constancy of Purpose** | Short-term profit focus; no long-term planning |
| 2 | **Emphasis on Short-term Profits** | Quarterly earnings drive kills investment |
| 3 | **Evaluation by Performance, Merit Rating** | Yearly ranking system destroys teamwork |
| 4 | **Mobility of Management** | Job-hopping managers never complete improvements |
| 5 | **Running a Company on Visible Figures Alone** | Common figures without context mislead |
| 6 | **Excessive Medical Costs** | Rising healthcare cost erodes competitive edge |
| 7 | **Excessive Costs of Liability** | Lawsuit-driven costs stifle innovation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Lesser Obstacles** include: over-emphasis on technology, search for examples, "our problems are different", and **Quality Circles (Japanese sense)** in their original form, which Deming felt were sometimes used to avoid system changes.

### 2.5 Real-World Utility

- **Manufacturing:** Toyota Production System, Ford's revival under Mullaly.
- **Healthcare:** Virginia Mason Medical Center's quality transformation.
- **Software:** Agile methodology (Scrum) is essentially a PDSA-driven framework.
- **Service Sector:** Banking, hospitality, and education quality reform.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Taguchi Loss Function (Companion to Deming Approach)

The Taguchi Loss Function formalizes Deming's principle: **"Any deviation from the target causes loss."** Below is the step-by-step derivation.

**Step 1 — Define the Target and Tolerance**
Let the target value of a quality characteristic be $T$, and the functional tolerance limit be $\Delta$. The customer's specification is often the customer's acceptable bound $T \pm \Delta$.

**Step 2 — Define the Loss at the Tolerance Limit**
Let $A$ be the loss to society (or cost) when the characteristic reaches the tolerance limit $\Delta$ away from the target.

**Step 3 — Establish Proportionality (Quadratic Assumption)**
Loss is assumed to be **proportional to the square of the deviation**, following the **principle of least squares** and the **maximum entropy principle**. This is the quadratic loss form.

$$
L(x) = k(x - T)^2
$$

**Step 4 — Determine the Constant k**
Substitute boundary condition: at $x = T + \Delta$, $L(x) = A$.

$$
A = k(\Delta)^2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad k = \frac{A}{\Delta^2}
$$

**Step 5 — Final Closed-Form Loss Function**

$$
L(x) = \frac{A}{\Delta^2}(x - T)^2
$$

**Step 6 — Economic Interpretation**
- A product **inside specification** but away from the target still incurs loss.
- This justifies Deming's call to **reduce variation**, not merely meet specs.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Deming's 14 Points Cross-Mapped with SoPK

| SoPK Dimension | Related 14 Points | Reasoning |
|---|---|---|
| **Appreciation for a System** | Points 1, 4, 9 | Constancy of purpose, supplier partnerships, breaking silos |
| **Knowledge of Variation** | Points 3, 5, 7 | Cease inspection, continuous improvement, leadership |
| **Theory of Knowledge** | Points 6, 13 | Training and education enable testable theories |
| **Psychology** | Points 2, 8, 10, 11, 12 | Drive out fear, eliminate slogans, quotas, merit ratings |

### 3.3 Step-by-Step Walkthrough: PDSA in Action (Order-Fulfillment Example)

**Step 1 — PLAN**
Identify the problem: Order delivery errors are 5% of total orders. Hypothesis: Double-verification at packing stage will reduce errors to 1%. Design experiment: 30-day trial on one product line.

**Step 2 — DO**
Execute the double-check process. Train packers. Record each error with date, time, order ID, and root cause. Collect 900 data points.

**Step 3 — STUDY**
Compute the error rate per day. Apply a **p-chart** (attribute control chart) to determine if the process is in statistical control.

$$
\bar{p} = \frac{\text{Total errors}}{\text{Total orders}} = \frac{9}{900} = 0.01
$$

$$
UCL = \bar{p} + 3\sqrt{\frac{\bar{p}(1-\bar{p})}{n}} = 0.01 + 3\sqrt{\frac{0.01 \times 0.99}{30}} \approx 0.064
$$

All daily proportions fall below UCL. Hypothesis **validated**.

**Step 4 — ACT**
Roll out double-verification across all product lines. Create Standard Operating Procedure (SOP) document. Begin new PDSA cycle to *further* reduce the remaining 1% error rate.

### 3.4 Deming's Chain Reaction — Logical Derivation

> [!NOTE]
> Deming's Chain Reaction is **not** a mathematical proof, but a **logical flow of economic consequences**. Deming's reasoning proceeds as follows:

**Premise A:** Better products and services inherently use **fewer resources** to produce.

**Premise B:** Fewer resources consumed and reduced rework directly **decrease the cost per unit**.

**Premise C:** Lower cost with higher quality **increases productivity** (more output per labor hour).

**Premise D:** Quality goods at competitive prices **capture market share** and create price stability.

**Premise E:** Market share and reputation **enable long-term survival** of the organization.

**Premise F:** A surviving organization **provides employment** and a foundation for community growth.

$$
\therefore \text{Quality} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Lower Cost} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Higher Productivity} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Market Leadership} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Long-term Survival} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Job Creation}
$$

### 3.5 The Red Beads Experiment — Analytical Reasoning

- **Setup:** 6 workers draw red beads from a bowl; "defects" (red beads) are counted.
- **Conclusion:** Workers' performance is determined by the **system** (bowl composition), not by effort.
- **Implication:** Punishing workers for red beads is meaningless; only **changing the system** (e.g., replacing the bowl) changes the defect rate.
- **Statistical Insight:** The **binomial distribution** of red beads demonstrates **common-cause variation**.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Deming's System of Profound Knowledge (SoPK)

```mermaid
graph TD
    sopkA[System of Profound Knowledge]
    sysA[Appreciation for a System]
    varA[Knowledge of Variation]
    knoA[Theory of Knowledge]
    psyA[Psychology]
    subA[Optimize whole system not parts]
    subB[Distinguish common vs special cause]
    subC[Build theories via PDSA]
    subD[Intrinsic motivation over fear]
    sopkA --> sysA
    sopkA --> varA
    sopkA --> knoA
    sopkA --> psyA
    sysA --> subA
    varA --> subB
    knoA --> subC
    psyA --> subD
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: The PDSA (Deming) Cycle

```mermaid
graph LR
    planB[PLAN - Identify problem and hypothesize]
    doB[DO - Implement on small scale]
    stuB[STUDY - Compare results to prediction]
    actB[ACT - Standardize or revise]
    stdB[New Standard SOP]
    planB --> doB
    doB --> stuB
    stuB --> actB
    actB --> stdB
    stdB --> planB
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Deming's Chain Reaction

```mermaid
graph LR
    qA[Improve Quality of Products and Services]
    cA[Costs Decrease from less rework and waste]
    pA[Productivity Increases from better processes]
    mA[Market Capture via better quality and lower price]
    sA[Stay in Business and Grow]
    jA[Provide More Jobs and Stability]
    qA --> cA
    cA --> pA
    pA --> mA
    mA --> sA
    sA --> jA
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: Deming's 14 Points — Functional Grouping

```mermaid
graph TD
    grp1[Purpose and Direction]
    pt1[Point 1: Constancy of Purpose]
    pt2[Point 2: New Philosophy]
    grp1 --> pt1
    grp1 --> pt2
    grp2[System and Process Quality]
    pt3[Point 3: Cease Inspection]
    pt5[Point 5: Improve Constantly]
    pt4[Point 4: Long Term Supplier Relations]
    grp2 --> pt3
    grp2 --> pt5
    grp2 --> pt4
    grp3[People and Leadership]
    pt6[Point 6: Institute Training]
    pt7[Point 7: Institute Leadership]
    pt8[Point 8: Drive Out Fear]
    pt9[Point 9: Break Down Barriers]
    grp3 --> pt6
    grp3 --> pt7
    grp3 --> pt8
    grp3 --> pt9
    grp4[Culture and Transformation]
    pt10[Point 10: Eliminate Slogans]
    pt11[Point 11: Eliminate Quotas]
    pt12[Point 12: Remove Merit Rating Barriers]
    pt13[Point 13: Education Programme]
    pt14[Point 14: Transformation for All]
    grp4 --> pt10
    grp4 --> pt11
    grp4 --> pt12
    grp4 --> pt13
    grp4 --> pt14
```

### 4.5 Block Architecture: Red Beads Experiment

```mermaid
graph TD
    inpA[System Inputs: Bowl with 4000 Beads, 800 Red, 3200 White]
    procA[Process: 6 Workers Draw 50 Beads Each]
    outA[Output: Count of Red Beads per Worker]
    mgrA[Manager's Mistake: Punish Workers for High Red Count]
    corA[Correct Action: Change the System, not the Worker]
    inpA --> procA
    procA --> outA
    outA --> mgrA
    mgrA --> corA
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define the Deming System of Profound Knowledge and list its four components.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Deming's System of Profound Knowledge is an integrated map of theory enabling managers to optimize any system. Its four components are:
1. **Appreciation for a System** – understanding interdependence of components.
2. **Knowledge of Variation** – distinguishing common cause from special cause.
3. **Theory of Knowledge** – building testable theories through PDSA.
4. **Psychology** – understanding human motivation and behavior in organizations.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*State Deming's Chain Reaction of quality improvement.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Deming Chain Reaction is a logical sequence of economic outcomes from quality improvement:

$$
\text{Quality} \uparrow \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Cost} \downarrow \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Productivity} \uparrow \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Market Share} \uparrow \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Stay in Business} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Provide Jobs}
$$

It demonstrates that quality is **not a cost** but a **strategic driver** of organizational survival and employment.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern:** Part B questions carry 14 marks with sub-parts (typically 7+7). You must attempt **one full question**.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a) [7 Marks] Explain Deming's 14 Points for Management in detail.**

**Model Answer:**

The 14 Points are obligations on top management for organizational transformation:

1. **Create Constancy of Purpose** – Commit to long-term innovation, product improvement, and R&D instead of short-term profit metrics.
2. **Adopt the New Philosophy** – Tolerate zero defects; quality must be a national, top-down philosophy.
3. **Cease Dependence on Inspection** – Quality must be built in, not bolted on. Inspection is too late, unreliable, and costly.
4. **End the Practice of Awarding Business on Price Tag Alone** – Build long-term supplier loyalty based on minimized total cost, not lowest initial tender.
5. **Improve Constantly Every Process** – Use PDSA, statistical methods, and Kaizen to remove root causes.
6. **Institute Training on the Job** – Workers must understand their processes, standards, and the source of variation.
7. **Institute Leadership** – Supervisors must help people and the system do a better job, not police numbers.
8. **Drive Out Fear** – Trust must replace fear so workers can openly report problems and suggestions.
9. **Break Down Barriers Between Departments** – Replace silos with teamwork; the buyer and seller in the next department are the *customer*.
10. **Eliminate Slogans and Exhortations** – Slogans cannot fix a broken system; they only frustrate workers.
11. **Eliminate Numerical Quotas** – Quotas limit output quality; replace with leadership and process capability.
12. **Remove Barriers to Pride of Workmanship** – Abolish merit ratings that create competition and destroy teamwork.
13. **Institute a Vigorous Programme of Education and Self-Improvement** – Continuous learning is an investment, not an expense.
14. **Put Everybody to Work to Accomplish the Transformation** – The 14 Points become a personal commitment; everyone participates.

---

**(b) [7 Marks] Explain Deming's Seven Deadly Diseases with examples.**

**Model Answer:**

The Seven Deadly Diseases are management behaviors that obstruct quality:

1. **Lack of Constancy of Purpose** – E.g., a CEO focusing only on quarterly share price, neglecting R&D investment.
2. **Emphasis on Short-term Profits** – E.g., cutting quality training budgets to boost annual EPS.
3. **Evaluation by Performance, Merit Rating, or Annual Review** – E.g., forced ranking of software developers destroys collaboration.
4. **Mobility of Top Management** – E.g., a CEO leaving after 3 years before PDSA cycles show results.
5. **Running a Company on Visible Figures Alone** – E.g., focusing on units produced without context for quality or returns.
6. **Excessive Medical Costs** – E.g., rising health insurance premiums eating into wages, hurting morale and costs.
7. **Excessive Costs of Liability** – E.g., lawsuit-driven defensive documentation in hospitals that adds no real value.

**Conclusion:** Deming warned that these diseases must be cured at the executive level for transformation to begin.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**(a) [7 Marks] Explain the PDSA (Plan–Do–Study–Act) Cycle with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer:**

The PDSA cycle, popularized by Deming, is the **scientific method of management**.

| Step | Meaning | Example: Hospital Reducing Patient Wait Time |
|---|---|---|
| **PLAN** | Identify problem; hypothesize improvement | Problem: Avg. wait = 45 min. Plan: Introduce triage redesign. Hypothesis: 45 → 20 min. |
| **DO** | Implement the plan on small scale; collect data | Run new triage for 30 days in 2 departments; record 600 data points. |
| **STUDY** | Compare results to prediction using charts and statistics | Mean wait dropped to 22 min. t-test confirms significance (p < 0.05). |
| **ACT** | Standardize if successful; iterate if not | Roll out triage redesign hospital-wide; begin new PDSA cycle on a new bottleneck. |

**Key Concept:** The PDSA cycle is **iterative, scientific, and never-ending**. Each cycle builds knowledge and reduces variation.

---

**(b) [7 Marks] Describe the Red Beads Experiment conducted by Deming and explain its significance.**

**Model Answer:**

**Setup:** Workers draw 50 beads from a bowl containing 4000 beads (800 red = "defects", 3200 white = "good"). Each worker's red bead count is tallied.

**Procedure:** Workers are publicly praised or criticized. Strict procedures are enforced. Demonstrations are held showing the system is "fair."

**Findings:**
- Despite training, motivation, and rule enforcement, red bead variation **persists**.
- The variation follows the **binomial distribution** dictated by bowl composition.
- A worker "improving" and a worker "performing poorly" differ only by **random chance**, not by effort.

**Significance:**
- Demonstrates that **performance is a function of the system, not the worker**.
- Management's job is to **change the system** (e.g., replace bowl with fewer red beads), not blame workers.
- Slogans, merit ratings, and exhortations **cannot override system constraints**.
- Reinforces SoPK and Deming's Points 7 (Leadership), 8 (No Fear), 10 (No Slogans), and 12 (No Merit Ratings).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Most common mistake:** Confusing **PDSA** (Deming's version) with **PDCA** (Deming's name in some texts for "Check"). Always use **STUDY** when referring to Deming; reserve "Check" for the original Shewhart/Juran version.
> 2. **Mark loss zone:** Writing the 14 Points in bullet form **without examples** will cap you at ~5/7. Each point demands an **application line**.
> 3. **For 14-mark questions:** Sub-part (a) and (b) are **independently graded**. Skipping (b) costs you 7 full marks. Always attempt both sub-parts even if a partial answer exists.
> 4. **Red Beads trap:** Many students narrate the experiment but **fail to connect it to the SoPK / 14 Points**. Always close with a "**therefore, this proves...**" sentence.
> 5. **Common formula error:** Writing $L(x) = k \vert x - T \vert$ instead of $L(x) = k(x - T)^2$ loses a mark in questions on quality loss.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Deming Approach (Module 1):**

- ⭐ Deming attributed **94% of quality problems to management**, 6% to workers.
- ⭐ The four components of **SoPK** = System + Variation + Theory of Knowledge + Psychology.
- ⭐ **14 Points** must be remembered in their numbered order; they are management's **obligations**, not options.
- ⭐ The **PDSA Cycle** is the operational engine of Deming's philosophy.
- ⭐ **Chain Reaction:** Quality ↑ → Cost ↓ → Productivity ↑ → Market Share ↑ → Survival → Jobs.
- ⭐ **Seven Deadly Diseases:** Lack of constancy, short-term profits, merit ratings, managerial mobility, visible-figures-only, medical costs, liability costs.
- ⭐ **Red Beads Experiment** proves that the system dictates performance.
- ⭐ **Taguchi Loss Function** $L(x) = k(x - T)^2$ is the mathematical complement to Deming's view of variation.
- ⭐ Deming's **"System of Profound Knowledge"** is the only theory of management he declared as **complete**.
- ⭐ "It is not necessary to change. Survival is not mandatory" — Deming's famous warning to complacent organizations.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Juran's Quality Trilogy
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET758 - QUALITY ENGINEERING AND MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Juran's Quality Trilogy

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Juran's Quality Trilogy** is a universal framework for managing quality in any organization, developed by Dr. Joseph M. Juran (a pioneer of quality management, often called the "Father of Quality"). It defines quality management as a systematic process consisting of three interconnected managerial processes known as the **Juran Trilogy**:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Juran's Quality Trilogy Definition (KTU 2024)**
> *"A universal approach to quality management consisting of three managerial processes: Quality Planning, Quality Control, and Quality Improvement, organized in a sequence known as the Juran Trilogy, applicable to all functions and levels of an organization."*

The three components are:
1. **Quality Planning (QP)** – Identifying customers and their needs, then developing products/processes to meet those needs.
2. **Quality Control (QC)** – Evaluating actual performance against goals and taking corrective action when performance falls short.
3. **Quality Improvement (QI)** – Establishing the infrastructure and processes to continuously improve performance levels beyond current standards.

> [!NOTE]
> Juran emphasized that managing quality requires a **managerial approach** — meaning quality is the responsibility of top-level management, not just floor-level workers. The trilogy is built on the principle that quality is not a single act but a **continuous spiral** of planning, controlling, and improving.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are **preparing a home-cooked meal for guests** (a real-world KTU-friendly analogy):

- **Quality Planning (the recipe stage):** You first identify *who* is coming (guests = customers), *what* they need (vegetarian? spicy? diabetic-friendly food = needs), and *how* you will prepare the meal (ingredients, cooking method, serving style = product/process design). You set the menu and the standards.

- **Quality Control (the tasting stage):** While cooking, you keep *tasting the food*. If the salt is too low, you add more. If it's burning, you reduce the flame. You are comparing the actual taste against the expected taste and *correcting* deviations.

- **Quality Improvement (the learning stage):** After the meal, you realize the biryani could have been better with a different marination technique. Next time, you change the recipe, source better ingredients, or invest in a new pressure cooker. You are now *raising the standard* of your cooking.

**The Trilogy never ends** — once you improve, you set a new higher goal, plan for it, control it, and improve again. This is the **spiral of quality** in any organization.

## 1.3 Key Terminology (KTU High-Yield Vocabulary)

> [!NOTE]
> **Key terms to memorize for KTU University Exam:**
>
> - **Customer (Internal & External):** Anyone who is affected by the product or process.
> - **Product/Process:** What is being delivered and how it is delivered.
> - **Specifications / Goals:** Quantitative targets that define "good" quality.
> - **Operational Definitions:** Precise, measurable descriptions of what a quality characteristic means.
> - **Diagnostic Journey & Remedial Journey:** Juran's two-part problem-solving process.
> - **Breakthrough:** A sudden, dramatic improvement in performance achieved through QI.
> - **Fitness for Use:** Juran's definition of quality.

## 1.4 Standard Metrics & Constants

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Numerical/Conceptual Constants (Board Exam Favorites):**
>
> - **Juran's Definition of Quality:** *"Quality is Fitness for Use"* — not conformance to specifications alone, but the product's ability to serve the customer's needs.
> - **Three Categories of Customers** (Per Juran): External, Internal, and Suppliers.
> - **Quality Planning Roadmap Steps (The "Roadmap" is QP's core process):** **6 Steps**
> - **Quality Control Universal Sequence Steps:** **4 Steps (The "Feedback Loop")**
> - **Quality Improvement Two-Part Journey:** **Diagnostic Journey + Remedial Journey**
> - **Year Juran Published the Trilogy:** First articulated in his 1986 book *"Juran's Quality Handbook"* (Juran's exact year not required by KTU, but concept is mandatory).
> - **Juran's Place in History:** Co-developed the **Pareto Principle (80/20 Rule)** with Vilfredo Pareto, used in Quality Improvement prioritization.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of the Trilogy — Structured Breakdown

### **Pillar 1: Quality Planning (QP)**

**Definition:** The process of preparing to meet quality goals. It establishes *what* must be done to satisfy customers.

**Why it exists:** Without planning, an organization reacts to problems instead of preventing them.

**How it works (The Quality Planning Roadmap – 6 Steps):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 6 Steps of Quality Planning (HIGH-YIELD — Almost always asked in KTU 2-mark or 14-mark questions):**
>
> 1. **Identify the customers** — both internal and external.
> 2. **Determine customer needs** — use market research, VOC (Voice of Customer), interviews, complaints.
> 3. **Translate needs into language of the producer** — develop technical specifications (e.g., "food should be low-salt" becomes "sodium content < 200 mg per serving").
> 4. **Develop a product that can meet the needs** — design the product/process features.
> 5. **Develop processes capable of producing the product** — choose machinery, methods, controls.
> 6. **Establish process controls and transfer to operations** — handover to production with feedback mechanisms.

**Output of QP:** A documented set of specifications, processes, and control plans ready to be deployed.

### **Pillar 2: Quality Control (QC)**

**Definition:** The operational process of measuring actual quality performance, comparing it to goals, and acting on the difference.

**Why it exists:** Even the best plans deviate during execution. QC is the *feedback loop* that detects and corrects those deviations.

**How it works (The Quality Control Sequence – 4 Steps):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 4 Steps of Quality Control (UNIVERSAL SEQUENCE — Board favorite):**
>
> 1. **Evaluate actual quality performance** — gather data using inspection, testing, sensors, statistical process control.
> 2. **Compare actual performance to quality goals (specifications)** — determine the gap (deviation).
> 3. **Act on the difference** — if performance meets goals, do nothing; if not, take corrective action.
> 4. **Establish a feedback loop** — provide the corrective action information back to the planning and execution stages.

**Key Insight:** Quality Control **does NOT set new goals** — it merely maintains the existing ones. The "operating forces" (human, machine, material, method, measurement, environment — the **6Ms**) are brought under control.

### **Pillar 3: Quality Improvement (QI)**

**Definition:** The process of breaking through to unprecedented levels of performance by establishing the *infrastructure* and *processes* to achieve it.

**Why it exists:** Competition, technology, and customer expectations evolve. Standing still means falling behind.

**How it works (The Two Journeys of QI):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Juran's Two Journeys in Quality Improvement (Frequently tested in 14-mark answers):**
>
> **A. Diagnostic Journey (Find the root cause):**
> - Establish the problem (define, identify, state goal).
> - Verify the problem with data.
> - Identify root causes (use Pareto analysis — 80/20 rule).
> - Validate the root causes with evidence.
>
> **B. Remedial Journey (Fix the root cause):**
> - Decide on a remedy for each root cause.
> - Establish controls to hold the gains.
> - Implement the remedy on a pilot basis.
> - Roll out the solution to all affected operations.

**Key Difference from QC:**
- **QC** = Maintains the *current* level.
- **QI** = Creates a *new, higher* level (called a **Breakthrough**).

## 2.2 Interrelationship — The Trilogy as a Spiral

The three processes are not sequential, isolated events. They form a **continuous spiral**:

$$ QP \rightarrow QC \rightarrow QI \rightarrow \text{(New) } QP \rightarrow \text{(New) } QC \rightarrow \text{(New) } QI \rightarrow \cdots $$

Each cycle of QI raises the performance baseline, requiring new planning, which is then controlled, and so on — analogous to a **spiral staircase** (a famous Juran illustration).

## 2.3 KTU Formula / Concept Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **No mathematical formulas exist for Juran's Trilogy — it is a conceptual management framework. Below is the conceptual "cheat sheet" summarizing the components KTU expects you to know.**

| # | Process | Full Name | Core Question it Answers | Key Steps | Output |
|---|---------|-----------|--------------------------|-----------|--------|
| 1 | **QP** | Quality Planning | *What must we do?* | 6-Step Roadmap | Specifications, Process design, Control plan |
| 2 | **QC** | Quality Control | *Are we doing it right?* | 4-Step Universal Sequence | Performance vs. Goal data, Corrective actions |
| 3 | **QI** | Quality Improvement | *How do we do it better?* | Diagnostic + Remedial Journeys | Breakthrough, New higher performance level |

| Concept | Juran's Term | KTU 2024 Explanation |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Definition of Quality | *Fitness for Use* | Customer satisfaction with the product's ability to meet needs |
| Problem Analysis Tool | **Pareto Principle (80/20 Rule)** | 80% of problems come from 20% of causes |
| Customers (3 types) | External, Internal, Suppliers | Anyone who receives the output of a process |
| Operating Forces | **6Ms** | Man, Machine, Material, Method, Measurement, Mother Nature (Environment) |
| Quality Loop | **Feedback Loop** | QC step 4 — feeds correction back to production |
| QP Output | **Design and Operational Specifications** | Technical targets derived from customer needs |
| QI Term | **Breakthrough** | A dramatic, structural improvement (not incremental) |
| QP Sign-off | **Transfer to Operations** | Handover of plans from design to production |
| QC Action | **Act on the Difference** | Take corrective action only when deviation is found |
| QI Sign-off | **Hold the Gains** | Implement controls to prevent regression to old level |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Manufacturing (e.g., Maruti, Tata Motors):** QP sets the car's crash-test and mileage specs; QC performs daily checks; QI introduces a new engine technology to beat competitors.
- **Software (e.g., Infosys, TCS):** QP plans the SDLC; QC tests and reviews; QI adopts DevOps/AI to improve release cycles.
- **Healthcare (e.g., KTU region hospitals):** QP plans patient care protocols; QC audits compliance; QI reduces infection rates through new sterilization techniques.
- **Service (e.g., Indian Railways):** QP designs booking systems; QC checks for delays/cancellations; QI introduces e-ticketing and live tracking.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix: When to Use QP vs. QC vs. QI

> [!NOTE]
> This matrix is frequently tested in KTU 14-mark questions with a scenario. Use it as a decision-making tool.

| Situation Observed | Problem Type | Process to Apply | Juran's Justification |
|--------------------|--------------|------------------|------------------------|
| A new product is being launched for the first time | No existing process | **Quality Planning** | Need to establish specs from scratch |
| Production output is fluctuating around the spec, with some defects | Chronic waste / sporadic deviation | **Quality Control** | The goal is to *meet* existing specs — control is enough |
| Competitor's product has better features; we are losing market share | Strategic gap | **Quality Improvement** | Need to break through to a *new* performance level |
| Defect rate has been 5% for years — "that's just how it is" | Cultural stagnation | **Quality Improvement** | Old level is no longer acceptable — breakthrough required |
| Defect rate suddenly jumps to 15% today | Sporadic / special cause | **Quality Control** | Firefighting — fix the deviation, restore the level |

## 3.2 The Quality Planning Roadmap — Exhaustive 6-Step Derivation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Quality Planning Roadmap is **Juran's proprietary 6-step process**. Every KTU answer should list all 6 steps with one-line explanations.

**Step 1: Identify the customers**
$$\text{Inputs: Customer list, market segmentation} \rightarrow \text{Output: Identified customers (internal \& external)}$$
- *Why:* You cannot satisfy a customer you have not identified. Includes the **end-user** AND the **next process in line** (internal customer).

**Step 2: Determine customer needs**
$$\text{Inputs: Customer interviews, complaints, surveys, observation} \rightarrow \text{Output: Customer needs statement}$$
- *Why:* Customers often state *wants* (e.g., "I want a fast car") but the *need* may be "I need to commute in 30 minutes."
- *How:* Use **affinity diagrams** and **Kano model** (introductory awareness only for KTU).

**Step 3: Translate needs into the producer's language (Specifications)**
$$\text{Customer need (qualitative)} \xrightarrow{\text{Translate}} \text{Specification (quantitative)}$$
- *Example:* "Easy to hold" becomes "Handle diameter = 3.5 cm, Material = slip-resistant rubber."
- *Why:* Producers and engineers think in numbers — customers think in feelings.

**Step 4: Develop a product that meets the specifications**
$$\text{Specs} \xrightarrow{\text{Design}} \text{Product prototype}$$
- *Why:* A spec is just a target. The product must *actually* meet it under real conditions.

**Step 5: Develop processes capable of producing the product**
$$\text{Product} \xrightarrow{\text{Process design}} \text{Capable process}$$
- *Why:* A brilliant design is useless if the factory cannot make it consistently. **Process capability** must be measured.

**Step 6: Establish process controls and transfer to operations**
$$\text{Capable process} \xrightarrow{\text{Controls + Handover}} \text{Operational production}$$
- *Why:* The handoff is critical. Without it, planning remains on paper.

## 3.3 The Quality Control Universal Sequence — Exhaustive 4-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Choose the control subject (what to measure)**
- *Why:* You cannot control everything. Choose based on customer impact and process criticality.

**Step 2: Choose a unit of measurement (the metric)**
- *Why:* Every quality characteristic needs an **operational definition** (e.g., "defect" must be precisely defined).

**Step 3: Establish the measurement method and frequency**
- *Why:* Measuring too often is wasteful; measuring too rarely loses information.

**Step 4: Interpret the difference and act on it**
$$\text{Action} = f(\text{Actual} - \text{Goal})$$
- *If* Actual = Goal: Continue as planned (no action).
- *If* Actual ≠ Goal: Diagnose the cause and correct it (this is the feedback loop).

## 3.4 Quality Improvement — Exhaustive Two-Journey Derivation

### **Diagnostic Journey (5 sub-steps)**

1. **Define the problem:** Symptom vs. Problem distinction (e.g., "5% defect rate" is symptom; "Why?" is problem).
2. **Verify the problem:** Use data — Pareto chart, histograms, control charts.
3. **Identify root causes:** Use cause-and-effect (Ishikawa) diagrams, the 5 Whys.
4. **Validate the root cause:** Pilot study, hypothesis testing.
5. **Establish the hypothesis for remedy.**

### **Remedial Journey (4 sub-steps)**

1. **Decide on a remedy** for each validated root cause.
2. **Implement the remedy** on a **pilot basis** (small-scale trial).
3. **Confirm the result** — measure again, validate breakthrough.
4. **Roll out to all operations** and **establish controls to hold the gains**.

## 3.5 Critical Comparison — QP vs. QC vs. QI

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is the most-asked KTU Part B sub-question (7 marks). Memorize this matrix:**

| Comparison Parameter | Quality Planning (QP) | Quality Control (QC) | Quality Improvement (QI) |
|----------------------|------------------------|------------------------|--------------------------|
| **Primary Goal** | Establish processes to meet goals | Maintain current performance | Achieve new, higher performance |
| **Timing** | Before production starts | During production | Anytime a breakthrough is needed |
| **Frequency** | Project-based (one-time) | Continuous (daily) | Project-based (periodic) |
| **Responsible for Goals** | Yes (sets them) | No (only follows them) | Yes (raises them) |
| **Output** | Plans, specs, controls | Performance data, fixes | New process capability |
| **Trigger** | New product / new customer | Deviation from spec | Strategic gap / chronic waste |
| **Juran's Key Word** | *Roadmap* | *Feedback Loop* | *Breakthrough* |
| **Relation to Specs** | Creates specs | Operates within specs | Changes the specs |

## 3.6 Why the Trilogy is *Universal* (Juran's Claim)

Juran argued that every nation, industry, and company in history has *always* performed these three activities, though often unproductively. His contribution was to:
1. **Identify** them as distinct, manageable processes.
2. **Name** them with universal terminology.
3. **Order** them into a coherent sequence (QP → QC → QI).
4. **Apply** a structured roadmap/journey to each (so they are not random).

This is why KTU teaches the Trilogy — it provides a **mental model** for tackling any quality problem in any engineering domain.
---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Mermaid Diagram — The Juran Trilogy Spiral

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["Quality Planning QP"] --> B["Quality Control QC"]
    B --> C["Quality Improvement QI"]
    C --> D["New Higher Performance Level"]
    D --> E["New Quality Planning QP 2"]
    E --> F["Quality Control QC 2"]
    F --> G["Quality Improvement QI 2"]
    G --> H["Continuous Spiral of Quality"]

    style A fill:#1e90ff,stroke:#003366,color:#ffffff
    style B fill:#28a745,stroke:#003300,color:#ffffff
    style C fill:#ff8c00,stroke:#663300,color:#ffffff

```

**How to read this diagram (for KTU answer writing):**
- The **flow goes QP → QC → QI → (New) QP** — a continuous loop.
- Each QI raises the bar; the new bar requires a *new* QP, which is then controlled, improved, and so on.
- This is the famous **"Juran's Spiral of Quality"** illustration.

## 4.2 Detailed Block Diagram — Quality Planning Roadmap (6 Steps)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1["Step 1: Identify Customers"] --> S2["Step 2: Determine Customer Needs"]
    S2 --> S3["Step 3: Translate Needs into Specifications"]
    S3 --> S4["Step 4: Develop Product to Meet Specs"]
    S4 --> S5["Step 5: Develop Capable Processes"]
    S5 --> S6["Step 6: Establish Controls and Transfer to Operations"]

    style S2 fill:#87ceeb,stroke:#003366,color:#000000
    style S3 fill:#87ceeb,stroke:#003366,color:#000000

    style S5 fill:#87ceeb,stroke:#003366,color:#000000
    style S6 fill:#1e90ff,stroke:#003366,color:#ffffff
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Quality Control Universal Sequence (4 Steps) with Feedback Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    QC1["Step 1: Evaluate Actual Performance"] --> QC2["Step 2: Compare with Quality Goals"]
    QC2 --> QC3{"Step 3: Is there a Difference?"}
    QC3 -- "No" --> QC4["Continue Operations No Action Needed"]
    QC3 -- "Yes" --> QC5["Diagnose Cause of Deviation"]
    QC5 --> QC6["Take Corrective Action"]
    QC6 --> QC7["Step 4: Establish Feedback Loop to Planning"]
    QC7 --> QC1

    style QC1 fill:#28a745,stroke:#003300,color:#ffffff
    style QC2 fill:#28a745,stroke:#003300,color:#ffffff

    style QC6 fill:#ff8c00,stroke:#663300,color:#ffffff

```

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Quality Improvement Two Journeys

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    DIAG["DIAGNOSTIC JOURNEY Find the Root Cause"] --> DJ1["Define the problem clearly"]
    DJ1 --> DJ2["Verify with data and Pareto"]
    DJ2 --> DJ3["Identify root causes 5 Whys Ishikawa"]
    DJ3 --> DJ4["Validate root cause with evidence"]

    DJ4 --> REM["REMEDIAL JOURNEY Fix the Root Cause"]
    REM --> RJ1["Decide on a remedy"]
    RJ1 --> RJ2["Pilot test the remedy"]
    RJ2 --> RJ3["Confirm the breakthrough"]
```

## 4.5 Block Diagram — Relationship Between QP, QC, and QI

```mermaid

    subgraph PLANNING["QUALITY PLANNING QP"]
        P1["Sets Quality Goals and Specifications"]
```

- **QP sets the goalposts.**
- **QC plays within those goalposts.**
- **QI moves the goalposts to a better field.**

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU Part A requires **2 questions of 3 marks each** from every module in the **University Exam**. Below are model questions with precise, exam-ready answers.

---

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]**
**Define Juran's Quality Trilogy. List its three components.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Juran's Quality Trilogy is a universal framework for managing quality proposed by Dr. Joseph M. Juran. It defines quality management as a systematic, structured process consisting of three interconnected managerial processes. The three components are:

1. **Quality Planning (QP):** Identifying customers and their needs, and developing products and processes to meet those needs.
2. **Quality Control (QC):** Evaluating actual quality performance against goals and taking corrective action on the difference.
3. **Quality Improvement (QI):** Establishing the infrastructure to continuously break through to higher levels of performance.

*(Marking scheme: 1 mark for definition + 1.5 marks for the 3 components + 0.5 mark for any one-line elaboration of each.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024 Model]**
**What is the difference between Quality Control and Quality Improvement as per Juran?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Quality Control (QC) | Quality Improvement (QI) |
|--------|----------------------|--------------------------|
| **Purpose** | Maintain current quality level | Achieve a new, higher level |
| **Action on goals** | Works *within* existing goals | *Changes* the goals (breakthrough) |
| **Trigger** | Day-to-day operations | Strategic, periodic projects |
| **Output** | Performance data + corrective action | New process capability |
| **Juran's term** | Feedback loop | Breakthrough |

**Key Point:** QC is *control* (no new goals), while QI is *improvement* (sets new goals).

*(Marking scheme: 2 marks for the table + 1 mark for the key distinguishing sentence.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Question (14 Marks) — Internal Choice (Two Alternatives)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU Part B in the End Semester Exam gives a **choice between two questions** (one of the two must be answered). Each Part B question carries **14 marks** and is typically broken into sub-parts (a) and (b) of 7 marks each.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 1**
**[KTU University Exam - KTU Model Paper Style]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the three components of Juran's Quality Trilogy with examples. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the 6-step Quality Planning Roadmap proposed by Juran. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Answer — Part (a) (7 marks)**

Juran's Quality Trilogy consists of three managerial processes, applicable to all functions and levels of an organization. They are:

**1. Quality Planning (QP):**
Quality planning is the process of preparing to meet quality goals. It involves identifying customers (both internal and external), determining their needs, translating those needs into technical specifications, and developing products and processes capable of meeting those specifications.
*Example:* A mobile phone manufacturer (e.g., Samsung) identifies that customers need a phone with long battery life. This is translated into the specification "5000 mAh battery" and a manufacturing process is designed to deliver it.

**2. Quality Control (QC):**
Quality control is the operational process of measuring actual quality performance, comparing it to the quality goals, and taking corrective action when performance falls short. It uses a 4-step universal sequence.
*Example:* The same phone manufacturer inspects each batch — if the battery's actual capacity is 4800 mAh, the batch is rejected and the production line is corrected.

**3. Quality Improvement (QI):**
Quality improvement is the process of breaking through to unprecedented levels of performance. It uses the Diagnostic Journey (find the root cause) and the Remedial Journey (fix the root cause).
*Example:* The company realizes the industry's new benchmark is 6000 mAh. It launches a QI project to redesign the battery cell chemistry to reach this new breakthrough level.

**Interrelationship:** The three processes form a continuous spiral — QP sets the standard, QC maintains it, and QI raises it, after which the new standard goes back to QP.

*(Marking scheme: 2 marks per component with example + 1 mark for the interrelationship.)*

---

#### **Model Answer — Part (b) (7 marks)**

Juran's Quality Planning Roadmap consists of **6 systematic steps**:

1. **Identify the customers** — Internal and external customers must be clearly identified. Internal customers are the next process in the workflow.
2. **Determine customer needs** — Use Voice of Customer (VOC) tools: interviews, surveys, complaint analysis, market research. Needs (not just wants) must be identified.
3. **Translate customer needs into the producer's language** — Develop measurable specifications. For example, "car should be fuel-efficient" becomes "mileage ≥ 20 km/l under standard test conditions."
4. **Develop a product that can meet the specifications** — Design the product so that it is physically capable of meeting the specs.
5. **Develop processes capable of producing the product** — Design the production process; ensure process capability (Cp, Cpk) is high enough.
6. **Establish process controls and transfer to operations** — Define control points, inspection methods, and feedback mechanisms. Hand over the plan to operations with documentation and training.

**Key Point:** The Roadmap provides a structured, repeatable, and universal approach to planning quality. It is a **project-based** process that is done *before* production starts.

*(Marking scheme: 1 mark per step + 1 mark for the key-point summary.)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 2**
**[KTU University Exam - KTU Model Paper Style]**

**(a)** Explain Juran's Quality Control Universal Sequence in detail. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss Juran's two journeys in Quality Improvement with examples. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Answer — Part (a) (7 marks)**

Juran's **Quality Control Universal Sequence** is a 4-step feedback loop applicable to any process:

1. **Evaluate actual quality performance:**
   The actual performance of the process is measured using inspection, testing, or statistical process control (SPC) charts. The data must be reliable and timely.
   *Example:* Measuring the diameter of manufactured shafts on the shop floor.

2. **Compare actual performance to quality goals (specifications):**
   The measured data is compared against the pre-set quality goals. The difference (deviation) is identified.
   *Example:* Comparing the measured shaft diameter (49.8 mm) to the spec (50 ± 0.1 mm).

3. **Act on the difference:**
   - If actual = goal → No action is required; continue operations.
   - If actual ≠ goal → Diagnose the cause (assignable cause) and take corrective action to bring the process back to spec.

4. **Establish a feedback loop:**
   The corrective action information is fed back to the planning stage and to the operators so that the same deviation does not recur. This closes the loop and enables continuous control.

**Key Concept:** QC *does not* set new goals — it only operates *within* the goals established by QP. The 6Ms (Man, Machine, Material, Method, Measurement, Mother Nature) are the controllable operating forces.

*(Marking scheme: 1.5 marks per step + 1 mark for the key concept summary.)*

---

#### **Model Answer — Part (b) (7 marks)**

Juran's Quality Improvement uses **two complementary journeys**:

**1. Diagnostic Journey (Finding the root cause):**
This is a structured analysis of *why* a chronic problem exists. Steps:
- **Define the problem** clearly (symptom vs. problem).
- **Verify the problem** with data (control charts, Pareto analysis).
- **Identify root causes** using 5-Whys, Ishikawa fishbone, or 80/20 rule.
- **Validate the root cause** with experiments or pilot studies.
*Example:* A hospital finds that 20% of its surgeries are getting delayed. The diagnostic journey reveals the root cause is unsterilized instruments (Pareto: 80% of delays are due to 20% of equipment).

**2. Remedial Journey (Fixing the root cause):**
This is the structured implementation of the solution. Steps:
- **Decide on a remedy** for each root cause.
- **Pilot test the remedy** on a small scale.
- **Confirm the breakthrough** with new data.
- **Roll out to all operations** and **establish controls to hold the gains.**
*Example (cont.):* The hospital implements a new sterilization protocol, pilots it in one ward, confirms zero delays, then rolls it out to all wards and trains staff to hold the new standard.

**Key Concept:** QI differs from QC because it **raises the performance level** (a "breakthrough") rather than maintaining it. Once the new level is achieved, it becomes the new baseline, and the Trilogy repeats.

*(Marking scheme: 3.5 marks for each journey with example.)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cause Mark Deductions in Juran's Trilogy Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing QC and QI** — Most students answer "Quality Control" when the question asks for "Quality Improvement." Remember: **QC maintains, QI improves (breakthrough).**
> 2. **Listing only 2 steps in QP Roadmap** — KTU expects **all 6 steps**. Forgetting the last step (Transfer to Operations) is a common 1-mark loss.
> 3. **Forgetting the Feedback Loop in QC** — Step 4 of QC is **NOT** "take action." It is **"establish a feedback loop."** Many students miss this distinction.
> 4. **Confusing "Customer" with "End User" only** — Juran's definition includes **internal customers** (the next process in the workflow). Missing this loses marks.
> 5. **Not mentioning "Breakthrough" in QI** — Every QI answer should explicitly use the word "breakthrough" and explain it as a *new, higher* performance level.
> 6. **Writing the Trilogy as a one-time sequence** — Juran emphasized a **continuous spiral**. Forgetting this loses the "interrelationship" marks in 14-mark answers.
> 7. **Skipping the Diagnostic vs. Remedial distinction** — In QI questions, students often combine the two journeys. KTU requires them to be presented **separately**.
> 8. **No example given** — KTU 14-mark questions *always* expect at least one example per concept. An answer without examples loses 1–2 marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Exam Prep):**

- **Author:** Dr. Joseph M. Juran (Father of Quality / Quality Guru).
- **Definition of Quality (Juran):** *"Fitness for Use"* — the product's ability to serve the customer's purpose.
- **Trilogy = 3 processes:** **QP, QC, QI.**
- **Quality Planning (QP) — 6-Step Roadmap:**
  1. Identify customers.
  2. Determine customer needs.
  3. Translate needs into specifications.
  4. Develop product to meet specs.
  5. Develop capable processes.
  6. Establish controls and transfer to operations.
- **Quality Control (QC) — 4-Step Universal Sequence:**
  1. Evaluate actual performance.
  2. Compare with quality goals.
  3. Act on the difference.
  4. Establish feedback loop.
- **Quality Improvement (QI) — Two Journeys:**
  - **Diagnostic Journey** → Find the root cause.
  - **Remedial Journey** → Fix the root cause.
- **Key Distinction:**
  - **QC** = *Maintain* the level (no new goals).
  - **QI** = *Raise* the level (breakthrough, new goals).
- **QC operating forces:** **6Ms** — Man, Machine, Material, Method, Measurement, Mother Nature (Environment).
- **Customers (3 types):** External, Internal, Suppliers.
- **Pareto Principle (80/20 Rule):** 80% of problems come from 20% of causes — used heavily in **QI** to prioritize root causes.
- **Trilogy is a Spiral, not a Cycle:** Each QI raises the bar, leading to a new QP, new QC, new QI, *ad infinitum*.
- **Management Responsibility:** Juran emphasized that the Trilogy is the responsibility of *management*, not just workers — quality is a *managerial* act.
- **The Trilogy is Universal:** Applies to manufacturing, services, software, healthcare, education — every domain.
- **Critical Terminology for KTU:**
  - *Roadmap* → QP
  - *Feedback Loop* → QC
  - *Breakthrough* → QI
  - *Diagnostic & Remedial Journey* → QI
  - *Fitness for Use* → Juran's Quality Definition
  - *Operational Definition* → How to measure a quality characteristic
  - *Process Capability* → A QP output measure
- **Diagram to remember for the exam:** The **Juran Spiral** — QP → QC → QI → (New) QP → (New) QC → (New) QI.
- **Juran's Famous Quote (good for introductory lines in answers):** *"Quality is fitness for use."*

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Crosby's Fourteen Steps for Quality Improvement
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET758 - QUALITY ENGINEERING AND MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Crosby's Fourteen Steps for Quality Improvement

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

**Crosby's Fourteen Steps for Quality Improvement** is a prescriptive, step-by-step organizational change management framework formulated by **Philip B. Crosby** (published in his 1979 seminal work *"Quality is Free"*) for transforming a company's quality culture from defect-acceptance to defect-prevention. 

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, the framework is a **Quality Management Roadmap (QMR)** that operationalizes the principle of **"Zero Defects"** and the doctrine of **"Quality is Free"** — the latter asserting that the cost of prevention is invariably lower than the cost of correction.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Philip B. Crosby (1926–2001) was an American quality management luminary whose work underpins Module 1 of PEMET758. His **Four Absolutes of Quality** and **14-Step Program** are the foundational building blocks of modern Total Quality Management (TQM).

## 1.2 The Four Absolutes of Quality (Crosby's Core Philosophy)

Before examining the 14 steps, the KTU examiner expects you to know Crosby's **Four Absolutes of Quality**:

| # | Absolute of Quality | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Quality = Conformance to Requirements** | Not elegance; not goodness. It is simply doing what the specification says. |
| 2 | **Prevention, not Inspection** | The system must be designed to *prevent* errors, not just catch them. |
| 3 | **Zero Defects Performance Standard** | The target must be perfection, not "good enough." |
| 4 | **Measure Quality by the Price of Non-Conformance (PONC)** | Quality is measured in monetary terms — the cost of doing things wrong. |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> **Real-World Analogy — "The Hospital Emergency Room vs. The Gym"**
> 
> Imagine two approaches to heart disease. 
> - **Reactive (Inspection-based):** Wait for a heart attack, then rush the patient to the ER for costly surgery. This is what the old "inspection" approach did in factories — find the defect, rework it, ship it.
> - **Preventive (Crosby's approach):** Encourage the patient to join a gym, eat well, and exercise daily. The cost of the gym membership is **far less** than the cost of bypass surgery. 
> 
> Crosby's 14 steps are the **"gym membership" of manufacturing** — a structured 14-action prescription to install prevention, awareness, training, and measurement into the corporate DNA so that defects (heart attacks) never happen in the first place.

## 1.4 Key Metrics in Crosby's Framework (KTU Standard Vocabulary)

- **PONC** — Price of Non-Conformance (in dollars/currency).
- **COQ** — Cost of Quality (Prevention + Appraisal + Failure).
- **ZD** — Zero Defects (the philosophical and operational target).
- **QIT** — Quality Improvement Team (cross-functional steering body).
- **ZDD** — Zero Defects Day (awareness event marking the cultural shift).

> [!NOTE]
> **Crosby's Famous One-Liner (High-frequency in KTU exams):**
> *"Quality is free. It's not a gift, but it is free. What costs money are the unquality things — all the actions that involve not doing jobs right the first time."*

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Fourteen Steps — Structured Logic Analysis

Crosby deliberately ordered the 14 steps in a **phased rollout sequence** — from top-down leadership commitment to cultural entrenchment. For KTU 2024 board exams, memorizing them in order is mandatory (commonly asked as a 6–8 mark question).

### PHASE A — Foundation (Steps 1–4)
*Establishing the strategic and measurement bedrock.*

**Step 1: Management Commitment & Involvement**
- **Why:** Quality cannot be delegated downward. Senior leadership must *visibly* commit — not merely sign a policy document.
- **How:** Management states in writing its commitment to the quality program, sets a deadline for the program to be operational, and allocates a personal quality budget.

**Step 2: Quality Improvement Team (QIT)**
- **Why:** A cross-functional steering committee is needed to coordinate the rollout.
- **How:** Form a QIT of 5–8 members drawn from various departments, chaired by a senior manager (often the CEO or a designate). The QIT acts as the "Board of Directors" of the quality program.

**Step 3: Quality Measurement**
- **Why:** "You cannot manage what you do not measure."
- **How:** Establish a uniform system to track the cost of non-conformance (PONC) and the location of defects. A defect location profile is drawn.

**Step 4: Cost of Quality (COQ) Evaluation**
- **Why:** Translates quality into the language of the boardroom — money.
- **How:** Quantify all quality-related costs (prevention, appraisal, internal failure, external failure) and present them to management as a percentage of sales.

### PHASE B — Awareness & Education (Steps 5–7)
*Building understanding and the prevention mindset.*

**Step 5: Quality Awareness**
- **Why:** Most employees are unaware of the true cost of defects and the meaning of conformance.
- **How:** Conduct company-wide awareness campaigns, posters, booklets, and seminars. The phrase *"Quality is Free"* is disseminated.

**Step 6: Corrective Action**
- **Why:** Existing systems produce defects daily; they must be fixed during the transition.
- **How:** Establish a formal Corrective Action Committee to analyse defect data and propose permanent systemic fixes (not just rework).

**Step 7: Zero Defects Planning**
- **Why:** A philosophical target must be set so the organization aims for perfection rather than "acceptable defect levels" (AQL).
- **How:** The QIT drafts a Zero Defects plan detailing how each department will adopt the ZD mindset and the timetable for rollout.

### PHASE C — Adoption (Steps 8–10)
*Embedding the philosophy into daily work.*

**Step 8: Training**
- **Why:** Without skills, awareness is hollow.
- **How:** All employees undergo formal quality training — defect identification, statistical tools, and the ZD philosophy. Supervisors are trained first.

**Step 9: Zero Defects Day (ZDD)**
- **Why:** A symbolic, company-wide event galvanizes the cultural shift.
- **How:** On a single, pre-announced day, the CEO formally launches ZD. Every employee receives a ZD pin and signs a pledge card. Media, ceremonies, and speeches are used.

**Step 10: Goal Setting**
- **Why:** Targets convert philosophy into measurable action.
- **How:** Each individual and team sets 30-day, 60-day, and 90-day improvement goals. Progress is reviewed periodically.

### PHASE D — Sustainability (Steps 11–14)
*Ensuring the program outlives the launch.*

**Step 11: Error Cause Removal**
- **Why:** Systems (not people) are the root cause of most errors. Employees must be encouraged to surface systemic blockers.
- **How:** Establish a formal channel (often a "Do It Right the First Time" — DIRFT — suggestion scheme) for employees to report systemic obstacles.

**Step 12: Recognition**
- **Why:** Behaviour sustained by recognition.
- **How:** Publicly celebrate contributors who exemplify the ZD philosophy. Tangible awards, certificates, and ceremonies reinforce the desired behaviour.

**Step 13: Quality Councils**
- **Why:** Permanent governance ensures the program does not atrophy.
- **How:** Constitute standing Quality Councils (often quarterly meetings) composed of professionals from different functions who share best practices and audit progress.

**Step 14: Do It Over Again**
- **Why:** Quality improvement is a journey, not a destination.
- **How:** After the initial cycle, restart the 14-step process to address remaining issues. The ZD philosophy must be **continuously refreshed**.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

Although Crosby's framework is largely management-oriented, the **Cost of Quality (COQ)** and **Price of Non-Conformance (PONC)** use quantifiable formulas. The KTU 2024 examiner may expect a numerical problem on COQ.

**Table 1: Crosby's Core Equations**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $COQ = C_P + C_A + C_{IF} + C_{EF}$ | Total Cost of Quality = Prevention + Appraisal + Internal Failure + External Failure | Currency (₹ / $) |
| 2 | $PONC = C_{IF} + C_{EF}$ | Price of Non-Conformance = the money wasted on doing things wrong | Currency |
| 3 | $COQ\ \% = \dfrac{COQ}{Sales\ Revenue} \times 100$ | Cost of Quality as a percentage of sales (typical industry benchmark: 15–40%) | % |
| 4 | $Z_{eff} = 1 - \dfrac{D_{actual}}{D_{target}}$ | Zero Defects effectiveness index, where $D$ is defect count | Dimensionless |
| 5 | $ROI_{Quality} = \dfrac{C_{waste\ avoided} - C_{program}}{C_{program}}$ | Return on investment of the quality program | Ratio |
| 6 | $DPMO = \dfrac{D_{total}}{N_{opportunities}} \times 10^6$ | Defects Per Million Opportunities (Six Sigma derivative, often referenced) | ppm |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Always remember that for Crosby, $COQ$ is the *sum* of the PAF (Prevention–Appraisal–Failure) categories. The **target** is to reduce $C_{IF}$ and $C_{EF}$ to near-zero by increasing $C_P$ (prevention investment) — a classic economic substitution.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Utility of Crosby's Framework

Crosby's 14-step program is widely adopted in:

- **Manufacturing industries** (automotive, electronics) for ISO 9000/TS 16949 implementation.
- **Service industries** (banks, hospitals, IT help-desks) where "conformance to requirements" applies to service-level agreements.
- **Software engineering** (the "definition of done" is a direct descendant of ZD thinking).
- **Government & Defence** (NASA's and ISRO's quality assurance manuals borrow heavily from Crosby's prevention doctrine).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Pattern Tip:** When asked to compare Crosby with Deming or Juran, always emphasize that **Deming emphasizes statistical control**, **Juran emphasizes customer-focused planning**, while **Crosby emphasizes prevention, conformance, and the zero-defects standard** — a behavioural/cultural approach.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

Because this topic belongs to **Quality Engineering and Management** (a non-programming subject), the "derivations" here are *conceptual and mathematical* — specifically, the **derivation of the Cost of Quality (COQ)** model and the **logical sequencing rationale** of Crosby's 14 steps.

## 3.1 Derivation of the Cost of Quality (COQ) Model — PAF Framework

Crosby operationalized quality cost measurement using the **PAF (Prevention–Appraisal–Failure) Model**. The total Cost of Quality is the sum of four mutually exclusive cost categories.

### 3.1.1 Step-by-Step Mathematical Derivation

**Step 1: Define the Universe of Quality Costs**

Let $\mathcal{Q}$ denote the total Cost of Quality incurred by an organization over a period (typically one financial year). Crosby partitioned $\mathcal{Q}$ into **four exhaustive, non-overlapping sub-categories**.

**Step 2: Identify the Four Sub-Categories**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathcal{Q} &= \{ \text{Prevention Costs} \} \cup \{ \text{Appraisal Costs} \} \cup \{ \text{Internal Failure Costs} \} \cup \{ \text{External Failure Costs} \} \\[4pt]
\mathcal{C}_P &\triangleq \text{Prevention Costs} \\[4pt]
\mathcal{C}_A &\triangleq \text{Appraisal Costs} \\[4pt]
\mathcal{C}_{IF} &\triangleq \text{Internal Failure Costs} \\[4pt]
\mathcal{C}_{EF} &\triangleq \text{External Failure Costs}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Apply Set-Theoretic Disjointness**

Since Crosby assumes the four categories are *mutually exclusive* (a cost is classified into exactly one bucket), the total cost is the arithmetic sum:

$$
\mathcal{Q} = \mathcal{C}_P + \mathcal{C}_A + \mathcal{C}_{IF} + \mathcal{C}_{EF}
$$

This is the **PAF Equation**, also called the **Cost of Quality Equation**.

**Step 4: Derive the Price of Non-Conformance (PONC)**

Failure costs are precisely the costs of *non-conformance* — the money lost because the product or service failed to meet requirements. Hence:

$$
PONC = \mathcal{C}_{IF} + \mathcal{C}_{EF}
$$

**Step 5: Express PONC as a Fraction of Total Quality Cost**

The PONC ratio is a key performance indicator in Crosby's framework. In a poorly managed organization, PONC can exceed 90% of $\mathcal{Q}$:

$$
\% PONC = \frac{\mathcal{C}_{IF} + \mathcal{C}_{EF}}{\mathcal{C}_P + \mathcal{C}_A + \mathcal{C}_{IF} + \mathcal{C}_{EF}} \times 100
$$

**Step 6: Express COQ as a Percentage of Sales (Boardroom Metric)**

Let $S$ denote annual sales revenue. The COQ ratio is the metric reported to the board:

$$
COQ\ \% = \frac{\mathcal{Q}}{S} \times 100
$$

**Step 7: Establish the Crosby Cost Substitution Principle**

Crosby's central economic insight is that investing in prevention ($\mathcal{C}_P$) reduces failure costs ($\mathcal{C}_{IF} + \mathcal{C}_{EF}$) by an even greater amount, making the *total* $\mathcal{Q}$ fall:

$$
\boxed{\;\Delta \mathcal{C}_P < -(\Delta \mathcal{C}_{IF} + \Delta \mathcal{C}_{EF}) \quad \Rightarrow \quad \Delta \mathcal{Q} < 0\;}
$$

**Interpretation:** A marginal increase in prevention spending produces a *larger* marginal reduction in failure costs, causing the net COQ to decline. This is the mathematical proof of "Quality is Free."

### 3.1.2 Illustrative Numerical Example (KTU-Style Problem)

> **Problem:** A manufacturing firm reports the following annual quality costs:
> - Prevention: ₹ 4,00,000
> - Appraisal: ₹ 2,00,000
> - Internal Failure: ₹ 10,00,000
> - External Failure: ₹ 8,00,000
> - Sales: ₹ 1,00,00,000
>
> Calculate: (i) Total COQ, (ii) PONC, (iii) COQ % of sales, (iv) % PONC.

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Step 1: Total COQ**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathcal{Q} &= \mathcal{C}_P + \mathcal{C}_A + \mathcal{C}_{IF} + \mathcal{C}_{EF} \\
&= 4{,}00{,}000 + 2{,}00{,}000 + 10{,}00{,}000 + 8{,}00{,}000 \\
&= 24{,}00{,}000 \text{ INR}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: PONC**

$$
\begin{aligned}
PONC &= \mathcal{C}_{IF} + \mathcal{C}_{EF} \\
&= 10{,}00{,}000 + 8{,}00{,}000 \\
&= 18{,}00{,}000 \text{ INR}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: COQ as % of Sales**

$$
\begin{aligned}
COQ\ \% &= \frac{24{,}00{,}000}{1{,}00{,}00{,}000} \times 100 \\
&= 24\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: % PONC**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\% PONC &= \frac{18{,}00{,}000}{24{,}00{,}000} \times 100 \\
&= 75\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** A PONC of 75% indicates that 75 paise of every rupee spent on quality is *waste* (failure cost) — a clear sign that prevention investment must be scaled up.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Phase-Wise Summary of Crosby's 14 Steps

The following matrix aligns each step with its phase, primary objective, KTU-typical keyword, and expected deliverable:

| Step | Name | Phase | Primary Objective | KTU Keyword | Deliverable |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Management Commitment | Foundation | Top-down ownership | "Top-down mandate" | Written policy & budget |
| 2 | Quality Improvement Team | Foundation | Cross-functional steering | "QIT constitution" | Charter & members |
| 3 | Quality Measurement | Foundation | Visibility of defects | "Defect profile" | Measurement system |
| 4 | Cost of Quality Evaluation | Foundation | Money-language reporting | "PAF model" | COQ report |
| 5 | Quality Awareness | Awareness | Mindset shift | "Awareness campaign" | Posters, seminars |
| 6 | Corrective Action | Awareness | Fix recurring defects | "Corrective Action Committee" | Issue log |
| 7 | Zero Defects Planning | Awareness | Set ZD roadmap | "ZD philosophy" | ZD plan document |
| 8 | Training | Adoption | Skill transfer | "DIRFT" (Do It Right First Time) | Training calendar |
| 9 | Zero Defects Day | Adoption | Cultural launch | "ZDD event" | ZD pin & pledge |
| 10 | Goal Setting | Adoption | Personal accountability | "30/60/90 day goals" | Goal sheet |
| 11 | Error Cause Removal | Sustainability | Remove systemic blockers | "Systemic fix" | Suggestion scheme |
| 12 | Recognition | Sustainability | Reinforce behaviour | "Behavioural reward" | Awards ceremony |
| 13 | Quality Councils | Sustainability | Permanent governance | "Standing council" | Council charter |
| 14 | Do It Over Again | Sustainability | Continuous improvement | "Cyclical refresh" | Re-launched program |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

This block diagram maps the **Prevention–Appraisal–Failure** cost categories and their functional flow inside an organization.

```mermaid

    REQ --> PREVENT
    REQ --> APPRAISE
    PREVENT --> APPRAISE

    APPRAISE --> EFAIL

    PREVENT -. "REDUCES" .-> IFAIL
    PREVENT -. "REDUCES" .-> EFAIL
    APPRAISE -. "DETECTS" .-> IFAIL
    APPRAISE -. "DETECTS" .-> EFAIL

    PREVENT --> TOT["TOTAL COQ EQUALS CP PLUS CA PLUS CIF PLUS CEF"]
    APPRAISE --> TOT

    EFAIL --> TOT

    TOT --> PONC["PONC EQUALS CIF PLUS CEF WASTE MONEY"]

```

This block diagram presents a comparative functional view of the three quality gurus — a common KTU 2014-Scheme and 2024-Scheme question.
