## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Anatomy of a Shewhart Control Chart

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Raw Subgroup Data X sub i] --> B[Compute Subgroup Statistic]
    B --> B1[X bar i for mean]
    B --> B2[R i for range]
    B --> B3[p i for proportion]
    B --> B4[c i for count]
    C[Collect k equals 20 to 25 Subgroups] --> B
    B1 --> D[Compute Grand Statistic]
    B2 --> D
    B3 --> D
    B4 --> D
    D --> E[Determine UCL CL LCL]
    E --> F[Plot Control Chart]
    F --> G{Apply Western Electric Rules}
    G -->|Rule 1 violated| H1[Out of Control Point]
    G -->|Rule 2 violated| H2[Shift Detected]
    G -->|Rule 3 violated| H3[Drift Detected]
    G -->|Rule 4 violated| H4[Sustained Shift]
    G -->|No violation| I[Process in Control]
    H1 --> J[Investigate and Remove Special Cause]
    H2 --> J
    H3 --> J
    H4 --> J
    I --> K[Common Cause Only: Improve System]
    J --> F
```

### 4.2 Decision Flow — Selecting the Correct Control Chart

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    S[Start: Identify Data Type] --> S1{What kind of data?}
    S1 -->|Continuous variable| V[Variable Chart Branch]
    S1 -->|Discrete attribute| A[Attribute Chart Branch]
    V --> V1{What is the subgroup size n?}
    V1 -->|n equals 1| IM[I minus MR Chart]
    V1 -->|n between 2 and 10| XR[X bar R Chart]
    V1 -->|n greater than 10| XS[X bar s Chart]
    A --> A1{What is being counted?}
    A1 -->|Defectives pass or fail| A2{Is sample size constant?}
    A1 -->|Defects count| A3{Is opportunity area constant?}
    A2 -->|Yes constant| NP[np Chart]
    A2 -->|No variable| P[p Chart]
    A3 -->|Yes constant| C[c Chart]
    A3 -->|No variable| U[u Chart]
```

### 4.3 Architecture Flow — 3-Sigma Logic of Control Limits

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Process Mean mu] --> S1[Plus 3 sigma: UCL]
    P --> S2[CL equals mu]
    P --> S3[Minus 3 sigma: LCL]
    S1 --> R1[Region A: Far Zone]
    S2 --> R2[Region B: 0 to 1 sigma]
    S3 --> R3[Region C: Far Zone]
    R1 --> X[Probability equals 0.00135 per tail]
    R2 --> Y[Probability equals 0.6826 inside 1 sigma]
    R3 --> X
    S1 -.->|Beyond 3 sigma| A1[Alarm: Rule 1 Violation]
    S3 -.->|Beyond 3 sigma| A1
```

### 4.4 Control Chart Process Map (Operational View)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    M1[Measure Quality Characteristic] --> M2[Record Data in Subgroups]
    M2 --> M3[Plot on Control Chart]
    M3 --> D{Is the process in control?}
    D -->|Yes| M4[Continue Routine Production]
    D -->|No| M5[Stop and Investigate Special Cause]
    M5 --> M6[Identify Root Cause]
    M6 --> M7[Apply Corrective Action]
    M7 --> M8[Verify Improvement with New Data]
    M8 --> M3
    M4 --> M1
```

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer / Definition Type)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam — May 2023, Module 3]`

**Define a Control Chart. State and explain the function of Upper Control Limit and Lower Control Limit.**

**Model Answer:**

A control chart is a statistical tool developed by Walter A. Shewhart in 1924 used to monitor, control, and improve a process over time by distinguishing between common cause and special cause variation. It consists of a time-series plot of a quality characteristic with a Center Line (CL) and two horizontal control limits — UCL and LCL — drawn at ±3 standard deviations from the center line.

- **Upper Control Limit (UCL):** The upper boundary, set at CL + 3σ. Any point above this is a strong statistical signal (probability of 0.00135) that a special cause is present, requiring investigation.
- **Lower Control Limit (LCL):** The lower boundary, set at CL − 3σ. Same function as UCL but on the lower side. If LCL is negative for an attribute chart, it is set to 0.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner Tip:** A common 1-mark lapse is failing to mention the **3-sigma convention** explicitly. Always state "drawn at ±3σ from the center line."

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Module 3]`

**Distinguish between Common Cause Variation and Special Cause Variation. Why is this distinction important in SPC?**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Common Cause Variation | Special Cause Variation |
|--------|------------------------|--------------------------|
| Source | Inherent to the process | External to the process |
| Pattern | Random, predictable | Non-random, unpredictable |
| Magnitude | Small, within ±3σ | Often large, may exceed 3σ |
| Treatment | Requires system change | Requires local corrective action |
| Examples | Machine vibration, raw material purity fluctuation | Tool breakage, operator error, power surge |
| Detection | Statistical control chart | Western Electric run rules |

**Importance of the distinction:** It prevents *tampering*. Deming observed that reacting to common cause variation as if it were special cause *increases* variation and cost. Identifying the correct cause type determines whether to (a) improve the system (common) or (b) remove a localized disturbance (special).

---

### PART B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU Pattern:** Two sub-parts of 7 marks each, with internal choice between Q-A and Q-B. Either Q-A or Q-B must be answered.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**`[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 3]`**

**(a)** Explain the statistical basis of control charts with reference to the Central Limit Theorem and the concept of 3σ limits. Why is 3σ chosen and not 2σ or 4σ? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The following data refer to the weights (in grams) of tablets packed by an automatic machine. Twenty samples of size 5 each are collected. The grand mean of sample means is $\bar{\bar{X}} = 250.2$ g, and the average range is $\bar{R} = 4.6$ g. Assuming subgroup size n = 5, construct the X̄ and R control charts. Determine whether the process is in statistical control. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution — Question A**

#### **Part (a) — Statistical Basis of Control Charts (7 marks)**

The control chart rests on three pillars:

1. **Central Limit Theorem (CLT):** When independent random samples of size n are drawn from a population with mean μ and finite standard deviation σ, the sampling distribution of the sample mean X̄ tends to a normal distribution with:
   - Mean: $E[\bar{X}] = \mu$
   - Standard deviation: $\sigma_{\bar{X}} = \dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$

   This holds even if the underlying distribution is non-normal, provided n ≥ 2. Hence, regardless of whether individual tablet weights are skewed, the means of subgroups of 5 will be approximately Gaussian.

2. **The 3σ Rule:** From the standard normal distribution, the area under the curve between μ − 3σ and μ + 3σ is **0.9973** (i.e., 99.73%). So, when a process is operating correctly, only **0.27% (≈ 1 in 370)** of points will fall outside ±3σ. A point *outside* ±3σ is, therefore, a strong evidence of process disturbance.

3. **Why 3σ and not 2σ or 4σ?**
   - **2σ limits:** Probability of false alarm = 4.55% → too many Type I errors; operators lose faith in the chart.
   - **4σ limits:** Probability of detection is poor (Type II error is too high); real disturbances go unnoticed.
   - **3σ limits:** Optimal economic balance — minimum total cost of Type I + Type II errors. It also corresponds approximately to the inflection point of the normal distribution (z = ±3 is the natural "alarm zone").

4. **Independence of Subgroup Statistics:** X̄ and R (or s) are charted together because X̄ tracks the **process mean (location)** while R tracks **process variability (spread)**. Both must be in control for a true picture of stability.

#### **Part (b) — Constructing X̄ and R Charts (7 marks)**

**Given:**
- n = 5, k = 20 (subgroups)
- $\bar{\bar{X}} = 250.2$ g
- $\bar{R} = 4.6$ g

**Step 1: Control chart constants for n = 5**

From the standard table:
$$A_2 = 0.577, \quad D_3 = 0, \quad D_4 = 2.114$$

**Step 2: X̄ Chart limits**

$$UCL_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} + A_2 \bar{R} = 250.2 + (0.577)(4.6) = 250.2 + 2.6542 = 252.8542 \approx 252.85 \text{ g}$$

$$CL_{\bar{X}} = 250.20 \text{ g}$$

$$LCL_{\bar{X}} = 250.2 - 2.6542 = 247.5458 \approx 247.55 \text{ g}$$

**Step 3: R Chart limits**

$$UCL_R = D_4 \bar{R} = (2.114)(4.6) = 9.7244 \approx 9.72 \text{ g}$$

$$CL_R = 4.60 \text{ g}$$

$$LCL_R = D_3 \bar{R} = 0 \text{ g}$$

**Step 4: Tabulation of Limits**

| Chart | LCL | CL | UCL |
|-------|-----|-----|-----|
| X̄ | 247.55 g | 250.20 g | 252.85 g |
| R | 0 g | 4.60 g | 9.72 g |

**Step 5: In-Control Conclusion (assuming all 20 plotted points lie within limits):**

- Both the X̄ chart and the R chart show no points outside the control limits.
- The process variation is consistent and predictable.
- **Process is in statistical control** under common cause variation only.

**Step 6: Engineering Recommendation:** Since the process is in control, the next step is to *improve the system* (e.g., reduce $\bar{R}$ by tightening machine maintenance, or re-center $\bar{\bar{X}}$ on the target value) rather than treating individual subgroup fluctuations as special events.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**`[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 3]`**

**(a)** Discuss the different types of attribute control charts (p, np, c, u). For each chart, state the statistic plotted, the formula for central line and control limits, and an appropriate industrial application. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** In a textile mill, 25 rolls of fabric of equal length (200 m) are inspected. The number of defects per roll is recorded as follows: 3, 4, 2, 5, 1, 4, 3, 2, 6, 1, 4, 3, 5, 2, 1, 3, 4, 2, 5, 3, 4, 1, 2, 3, 4. Construct a c-chart and comment on the state of control. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution — Question B**

#### **Part (a) — Attribute Control Charts (7 marks)**

| Chart | Statistic Plotted | CL | UCL & LCL | Sample Size | Application |
|-------|-------------------|-----|-----------|-------------|-------------|
| **p-chart** | $p_i = D_i / n_i$ | $\bar{p} = \frac{\sum D_i}{\sum n_i}$ | $\bar{p} \pm 3\sqrt{\dfrac{\bar{p}(1-\bar{p})}{n}}$ | Variable nᵢ | Monitoring % defectives in batches of unequal size |
| **np-chart** | $np_i = D_i$ | $n\bar{p}$ | $n\bar{p} \pm 3\sqrt{n\bar{p}(1-\bar{p})}$ | Constant n | Monitoring # of defectives in fixed sample size |
| **c-chart** | $c_i$ (defect count) | $\bar{c} = \dfrac{\sum c_i}{k}$ | $\bar{c} \pm 3\sqrt{\bar{c}}$ | Constant opportunity | Counting flaws on a fixed-area fabric, IC chip |
| **u-chart** | $u_i = c_i / n_i$ | $\bar{u} = \dfrac{\sum c_i}{\sum n_i}$ | $\bar{u} \pm 3\sqrt{\dfrac{\bar{u}}{n_i}}$ | Variable opportunity | Defects per car, per page, per transaction |

**Key Distinctions:**
- p and np track **defectives** (items with one or more defects) — binary classification.
- c and u track **defects** (total number of flaws) — count data.
- p and u allow variable sample size; np and c require constant n or constant opportunity.
- c-chart assumes Poisson distribution; p-chart assumes Binomial distribution.

#### **Part (b) — c-Chart Construction (7 marks)**

**Step 1: Compute the average number of defects c-bar**

$$\bar{c} = \frac{\sum c_i}{k} = \frac{3+4+2+5+1+4+3+2+6+1+4+3+5+2+1+3+4+2+5+3+4+1+2+3+4}{25} = \frac{76}{25} = 3.04 \text{ defects/roll}$$

**Step 2: Compute standard deviation (Poisson assumption)**

$$\sigma_c = \sqrt{\bar{c}} = \sqrt{3.04} = 1.7436$$

**Step 3: Compute control limits**

$$UCL_c = \bar{c} + 3\sigma_c = 3.04 + 3(1.7436) = 3.04 + 5.2307 = 8.27 \text{ defects}$$

$$CL_c = 3.04 \text{ defects}$$

$$LCL_c = 3.04 - 5.2307 = -2.19 \rightarrow \text{set to } 0 \text{ (negative defects impossible)}$$

**Step 4: Plot inspection — identify out-of-control points**

Maximum value in the dataset: 6 (roll #9). Since 6 < 8.27 (UCL), no point exceeds the upper limit.
Minimum value: 1 — well above LCL = 0.

**Step 5: Conclusion**

- All 25 plotted points lie within the limits [0, 8.27].
- The process is **in statistical control** with respect to fabric defects.
- Recommendation: Continue routine inspection. To *improve* quality (reduce $\bar{c}$), management should focus on the underlying causes of variation in the weaving process (yarn quality, machine calibration, humidity control).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls that COST MARKS:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to set LCL = 0** for c, u, p, or np charts when the calculated LCL is negative → **Lose 1 mark.**
> 2. **Wrong constant for n.** A2 for n=5 is 0.577, not 1.023 (which is for n=3). Mixing up D3, D4, A2 → **Lose 2 marks in computation.**
> 3. **Not stating the 3-sigma basis** in part-(a) theory → lose 1 mark.
> 4. **Confusing Control Limits with Specification Limits.** UCL/LCL come from *process data*; USL/LSL come from *customer specs*. → **Lose 1 mark.**
> 5. **Omitting Western Electric Tests** during interpretation → lose 1 mark.
> 6. **Calling p-chart a "probability chart"** — p is the *proportion* defective, not probability in the general sense.

---

### **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Revision — 60-Second Snapshot of "Control Charts"**

- ✅ **Father of Control Chart:** Walter A. Shewhart (1924), Bell Labs.
- ✅ **Control Limits** are at **±3σ** — not specification limits.
- ✅ **CL = process mean** (grand statistic), not the target.
- ✅ **In-control process** = only common cause variation present.
- ✅ **Out-of-control signal** = point outside UCL/LCL OR Western Electric rule violation.
- ✅ **X̄-R chart:** use for n between 2 and 10. **X̄-s chart:** use for n > 10. **I-MR chart:** use for n = 1.
- ✅ **p-chart:** variable sample size, defectives. **np-chart:** constant sample size, defectives.
- ✅ **c-chart:** constant opportunity, defects. **u-chart:** variable opportunity, defects.
- ✅ **Memorize:** A2 = 0.577, D4 = 2.114 (for n = 5).
- ✅ **If LCL < 0** → set LCL = 0 (truncate).
- ✅ **Western Electric Tests (4 main rules):** point beyond 3σ; 2/3 beyond 2σ same side; 4/5 beyond 1σ same side; 8 on same side of CL.
- ✅ **Deming's 2 voices:** VOC (spec limits) and VOP (control limits) — must be balanced, not confused.
- ✅ **Tampering** = reacting to common cause as if it were special cause — *increases* variation.
- ✅ **Process capability (Cp, Cpk)** is the *next* topic after control charts — it judges "can the process meet specs?" while control charts judge "is the process stable?"
- ✅ **Rational subgroup principle:** select subgroups such that within-subgroup variation is minimized and between-subgroup variation is maximized.
- ✅ **Trial control limits** (from 20–25 preliminary samples) should be revised by removing out-of-control points and re-computing — this is called **Phase 1** and **Phase 2** of control chart implementation.
- ✅ **OCG vs SPC:** Process Control charts use σ̂ from *process data* (R̄/d2, s̄/c4); *NOT* the population standard deviation.
- ✅ **Examination answer tip:** Always draw a *rough sketch* of the chart with UCL, CL, LCL marked and points plotted — earns 1–2 extra marks in ESE even when not explicitly asked.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: X-bar and R Chart Problems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET758 - QUALITY ENGINEERING AND MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** X-bar and R Chart Problems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 X-bar and R Charts — Formal Definition

In Statistical Process Control (SPC), the **X-bar (X̄) Chart** and the **R (Range) Chart** are two complementary control charts used together to monitor the **central tendency** and the **dispersion (variability)** of a manufacturing or service process when data is collected in **rational subgroups** of small size (typically n = 2 to 10).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:**
> The **X̄ chart** monitors the process **mean (location)** by plotting the averages of small samples (subgroups), while the **R chart** monitors the process **variability (spread)** by plotting the ranges (maximum − minimum) of the same subgroups. Both charts operate on the principle that if a process is in statistical control, the plotted points will fall randomly within the 3-sigma control limits.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Temperature Monitoring" Intuition

Imagine you are a chef monitoring a soup kitchen's stockpot. Every 30 minutes, you:
1. **Take 4 small spoonfuls** (a subgroup) from different parts of the pot.
2. **Measure the average temperature** of those 4 spoonfuls (this is your X̄).
3. **Note the difference between the hottest and coldest spoonful** (this is your R).

- The **X̄ chart** answers: *"Is my soup at the correct average temperature today?"* (e.g., consistently near 90 °C).
- The **R chart** answers: *"Is my soup's temperature uniform across the pot?"* (i.e., not half-boiling, half-cold).

If the **R chart** is unstable (wide swings), the **X̄ chart is meaningless** — because averaging wildly inconsistent data is like averaging the temperature of the sun and an iceberg.

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Rule (KTU High-Yield):** The **R chart must be analyzed FIRST** before interpreting the X̄ chart. Variability must be stable before location can be trusted.

## 1.3 Standard KTU Notation

| Symbol | Meaning | Type |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **n** | Subgroup (sample) size | **Integers: 2 to 10** |
| **k** | Number of subgroups | Typically 20–25 |
| **X̄ᵢ** | Mean of the i-th subgroup | Variable |
| **Rᵢ** | Range of the i-th subgroup = Xᵢ(max) − Xᵢ(min) | Variable |
| **X̄̄** (X-double-bar) | Grand mean of all subgroup means | Constant |
| **R̄** (R-bar) | Average of all subgroup ranges | Constant |
| **A₂, D₃, D₄** | Tabulated control chart constants (function of n) | Tabulated |
| **σ̂ (sigma-hat)** | Estimated process standard deviation | Variable |

> [!TIP]
> The values of **A₂, D₃, D₄** are derived from the assumption of normality and are tabulated for n = 2 to 10 in the **AIAG SPC Reference Tables**, which KTU expects students to memorize for n = 2, 3, 4, 5, and 6.

## 1.4 Physical / Engineering Constants

The following constants are NOT physical — they are **statistical** tabulated values based on the normal distribution:

| n | A₂ | D₃ | D₄ | d₂ |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| **2** | 1.880 | 0 | 3.267 | 1.128 |
| **3** | 1.023 | 0 | 2.574 | 1.693 |
| **4** | 0.729 | 0 | 2.282 | 2.059 |
| **5** | 0.577 | 0 | 2.114 | 2.326 |
| **6** | 0.483 | 0 | 2.004 | 2.534 |
| **7** | 0.419 | 0.076 | 1.924 | 2.704 |
| **8** | 0.373 | 0.136 | 1.864 | 2.847 |
| **9** | 0.337 | 0.184 | 1.816 | 2.970 |
| **10** | 0.308 | 0.223 | 1.777 | 3.078 |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — Why X̄ and R?

When the subgroup size **n ≤ 10**, computing the standard deviation within each subgroup is **statistically inefficient** (unstable, high variance estimator). The **range R** is a much more efficient estimator of variability for small samples.

- For **n = 2 to 5**, R is **nearly as efficient** as the sample standard deviation s.
- The relationship: $\hat{\sigma} = \dfrac{\bar{R}}{d_2}$, where **d₂** is a bias-correction factor (tabulated above).

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind the Control Limits

The X̄ chart's standard error (standard deviation of subgroup means) is:

$$ \sigma_{\bar{X}} = \dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}} = \dfrac{\bar{R}}{d_2 \sqrt{n}} $$

Applying the **3-sigma principle** (99.73% confidence for a normal distribution):

$$ UCL_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} + 3\sigma_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} + \dfrac{3}{d_2\sqrt{n}} \cdot \bar{R} $$

The factor $\dfrac{3}{d_2\sqrt{n}}$ is conventionally tabulated as **A₂**, giving:

$$ UCL_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} + A_2 \bar{R} $$

For the R chart, the standard error of the range is proportional to R itself. The 3-sigma limits yield the tabulated constants **D₃ and D₄**:

$$ UCL_R = D_4 \bar{R}, \qquad LCL_R = D_3 \bar{R} $$

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| **Quantity** | **Formula** | **Use / Interpretation** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Subgroup Mean | $\bar{X}_i = \dfrac{\sum_{j=1}^{n} X_{ij}}{n}$ | Average of n observations in subgroup i |
| Subgroup Range | $R_i = X_{i(\max)} - X_{i(\min)}$ | Spread within subgroup i |
| Grand Mean | $\bar{\bar{X}} = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{k} \bar{X}_i}{k}$ | Centerline of X̄ chart |
| Average Range | $\bar{R} = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{k} R_i}{k}$ | Centerline of R chart |
| UCL of X̄ | $UCL_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} + A_2 \bar{R}$ | Upper control limit for process mean |
| LCL of X̄ | $LCL_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} - A_2 \bar{R}$ | Lower control limit for process mean |
| UCL of R | $UCL_R = D_4 \bar{R}$ | Upper control limit for variability |
| LCL of R | $LCL_R = D_3 \bar{R}$ | Lower control limit (often 0 for n ≤ 6) |
| Estimated σ | $\hat{\sigma} = \dfrac{\bar{R}}{d_2}$ | Process standard deviation estimate |
| Process Capability | $C_p = \dfrac{USL - LSL}{6\hat{\sigma}}$ | Capability index (when in control) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Units Note:** All control limits share the same units as the raw measurements (e.g., mm, °C, kg, MPa). A₂, D₃, D₄ are **dimensionless** multipliers.

## 2.4 Engineering Utility — Where This is Used in Industry

X̄ and R charts are the **workhorses** of SPC and are mandated by:
- **IATF 16949** (Automotive Quality Management)
- **AS9100** (Aerospace)
- **ISO 9001** Clause 9.1.1 (Monitoring, measurement, analysis)

Real-world applications:
1. **CNC machining** — monitoring shaft diameters every hour.
2. **Bottling plants** — checking fill volumes in groups of 5.
3. **Pharmaceutical tablet pressing** — tablet weight and hardness.
4. **Foundries** — tensile strength of cast iron samples.
5. **Banking** — monitoring average transaction processing time.

## 2.5 The Logic Flow for Solving KTU Problems

The KTU-expected **step-by-step methodology** is:

1. **Tabulate** the raw subgroup data (n, k).
2. **Compute** X̄ᵢ and Rᵢ for each subgroup.
3. **Compute** X̄̄ (grand mean) and R̄ (average range).
4. **Compute trial control limits** using A₂, D₃, D₄.
5. **Plot** the points and check for **out-of-control signals**:
   - Any point outside the 3-sigma limits.
   - Run of 7 or more points on one side of the centerline.
   - Trend patterns (KTU's "Western Electric Rules" subset).
6. **Remove out-of-control points** and **recompute** revised control limits.
7. **Interpret** the final state of the process (in control vs. out of control).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of A₂, D₃, D₄ (Statistical Foundation)

### Step 1: Distribution of Subgroup Mean

If individual measurements X ~ N(μ, σ²), then for a subgroup of size n:

$$ \bar{X} \sim N\left(\mu, \dfrac{\sigma^2}{n}\right) $$

This means the standard deviation of X̄ is $\sigma / \sqrt{n}$.

### Step 2: Estimating σ from R̄

For a subgroup of size n drawn from a normal distribution, the expected value of the range R is:

$$ E(R) = d_2 \cdot \sigma $$

where **d₂** is a constant (tabulated earlier). Therefore, the unbiased estimator of σ is:

$$ \hat{\sigma} = \dfrac{\bar{R}}{d_2} $$

### Step 3: Standard Error of X̄ in Terms of R̄

Substituting the estimate of σ:

$$ \sigma_{\bar{X}} = \dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}} = \dfrac{\bar{R}}{d_2 \sqrt{n}} $$

### Step 4: 3-Sigma Control Limits for X̄

By definition of 3-sigma limits:

$$ UCL_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} + 3 \cdot \sigma_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} + \dfrac{3\bar{R}}{d_2\sqrt{n}} $$

Defining:

$$ A_2 \equiv \dfrac{3}{d_2 \sqrt{n}} $$

we recover the practical formula:

$$ UCL_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} + A_2 \bar{R} $$

### Step 5: Distribution of R

The range R follows a distribution whose mean and standard deviation are:

$$ \mu_R = d_2 \sigma, \qquad \sigma_R = d_3 \sigma $$

where d₃ is another tabulated constant.

### Step 6: 3-Sigma Control Limits for R

The 3-sigma limits for the range are:

$$ UCL_R = \bar{R} + 3 \sigma_R = \bar{R} + 3 d_3 \sigma $$

Substituting $\sigma = \bar{R} / d_2$:

$$ UCL_R = \bar{R} + \dfrac{3 d_3 \bar{R}}{d_2} = \bar{R}\left(1 + \dfrac{3 d_3}{d_2}\right) $$

Defining:

$$ D_4 \equiv 1 + \dfrac{3 d_3}{d_2}, \qquad D_3 \equiv 1 - \dfrac{3 d_3}{d_2} $$

we obtain:

$$ UCL_R = D_4 \bar{R}, \qquad LCL_R = D_3 \bar{R} $$

> [!NOTE]
> For n ≤ 6, the value of D₃ becomes negative. By convention (and AIAG tables), D₃ is set to **0** (since range cannot be negative). This is a **KTU-favorite** exam point.

## 3.2 Fully Worked Example — KTU 14-Mark Standard Problem

> **Problem Statement (KTU 2024 Pattern):**
> A quality inspector takes 5 samples (subgroups) of size 4 from a manufacturing process. The data (in mm) for the diameter of a shaft is given below. Construct the X̄ and R charts, identify any out-of-control points, and compute revised control limits after removing them.

| Subgroup | Obs 1 | Obs 2 | Obs 3 | Obs 4 |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 1 | 10.0 | 10.2 | 9.8 | 10.0 |
| 2 | 10.1 | 9.9 | 10.0 | 10.2 |
| 3 | 10.3 | 10.1 | 9.9 | 10.0 |
| 4 | 9.8 | 9.6 | 9.9 | 9.7 |
| 5 | 10.0 | 10.1 | 10.2 | 10.0 |

### Solution Step 1: Compute Subgroup Means (X̄ᵢ) and Ranges (Rᵢ)

For **Subgroup 1**: values = 10.0, 10.2, 9.8, 10.0

$$ \bar{X}_1 = \dfrac{10.0 + 10.2 + 9.8 + 10.0}{4} = \dfrac{40.0}{4} = 10.00 $$

$$ R_1 = X_{\max} - X_{\min} = 10.2 - 9.8 = 0.40 $$

For **Subgroup 2**: values = 10.1, 9.9, 10.0, 10.2

$$ \bar{X}_2 = \dfrac{10.1 + 9.9 + 10.0 + 10.2}{4} = \dfrac{40.2}{4} = 10.05 $$

$$ R_2 = 10.2 - 9.9 = 0.30 $$

For **Subgroup 3**: values = 10.3, 10.1, 9.9, 10.0

$$ \bar{X}_3 = \dfrac{10.3 + 10.1 + 9.9 + 10.0}{4} = \dfrac{40.3}{4} = 10.075 $$

$$ R_3 = 10.3 - 9.9 = 0.40 $$

For **Subgroup 4**: values = 9.8, 9.6, 9.9, 9.7

$$ \bar{X}_4 = \dfrac{9.8 + 9.6 + 9.9 + 9.7}{4} = \dfrac{39.0}{4} = 9.75 $$

$$ R_4 = 9.9 - 9.6 = 0.30 $$

For **Subgroup 5**: values = 10.0, 10.1, 10.2, 10.0

$$ \bar{X}_5 = \dfrac{10.0 + 10.1 + 10.2 + 10.0}{4} = \dfrac{40.3}{4} = 10.075 $$

$$ R_5 = 10.2 - 10.0 = 0.20 $$

### Solution Step 2: Tabulate Results

| Subgroup (i) | X̄ᵢ | Rᵢ |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 1 | 10.000 | 0.40 |
| 2 | 10.050 | 0.30 |
| 3 | 10.075 | 0.40 |
| 4 | **9.750** | 0.30 |
| 5 | 10.075 | 0.20 |
| **Sum** | **49.950** | **1.600** |

### Solution Step 3: Compute X̄̄ and R̄

$$ \bar{\bar{X}} = \dfrac{\sum \bar{X}_i}{k} = \dfrac{49.950}{5} = 9.990 \text{ mm} $$

$$ \bar{R} = \dfrac{\sum R_i}{k} = \dfrac{1.600}{5} = 0.320 \text{ mm} $$

### Solution Step 4: Look Up Constants for n = 4

From the table (Section 1.4):
- **A₂ = 0.729**
- **D₃ = 0**
- **D₄ = 2.282**

### Solution Step 5: Compute Trial Control Limits for X̄ Chart

$$ UCL_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} + A_2 \bar{R} = 9.990 + (0.729)(0.320) $$

$$ UCL_{\bar{X}} = 9.990 + 0.23328 = 10.223 \text{ mm} $$

$$ LCL_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} - A_2 \bar{R} = 9.990 - 0.23328 = 9.757 \text{ mm} $$

$$ CL_{\bar{X}} = 9.990 \text{ mm} $$

### Solution Step 6: Compute Trial Control Limits for R Chart

$$ UCL_R = D_4 \bar{R} = (2.282)(0.320) = 0.730 \text{ mm} $$

$$ LCL_R = D_3 \bar{R} = (0)(0.320) = 0 \text{ mm} $$

$$ CL_R = 0.320 \text{ mm} $$

### Solution Step 7: Check for Out-of-Control Points

- **X̄ Chart:** Subgroup 4 has X̄ = 9.75, which is **below LCL = 9.757**. ⚠️ **OUT OF CONTROL.**
- **R Chart:** All R values (0.40, 0.30, 0.40, 0.30, 0.20) are within [0, 0.730]. ✅ **In control.**

### Solution Step 8: Remove Subgroup 4 and Recompute

Without Subgroup 4:

$$ \sum \bar{X}_i = 49.950 - 9.750 = 40.200 $$

$$ \bar{\bar{X}}_{new} = \dfrac{40.200}{4} = 10.050 \text{ mm} $$

$$ \sum R_i = 1.600 - 0.30 = 1.300 $$

$$ \bar{R}_{new} = \dfrac{1.300}{4} = 0.325 \text{ mm} $$

### Solution Step 9: Revised Control Limits

$$ UCL_{\bar{X}} = 10.050 + (0.729)(0.325) = 10.050 + 0.23693 = 10.287 \text{ mm} $$

$$ LCL_{\bar{X}} = 10.050 - 0.23693 = 9.813 \text{ mm} $$

$$ UCL_R = (2.282)(0.325) = 0.742 \text{ mm} $$

$$ LCL_R = 0 \text{ mm} $$

### Solution Step 10: Interpretation

After removing Subgroup 4, the process is **in statistical control** with the **centerline at 10.050 mm** and **3-sigma limits of (9.813, 10.287) mm**. The estimated process standard deviation is:

$$ \hat{\sigma} = \dfrac{\bar{R}_{new}}{d_2} = \dfrac{0.325}{2.059} = 0.158 \text{ mm} $$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — How KTU Tests "X-bar and R Chart Problems"

| **KTU Cognitive Level** | **Question Type** | **Expected Action by Student** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Remember (L1) | List A₂, D₃, D₄ for given n | Direct table lookup |
| Understand (L2) | Explain why R chart is plotted first | Variability precedes location |
| Apply (L3) | Compute trial control limits | Subgroup stat → grand stat → limits |
| Analyze (L4) | Identify out-of-control points | Plot vs. UCL/LCL; flag violations |
| Evaluate (L5) | Revise limits; recommend action | Remove assignable cause; recompute |
| Create (L6) | Recommend OCAP for the process | Out-of-control action plan writing |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — X̄ and R Chart Computation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    rawData["Raw Subgroup Data Matrix (k x n)"] --> calcSubStats["Step 1: Compute Xbar_i and R_i per Subgroup"]
    calcSubStats --> grandStats["Step 2: Compute Xdoublebar and Rbar"]
    grandStats --> lookupConstants["Step 3: Look up A2, D3, D4 from Tables (based on n)"]
    lookupConstants --> trialLimits["Step 4: Compute TRIAL Control Limits"]
    trialLimits --> plotCharts["Step 5: Plot Xbar Chart AND R Chart"]
    plotCharts --> oocCheck{"Step 6: Out-of-Control Points?"}
    oocCheck -->|Yes, in R chart| stopProcess["STOP: Process Variability Unstable. Investigate Assignable Causes."]
    oocCheck -->|No, in Xbar chart only| flagXbar["Flag Xbar Violation: Investigate Process Mean Shift"]
    oocCheck -->|No, all in control| acceptLimits["ACCEPT Trial Limits as Final"]
    stopProcess --> removeOOC["Remove Out-of-Control Subgroups"]
    flagXbar --> removeOOC
    removeOOC --> revisedStats["Step 7: Recompute Xdoublebar_new and Rbar_new"]
    revisedStats --> revisedLimits["Step 8: Compute REVISED Control Limits"]
    revisedLimits --> monitor["Step 9: Use for Ongoing Process Monitoring"]
    acceptLimits --> monitor
    monitor --> output["Final Output: Process Capability, Cp, Cpk"]
```

## 4.2 Schematic — The 3-Sigma Control Chart Anatomy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph sXbar["XBAR CHART (Process Mean)"]
        uclX["UCL = Xdoublebar + A2 x Rbar"]
        clX["CL = Xdoublebar (Grand Mean)"]
        lclX["LCL = Xdoublebar - A2 x Rbar"]
    end
    subgraph sR["R CHART (Process Variability)"]
        uclR["UCL = D4 x Rbar"]
        clR["CL = Rbar (Average Range)"]
        lclR["LCL = D3 x Rbar (usually 0)"]
    end
    sXbar --> priority["PRIORITY ORDER"]
    priority --> firstR["1. Interpret R Chart FIRST"]
    firstR --> thenX["2. THEN Interpret Xbar Chart"]
    sR --> firstR
```

## 4.3 Conceptual Logic Flow — Subgroup Formation Strategy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    process["Manufacturing Process Flow"] --> decision{"What are we monitoring?"}
    decision -->|Within-piece variation| subgroup1["Subgroup = Items from SAME piece at different locations"]
    decision -->|Piece-to-piece variation| subgroup2["Subgroup = Consecutive pieces over TIME"]
    decision -->|Batch-to-batch variation| subgroup3["Subgroup = Items from SAME batch"]
    subgroup1 --> note1["Minimize WITHIN-group variation; maximize BETWEEN-group variation"]
    subgroup2 --> note1
    subgroup3 --> note1
    note1 --> result["Rational Subgroups Maximize Chance of Detecting Shifts in Xbar and R"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define X̄ chart and R chart. Why is the R chart interpreted before the X̄ chart?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **X̄ chart** is a variables control chart that plots the averages of small subgroups (sample size n) to monitor the **process mean (central tendency)**. (1 mark)
- **R chart** is a variables control chart that plots the within-subgroup ranges to monitor the **process variability (dispersion)**. (1 mark)
- The R chart is interpreted first because **if process variability is unstable, the X̄ chart's control limits (which depend on R̄) are unreliable**, and subgroup means become uninterpretable. Variability must be stable before the location (mean) can be trusted. (1 mark)

---

### **Question 2: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*For a control chart with subgroup size n = 5, the following tabulated constants apply: A₂ = 0.577, D₃ = 0, D₄ = 2.114. The grand mean is 50.0 mm and the average range is 2.0 mm. Calculate the control limits for the X̄ and R charts.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

For the **X̄ chart**:
- UCL = X̄̄ + A₂ R̄ = 50.0 + (0.577)(2.0) = 50.0 + 1.154 = **51.154 mm**
- LCL = X̄̄ − A₂ R̄ = 50.0 − 1.154 = **48.846 mm**

For the **R chart**:
- UCL = D₄ R̄ = (2.114)(2.0) = **4.228 mm**
- LCL = D₃ R̄ = 0 × 2.0 = **0 mm**

(1 mark each pair of limits, with explicit A₂/D₄ substitution)

---

## 5.2 Part B Question Choice (14 Marks)

### **Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** A CNC machine produces bearings with target diameter 25.00 mm. Twenty-five subgroups of size 5 are sampled, and the following summary statistics are obtained:
- Grand mean, X̄̄ = 25.05 mm
- Average range, R̄ = 0.40 mm

Using the constants for n = 5 (A₂ = 0.577, D₃ = 0, D₄ = 2.114), construct the X̄ and R charts by computing all control limits. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** During the monitoring period, the following 6 consecutive subgroup means were observed: 25.10, 25.12, 25.15, 25.18, 25.20, 25.22 mm. With reference to the X̄ control limits calculated in part (a), identify the type of out-of-control pattern and recommend an Out-of-Control Action Plan (OCAP). **[7 marks]**

---

### **Model Solution to Question A:**

#### Part (a) Solution [7 marks]:

**Step 1: Identify constants for n = 5**
- A₂ = 0.577, D₃ = 0, D₄ = 2.114

**Step 2: X̄ Chart Control Limits**
- UCL_X̄ = X̄̄ + A₂ R̄ = 25.05 + (0.577)(0.40) = 25.05 + 0.2308 = **25.281 mm**
- CL_X̄ = X̄̄ = **25.050 mm**
- LCL_X̄ = 25.05 − 0.2308 = **24.819 mm**

**Step 3: R Chart Control Limits**
- UCL_R = D₄ R̄ = (2.114)(0.40) = **0.846 mm**
- CL_R = R̄ = **0.400 mm**
- LCL_R = D₃ R̄ = 0 × 0.40 = **0 mm**

(7 marks — distributed as 3 marks for X̄ limits, 3 marks for R limits, 1 mark for correct constant lookup)

#### Part (b) Solution [7 marks]:

**Step 1: Compute the X̄ control limits (from part a)**
- UCL = 25.281 mm, LCL = 24.819 mm, CL = 25.050 mm

**Step 2: Pattern Recognition**
- All 6 points (25.10, 25.12, 25.15, 25.18, 25.20, 25.22) are **above the centerline (25.050)** and are **monotonically increasing**. (2 marks)

**Step 3: Identify the Out-of-Control Pattern**
- This is a **TREND pattern** (specifically an **upward trend**). A run of 6 or more points showing a monotonic increase signals non-random behavior, indicating an **assignable cause** such as **tool wear** (a classic cause in CNC machining). (2 marks)

**Step 4: OCAP Recommendation** (3 marks):
1. **Stop the machine** and isolate the bearings produced during this period for 100% inspection.
2. **Investigate the cutting tool** — measure for wear; replace if beyond tolerance.
3. **Check coolant flow** and spindle alignment.
4. **Recompute control limits** after the tool is replaced, using only in-control data.
5. **Implement preventive tool-change schedule** based on the trend detection.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Alternate]**

**(a)** The following data represents the weight (in grams) of 5 bags of rice drawn from a filling machine, taken across 6 subgroups of size 4. Compute the subgroup means (X̄ᵢ) and ranges (Rᵢ), and then compute the grand mean (X̄̄) and average range (R̄). **[7 marks]**

| Subgroup | Wt 1 | Wt 2 | Wt 3 | Wt 4 |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 1 | 1005 | 1010 | 998 | 1002 |
| 2 | 1003 | 1005 | 1000 | 1008 |
| 3 | 1010 | 1012 | 1008 | 1015 |
| 4 | 1004 | 1006 | 1002 | 1000 |
| 5 | 1006 | 1009 | 1004 | 1007 |
| 6 | 1002 | 1004 | 1000 | 1006 |

**(b)** Using n = 4 constants (A₂ = 0.729, D₃ = 0, D₄ = 2.282), construct the X̄ and R charts, identify out-of-control subgroups, and recompute the revised control limits after removing any out-of-control subgroups. **[7 marks]**

---

### **Model Solution to Question B:**

#### Part (a) Solution [7 marks]:

**Subgroup 1:** Sum = 1005 + 1010 + 998 + 1002 = 4015
- X̄₁ = 4015/4 = **1003.75 g**, R₁ = 1010 − 998 = **12 g**

**Subgroup 2:** Sum = 1003 + 1005 + 1000 + 1008 = 4016
- X̄₂ = 4016/4 = **1004.00 g**, R₂ = 1008 − 1000 = **8 g**

**Subgroup 3:** Sum = 1010 + 1012 + 1008 + 1015 = 4045
- X̄₃ = 4045/4 = **1011.25 g**, R₃ = 1015 − 1008 = **7 g**

**Subgroup 4:** Sum = 1004 + 1006 + 1002 + 1000 = 4012
- X̄₄ = 4012/4 = **1003.00 g**, R₄ = 1006 − 1000 = **6 g**

**Subgroup 5:** Sum = 1006 + 1009 + 1004 + 1007 = 4026
- X̄₅ = 4026/4 = **1006.50 g**, R₅ = 1009 − 1004 = **5 g**

**Subgroup 6:** Sum = 1002 + 1004 + 1000 + 1006 = 4012
- X̄₆ = 4012/4 = **1003.00 g**, R₆ = 1006 − 1000 = **6 g**

**Grand Mean and Average Range:**

$$ \bar{\bar{X}} = \dfrac{1003.75 + 1004.00 + 1011.25 + 1003.00 + 1006.50 + 1003.00}{6} = \dfrac{6031.50}{6} = 1005.25 \text{ g} $$

$$ \bar{R} = \dfrac{12 + 8 + 7 + 6 + 5 + 6}{6} = \dfrac{44}{6} = 7.333 \text{ g} $$

(7 marks — 0.5 per X̄ᵢ, 0.5 per Rᵢ, 1 mark for X̄̄, 0.5 mark for R̄)

#### Part (b) Solution [7 marks]:

**Step 1: Trial Control Limits for X̄ Chart (n = 4, A₂ = 0.729)**
- UCL_X̄ = 1005.25 + (0.729)(7.333) = 1005.25 + 5.346 = **1010.596 g**
- CL_X̄ = **1005.25 g**
- LCL_X̄ = 1005.25 − 5.346 = **999.904 g**

**Step 2: Trial Control Limits for R Chart (D₃ = 0, D₄ = 2.282)**
- UCL_R = (2.282)(7.333) = **16.734 g**
- CL_R = **7.333 g**
- LCL_R = **0 g**

**Step 3: Check for Out-of-Control Points**
- **R Chart:** All R values (12, 8, 7, 6, 5, 6) are within [0, 16.734] → **In control** ✅
- **X̄ Chart:** X̄₃ = 1011.25 > UCL = 1010.596 → **OUT OF CONTROL** ⚠️

**Step 4: Remove Subgroup 3 and Recompute**

$$ \bar{\bar{X}}_{new} = \dfrac{6031.50 - 1011.25}{5} = \dfrac{5020.25}{5} = 1004.05 \text{ g} $$

$$ \bar{R}_{new} = \dfrac{44 - 7}{5} = \dfrac{37}{5} = 7.40 \text{ g} $$

**Step 5: Revised Control Limits**

X̄ Chart:
- UCL = 1004.05 + (0.729)(7.40) = 1004.05 + 5.395 = **1009.445 g**
- LCL = 1004.05 − 5.395 = **998.655 g**
- CL = **1004.05 g**

R Chart:
- UCL = (2.282)(7.40) = **16.887 g**
- LCL = **0 g**
- CL = **7.40 g**

**Step 6: Interpretation**
After removing the out-of-control Subgroup 3, the filling process is **in statistical control** with a **centerline of 1004.05 g** and estimated process standard deviation:

$$ \hat{\sigma} = \dfrac{7.40}{d_2} = \dfrac{7.40}{2.059} = 3.594 \text{ g} $$

(7 marks — 2 marks for trial limits, 1 mark for OOC detection, 2 marks for revised limits, 2 marks for interpretation)

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in X̄ and R Chart Problems:**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting to look up the right n:** Many students use A₂ = 0.729 (n=4) when the problem states n=5. Always verify the subgroup size **before** picking constants. (-1 mark)
> 
> 2. **Setting LCL_R negative:** For n = 2 to 6, the calculated D₃ R̄ may be negative, but range cannot be negative. KTU expects LCL_R = 0 in such cases. (-0.5 mark)
> 
> 3. **Skipping the X̄̄ and R̄ calculations:** Jumping directly to the formula without showing the intermediate computation of grand mean and average range is treated as incomplete. (-2 marks)
> 
> 4. **Not removing out-of-control subgroups before revision:** If asked for "revised limits," you MUST show that the out-of-control subgroups were excluded and the new X̄̄ and R̄ were recomputed. (-2 marks)
> 
> 5. **R chart interpretation after X̄ chart:** The R chart MUST be interpreted first. Drawing conclusions on the X̄ chart before checking the R chart's stability loses full method marks. (-1 mark)
> 
> 6. **Ignoring run/trend rules:** A point may be **within** the 3-sigma limits but still be **out of control** (e.g., 7 points in a row on one side, or a trend). KTU expects awareness of these "Western Electric Rules." (-1 mark)

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — X̄ and R Chart Problems**

- **Definition:** X̄ chart monitors **process mean**; R chart monitors **process variability** (range).
- **Pair them together:** They are always plotted as a pair, never independently.
- **Subgroup size:** n = 2 to 10 (most commonly n = 4 or 5).
- **Order of interpretation:** **R chart FIRST** → then X̄ chart.
- **Trial limits formulas:**
  - UCL_X̄ = X̄̄ + A₂ R̄
  - LCL_X̄ = X̄̄ − A₂ R̄
  - UCL_R = D₄ R̄
  - LCL_R = D₃ R̄
- **Constant selection:** A₂, D₃, D₄ depend **only on n**; memorize for n = 2, 3, 4, 5, 6.
- **D₃ = 0 for n ≤ 6** (because range cannot be negative).
- **Process σ estimate:** $\hat{\sigma} = \bar{R} / d_2$ (use this for capability indices).
- **Out-of-control signals:** Points outside 3σ, run of ≥7, monotonic trends, cycles, stratification.
- **Revised limits:** Always recompute X̄̄ and R̄ **after** removing flagged subgroups.
- **KTU focus areas:** Constant lookup, formula application, OOC detection, revised limits, and OCAP recommendations.
- **Units discipline:** Control limits and subgroup statistics share the **same units** as raw data; A₂, D₃, D₄ are **dimensionless**.
- **Western Electric Rules (subset KTU uses):** 1 point beyond 3σ, 2 of 3 beyond 2σ, 4 of 5 beyond 1σ, 8 in a row on one side.
- **Industrial mandate:** X̄ and R charts are foundational in IATF 16949 / ISO 9001 SPC programs.
- **Estimation basis:** All formulas assume the process data is **approximately normally distributed**; verify via histogram or normality test for full credit.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Process Capability
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET758 - QUALITY ENGINEERING AND MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Process Capability

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT] **Process Capability Definition**
> **Process Capability** is a statistical measure of the inherent variability of a process in its **stable state** (under statistical control), expressed as the ability of the process to produce output that conforms to the **engineering specification limits** (USL and LSL). It is quantified using dimensionless indices such as **Cp, Cpk, Pp, and Ppk**, which compare the **Voice of the Process (VOP)** — the natural spread — to the **Voice of the Customer (VOC)** — the design tolerance band.

In KTU parlance, Process Capability answers the single most important question in any quality function deployment:

> *"Is my process fundamentally *able* to meet the customer's specification window?"*

The primary capability indices evaluated under KTU Module 3 are:

- **Cp** — Process Capability Index (spread-based, assumes centering).
- **Cpk** — Process Capability Index (centred, penalises shift in mean).
- **Pp / Ppk** — Process Performance Indices (long-term, using overall σ).
- **Cpm** — Taguchi capability index (penalises deviation from target T).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Highway Lane" Model

> [!NOTE] **Plain-English Intuition**
> Imagine driving a car down a single-lane highway. The **specification limits (USL / LSL)** are the white-painted road edges. The **process mean (μ)** is the position your steering tends to hold the car. The **process standard deviation (σ)** is how much your hands wobble — i.e., the jitter.
>
> - **A high Cp** = a wide road with little wobble. You will never run off the edge. *Capable.*
> - **A low Cp** = a narrow road with heavy wobble. You are constantly swerving off the edge. *Not capable.*
> - **A high Cp but low Cpk** = wide road, but you are hugging the left edge because your steering is biased. You *could* be capable, but you are *not currently centred*.
> - **A high Cpk** = the process is both **predictable (low σ)** AND **centred on target**.

This analogy captures the essential dual requirement of any modern quality system (ISO 9001:2015, IATF 16949, AS9100): a process must be **statistically stable** AND **statistically capable**.

---

## 1.3 Core Statistical Vocabulary (KTU Board Favourites)

| Symbol | Meaning | Engineering Interpretation |
|:------:|:-------:|:--------------------------:|
| **USL** | Upper Specification Limit | Maximum permissible value |
| **LSL** | Lower Specification Limit | Minimum permissible value |
| **T** | Target Value | The "bull's-eye" nominal dimension |
| **μ** | Process Mean | Long-run average of the quality characteristic |
| **σ** | Process Standard Deviation | Inherent process variability |
| **σ_within** | Within-subgroup std dev | Short-term capability (R-bar / d2) |
| **σ_overall** | Overall std dev | Long-term performance |
| **Pp, Ppk** | Performance Indices | Long-term metrics |
| **Cp, Cpk** | Capability Indices | Short-term potential metrics |
| **DPMO** | Defects Per Million Opportunities | Six-Sigma metric |

> [!IMPORTANT] **The 6σ "Voice of the Process"**
> For any **normally distributed** stable process, the natural tolerance band (in which **99.73 %** of all output falls) is:
> **μ ± 3σ** ⇒ Natural Process Spread = **6σ**
> This is the famous "three-sigma limits" you have encountered in statistical process control (SPC) charts.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Voices — VOP vs VOC

Process Capability analysis is fundamentally a *comparison* between two signals:

| Voice | Symbol | Meaning | Source |
|:-----:|:------:|:--------|:-------|
| **Voice of the Customer (VOC)** | USL − LSL | What the customer demands (tolerance) | Engineering Design |
| **Voice of the Process (VOP)** | 6σ | What the process naturally delivers | Statistical data |

A process is **capable** if and only if VOC ≥ VOP.

---

## 2.2 Detailed Logic Steps of Capability Computation

### Step 1 — Establish Statistical Control
Before computing any capability index, the process **must** be in statistical control on an X-bar / R or X-bar / S control chart. Otherwise the σ estimate is contaminated by *assignable causes* (tool wear, material lot variation, operator drift).

### Step 2 — Estimate σ
Two distinct estimators are used:

$$\hat{\sigma}_{\text{within}} \;=\; \frac{\bar{R}}{d_2} \quad \text{(short-term, from control chart R-bar)}$$

$$\hat{\sigma}_{\text{overall}} \;=\; s \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{\sum (x_i-\bar{x})^2}{n-1}} \quad \text{(long-term)}$$

### Step 3 — Compute the Spread-Based Index Cp

$$C_p \;=\; \frac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sigma}$$

Cp answers: *"How many times does my process spread fit inside the tolerance window?"*

### Step 4 — Compute the Centred Index Cpk

$$C_{pk} \;=\; \min\!\left( C_{pu},\, C_{pl} \right)$$

where

$$C_{pu} \;=\; \frac{\text{USL} - \mu}{3\sigma} \qquad C_{pl} \;=\; \frac{\mu - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma}$$

### Step 5 — Compute the Performance Indices (Long-Term)

$$P_p \;=\; \frac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sigma_{\text{overall}}}$$

$$P_{pk} \;=\; \min\!\left( \frac{\text{USL} - \mu}{3\sigma_{\text{overall}}},\, \frac{\mu - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma_{\text{overall}}} \right)$$

### Step 6 — Compute the Taguchi Index Cpm

$$C_{pm} \;=\; \frac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sqrt{\sigma^2 + (\mu - T)^2}}$$

Cpm penalises **any** deviation of the mean from the target value T (the "bull's-eye" concept from Taguchi's loss function philosophy).

---

## 2.3 The Six-Sigma Philosophy (KTU Frequently Tested)

> [!IMPORTANT] **Motorola Six-Sigma Definition**
> A process operates at **Six-Sigma quality** when the distance from the process mean to the nearest specification limit is **6 standard deviations**, i.e., the process spread (12σ across both sides) fits inside the tolerance window with a margin of ±6σ on each side.
> Defect level at 6σ: **3.4 DPMO** (allowing 1.5σ long-term drift).

The number of sigma levels (Z) from the mean to the nearest spec is called the **Z-score** or **process Z-value**:

$$Z_{\text{ST}} \;=\; \frac{\text{USL} - \mu}{3\sigma_{\text{ST}}} \qquad \text{(short-term, no 1.5σ shift)}$$

$$Z_{\text{LT}} \;=\; Z_{\text{ST}} + 1.5 \qquad \text{(long-term, after Motorola 1.5σ drift allowance)}$$

| Sigma Level | Z_LT | DPMO | Yield % |
|:-----------:|:----:|:----:|:-------:|
| 3σ | 4.5 | 66,807 | 93.32 % |
| 4σ | 5.5 | 233 | 99.977 % |
| 5σ | 6.5 | 3.4 | 99.9997 % |
| **6σ** | **7.5** | **0.002** | **99.9999998 %** |

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Exam-Ready)

> [!NOTE] **Exhaustive Capability Formula Reference — Memorise This Table**

| # | Index / Parameter | Formula | Meaning | When Used |
|:-:|:------------------|:--------|:--------|:----------|
| 1 | **Cp** | $\dfrac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sigma}$ | Spread capability | Centred process |
| 2 | **Cpu / Cpk_upper** | $\dfrac{\text{USL} - \mu}{3\sigma}$ | One-sided (upper) | Unilateral tolerance |
| 3 | **Cpl / Cpk_lower** | $\dfrac{\mu - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma}$ | One-sided (lower) | Unilateral tolerance |
| 4 | **Cpk** | $\min(C_{pu},\, C_{pl})$ | Centred capability | Real-world offset processes |
| 5 | **Pp** | $\dfrac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sigma_{\text{overall}}}$ | Long-term spread | Initial capability study |
| 6 | **Ppk** | $\min\!\left( \dfrac{\text{USL}-\mu}{3\sigma_{\text{ov}}}, \dfrac{\mu - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma_{\text{ov}}} \right)$ | Long-term performance | Production monitoring |
| 7 | **Cpm** | $\dfrac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sqrt{\sigma^2 + (\mu - T)^2}}$ | Taguchi target index | Loss-function philosophy |
| 8 | **Cpk = Cp × (1 − k)** | $C_{pk} = C_p (1 - k)$ ; $k = \dfrac{2\mu - (\text{USL}+\text{LSL})}{\text{USL}-\text{LSL}}$ | Centring factor | Quick check |
| 9 | **DPMO** | $\dfrac{\text{Defects} \times 10^6}{\text{Units} \times \text{Opportunities/unit}}$ | Defect density | Six-Sigma project |
| 10 | **Z_ST** | $\dfrac{\text{USL} - \mu}{3\sigma}$ (or to LSL) | Short-term sigma | Short-term study |
| 11 | **Z_LT** | $Z_{\text{ST}} + 1.5$ | Long-term sigma | Six-Sigma reporting |
| 12 | **Process Yield** | $\Phi(Z_{\text{LT}}) \times 100$ | % within spec | Reporting |

**Interpretation Benchmarks (Industry-Standard, KTU-Approved):**

| Cpk (or Ppk) | Process Capability Verdict | Action Required |
|:------------:|:---------------------------|:----------------|
| < 1.00 | **Not Capable** | Stop production; fundamental redesign |
| 1.00 – 1.33 | **Marginally Capable** | Tighten control; 100 % inspection |
| 1.33 – 1.67 | **Capable** | Continuous monitoring |
| **≥ 1.67** | **Highly Capable** (Six-Sigma territory) | Maintain & reduce variation |

> [!IMPORTANT] **Key Engineering Utility**
> Process Capability indices are the **lingua franca** of automotive (IATF 16949 → PPAP), aerospace (AS9100 → First Article Inspection), semiconductor, pharmaceutical, and medical-device industries. **Cpk ≥ 1.33 is the minimum** for critical-to-quality (CTQ) characteristics on most KTU industrial case studies.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Cp — Spread-Based Index

**Starting Concept.** The natural process spread for a **stable, normally distributed** process covers **99.73 %** of output and equals 6σ (three sigmas above plus three sigmas below the mean). The engineering tolerance window equals (USL − LSL).

**Reasoning:** If we want to know how many "process widths" fit into the "engineering width", we form the ratio:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{How many } 6\sigma \text{ widths fit into (USL - LSL)} &\;\Rightarrow\; C_p = \frac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sigma}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Unit Check:** Both numerator and denominator have the same engineering units (mm, °C, ohm, etc.), hence Cp is **dimensionless** — by design, it is a *pure number*.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Cpk — Centred Index

**Starting Concept.** Cp is optimistic — it assumes μ sits exactly mid-tolerance. Real processes drift. The Cpk index corrects this by measuring the **worst-case one-sided distance** from μ to the nearest specification limit.

**One-sided upper distance (in σ units):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{pu} &= \frac{\text{USL} - \mu}{3\sigma}
\end{aligned}
$$

**One-sided lower distance (in σ units):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{pl} &= \frac{\mu - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Definition of Cpk (the conservative "worst-side" index):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{pk} &= \min(C_{pu},\, C_{pl}) \\[4pt]
       &= \min\!\left( \frac{\text{USL} - \mu}{3\sigma},\; \frac{\mu - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma} \right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Relation to Cp through the centring factor k:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
k &= \frac{2\mu - (\text{USL} + \text{LSL})}{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}} \\[6pt]
C_{pk} &= C_p \cdot (1 - \vert k \vert) \;=\; (1 - k)\,C_p \quad \text{(if μ between LSL and USL)}
\end{aligned}
$$

When **k = 0** (perfect centring): **Cpk = Cp**. When **|k| = 1** (mean touches a spec): **Cpk = 0**.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example #1 — Classical Cp / Cpk Calculation

> [!NOTE] **Problem Statement**
> A CNC lathe produces shafts of nominal diameter **25.00 mm**.
> Specifications: **LSL = 24.90 mm**, **USL = 25.10 mm**.
> A capability study of 100 shafts yielded:
> **Sample mean x̄ = 25.04 mm**, **Standard deviation s = 0.020 mm**.
> Compute Cp, Cpk and interpret.

**Step 1 — Compute Cp (spread-based):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_p &= \frac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sigma} \\[6pt]
    &= \frac{25.10 - 24.90}{6 \times 0.020} \\[6pt]
    &= \frac{0.20}{0.120} \\[6pt]
    &= 1.667
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Cpu (one-sided upper):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{pu} &= \frac{\text{USL} - \mu}{3\sigma} \\[6pt]
       &= \frac{25.10 - 25.04}{3 \times 0.020} \\[6pt]
       &= \frac{0.06}{0.060} \\[6pt]
       &= 1.000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Cpl (one-sided lower):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{pl} &= \frac{\mu - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma} \\[6pt]
       &= \frac{25.04 - 24.90}{0.060} \\[6pt]
       &= \frac{0.14}{0.060} \\[6pt]
       &= 2.333
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Cpk:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{pk} &= \min(C_{pu},\, C_{pl}) \\[6pt]
       &= \min(1.000,\, 2.333) \\[6pt]
       &= 1.000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute centring factor k:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
k &= \frac{2\mu - (\text{USL} + \text{LSL})}{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}} \\[6pt]
  &= \frac{2(25.04) - (25.10 + 24.90)}{0.20} \\[6pt]
  &= \frac{50.08 - 50.00}{0.20} \\[6pt]
  &= \frac{0.08}{0.20} = 0.40
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verification:** $C_{pk} = C_p (1 - k) = 1.667 \times (1 - 0.40) = 1.667 \times 0.60 = 1.000$ ✓

**Interpretation:**

> [!IMPORTANT] **Engineering Verdict**
> - **Cp = 1.667** indicates the *spread* is acceptable (≥ 1.33).
> - **Cpk = 1.000** indicates the process is **off-centre** by 40 % of half-tolerance. The mean has drifted toward the upper spec limit.
> - **Recommended corrective action:** Re-centre the CNC machine tool offset to bring μ from 25.04 mm → 25.00 mm. After re-centring, Cpk would rise from 1.00 → 1.667 (ideal target value).

---

## 3.4 Worked Example #2 — Six-Sigma Z-Value & DPMO Conversion

> [!NOTE] **Problem Statement**
> A pharmaceutical tablet press fills capsules. Target weight = 500 mg.
> Specification: LSL = 490 mg, USL = 510 mg.
> Sample size: **1 000 000 capsules** produced in one week. Defects found: **233**.
> Compute: (a) the short-term Z-score, (b) the long-term sigma level, (c) DPMO, and (d) the process yield.

**Step 1 — Compute DPMO:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{DPMO} &= \frac{\text{Number of Defects} \times 10^{6}}{\text{Number of Units} \times \text{Opportunities per Unit}} \\[6pt]
            &= \frac{233 \times 10^{6}}{1\,000\,000 \times 1} \\[6pt]
            &= 233 \text{ DPMO}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert DPMO to Defect Rate (per unit):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
p &= \frac{\text{DPMO}}{10^{6}} = \frac{233}{1\,000\,000} = 2.33 \times 10^{-4}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the One-Sided Tail Probability:**

Assuming one-sided specification (LSL only), the area beyond LSL is 2.33 × 10⁻⁴.

For a standard normal Z:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(Z < -Z_{\text{ST}}) &= 2.33 \times 10^{-4} \\[6pt]
\Rightarrow Z_{\text{ST}} &= 3.5 \quad (\text{from standard normal tables})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the 1.5σ Long-Term Shift Correction:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Z_{\text{LT}} &= Z_{\text{ST}} + 1.5 \\[6pt]
              &= 3.5 + 1.5 = 5.0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute Process Yield:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Yield} &= \Phi(Z_{\text{LT}}) \times 100\% \\[6pt]
             &= \Phi(5.0) \times 100\% \\[6pt]
             &= (1 - 2.87 \times 10^{-7}) \times 100\% \\[6pt]
             &\approx 99.99997\,\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** This is a **5-Sigma** process. KTU benchmark for "world-class" is **6σ** (Z_LT ≥ 6.0).

---

## 3.5 Worked Example #3 — Taguchi Cpm Computation

> [!NOTE] **Problem Statement**
> A resistor manufacturing line has nominal resistance T = 100 Ω.
> Specs: LSL = 95 Ω, USL = 105 Ω.
> Process data: μ = 100.5 Ω, σ = 1.2 Ω.
> Compute Cp, Cpk, and Cpm. Comment.

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_p &= \frac{105 - 95}{6 \times 1.2} = \frac{10}{7.2} = 1.389 \\[6pt]
C_{pu} &= \frac{105 - 100.5}{3.6} = \frac{4.5}{3.6} = 1.250 \\[6pt]
C_{pl} &= \frac{100.5 - 95}{3.6} = \frac{5.5}{3.6} = 1.528 \\[6pt]
C_{pk} &= \min(1.250,\, 1.528) = 1.250 \\[6pt]
C_{pm} &= \frac{10}{6 \sqrt{1.2^{2} + (100.5 - 100)^{2}}} \\[6pt]
       &= \frac{10}{6\sqrt{1.44 + 0.25}} \\[6pt]
       &= \frac{10}{6 \sqrt{1.69}} \\[6pt]
       &= \frac{10}{6 \times 1.30} = \frac{10}{7.80} = 1.282
\end{aligned}
$$

**Comment:** Cpm (1.282) is **lower** than Cpk (1.250) → wait, Cpm is **higher** than Cpk here? Actually Cpm (1.282) > Cpk (1.250) because the deviation from target (|μ − T| = 0.5) is small relative to σ. If μ were further from T, Cpm would *drop* below Cpk, exposing Taguchi's philosophy that **any deviation from target incurs quality loss**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Process Capability Analysis Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Customer Requirement: USL LSL Target T] --> B0[Collect Subgroup Data n >= 25]
    B0 --> C0[Establish Statistical Control: Xbar R Chart]
    C0 --> D0{Process in Control?}
    D0 -- No --> E0[Remove Assignable Causes: Tooling Lot Operator]
    E0 --> B0
    D0 -- Yes --> F0[Estimate Sigma: within and overall]
    F0 --> G0[Compute Cp Spread Index]
    F0 --> H0[Compute Cpk Centred Index]
    F0 --> I0[Compute Pp Ppk Long Term]
    F0 --> J0[Compute Cpm Taguchi Index]
    G0 --> K0[Plot Histogram with Spec Limits]
    H0 --> K0
    I0 --> K0
    J0 --> K0
    K0 --> L0{Cpk Greater Than 1.33}
    L0 -- Yes --> M0[Process Capable: Routine SPC Monitoring]
    L0 -- No --> N0[Not Capable: Re Centre Reduce Sigma]
    N0 --> O0[DOE Root Cause Analysis]
    O0 --> P0[Implement Corrective Action]
    P0 --> B0
```

## 4.2 Schematic — Cp vs Cpk Centring Geometry

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CaseA[Case A: Cp and Cpk Equal at 1.67]
        A1[LSL] --> A2[Mean Mu centred]
        A2 --> A3[USL]
        A1 -.6 Sigma.-> A2
        A2 -.6 Sigma.-> A3
    end
    subgraph CaseB[Case B: Cp 1.67 but Cpk 1.00 Mean Shifted Up]
        B1[LSL] --> B2[Mean Mu shifted high]
        B2 --> B3[USL]

        B2 -.2 Sigma.-> B3
    end
    subgraph CaseC[Case C: Cp 0.83 and Cpk 0.83 Process Inadequate]
        C1[LSL] --> C2[Mean Mu centred]
        C2 --> C3[USL]
        C1 -.5 Sigma.-> C2
        C2 -.5 Sigma.-> C3
    end
```

## 4.3 Six-Sigma Process Spacing Model

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    N1[LSL] --> N2[Mu Minus 6 Sigma]
    N2 --> N3[Mu Minus 3 Sigma]
    N3 --> N4[Mu Process Mean]
    N4 --> N5[Mu Plus 3 Sigma]
    N5 --> N6[Mu Plus 6 Sigma]
    N6 --> N7[USL]
    N1 -. 12 Sigma .-> N7
    N2 -. Short Term Limit .-> N6
    N5 -. Short Term Limit .-> N6
    N4 -. 1.5 Sigma Drift .-> N1
```

## 4.4 Capability Index Decision Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q0[Compute Cpk and Ppk] --> Q1{Is Cpk Greater Than 1.67}
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2[World Class Six Sigma]
    Q1 -- No --> Q3{Is Cpk Between 1.33 and 1.67}
    Q3 -- Yes --> Q4[Capable Process Routine Monitoring]
    Q3 -- No --> Q5{Is Cpk Between 1.00 and 1.33}
    Q5 -- Yes --> Q6[Marginally Capable Tight Control 100 percent Inspection]
    Q5 -- No --> Q7{Cpk Less Than 1.00}
    Q7 -- Yes --> Q8[Not Capable Stop Production Fundamental Redesign]
    Q8 --> Q9[DOE Failure Mode Analysis]
    Q9 --> Q10[Re Engineer Process or Relax Spec]
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Define Process Capability. Differentiate between Cp and Cpk.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, May 2024 model]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Process Capability** is a statistical measure of the ability of a stable process to produce output within specification limits, expressed as the ratio of the engineering tolerance (Voice of the Customer) to the natural process spread (Voice of the Process).
>
> | Aspect | Cp | Cpk |
> |:------|:--|:----|
> | What it measures | Spread only | Spread **and** centring |
> | Formula | (USL − LSL) / 6σ | min[(USL − μ)/3σ, (μ − LSL)/3σ] |
> | When used | Centred process (μ = T) | Off-centred real processes |
> | Value range | Cp ≥ Cpk always | Cp = Cpk only when μ is mid-tolerance |

**(Marks allocation: Definition 1 m, Cp formula 0.5 m, Cpk formula 0.5 m, distinction table 1 m.)**

---

### **Q2. What is Six-Sigma quality? Mention the role of the 1.5σ shift.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Six-Sigma quality** is the level of process performance where the distance from the process mean to the nearest specification limit is at least **6 standard deviations** on each side, allowing for a **1.5σ long-term mean drift**. This corresponds to a defect rate of **3.4 DPMO** (3.4 defects per million opportunities).
>
> The **1.5σ shift** is a Motorola-incorporated empirical correction that accounts for the long-term mean drift of any real process between re-calibrations. Hence the *short-term* Z-score requirement is 4.5σ to deliver a *long-term* 6σ performance.
>
> **Six-Sigma = Process variation so small that even after the mean shifts by 1.5σ, no output exceeds the specification limits.**

**(Marks: Definition 1.5 m, DPMO value 0.5 m, role of 1.5σ shift 1 m.)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting units**: Cp is dimensionless — students sometimes write "Cp = 1.67 mm" and lose 1 mark.
> 2. **Confusing σ_within vs σ_overall**: Cp uses **within-subgroup σ** (from R-bar / d2), while Pp uses **overall σ** (s). Interchanging them is a **2-mark penalty**.
> 3. **Skipping the centring factor k**: When Cpk is asked, always compute both Cpu and Cpl separately, then take the minimum. Skipping this step loses 1 mark.
> 4. **Mis-stating DPMO formula**: DPMO uses *opportunities per unit*, not defects per unit. Read the question carefully.
> 5. **Ignoring statistical control**: Examiners love to add "the process is in statistical control" as a precondition. Failing to mention it loses 0.5 mark.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 model]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** A grinding operation produces piston pins. Specification: **diameter = 20.00 ± 0.05 mm** (i.e., LSL = 19.95, USL = 20.05 mm). A sample of 50 pins taken under statistical control gave:

- Sample mean x̄ = **20.025 mm**
- Average range R̄ = **0.018 mm**
- (For subgroup size n = 5, control-chart constant **d₂ = 2.326**)

**Compute Cp and Cpk and interpret the process capability.**

#### Step-by-Step Model Solution:

**Step 1 — Estimate σ from the within-subgroup range:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\hat{\sigma}_{\text{within}} &= \frac{\bar{R}}{d_2} = \frac{0.018}{2.326} = 0.00774 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Cp:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_p &= \frac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sigma} = \frac{20.05 - 19.95}{6 \times 0.00774} \\[6pt]
    &= \frac{0.10}{0.04644} = 2.153
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Cpu and Cpl:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{pu} &= \frac{20.05 - 20.025}{3 \times 0.00774} = \frac{0.025}{0.02322} = 1.077 \\[6pt]
C_{pl} &= \frac{20.025 - 19.95}{3 \times 0.00774} = \frac{0.075}{0.02322} = 3.230
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Cpk (worst-case):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{pk} = \min(1.077,\, 3.230) = 1.077
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Centring factor:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
k = \frac{2(20.025) - (20.05 + 19.95)}{0.10} = \frac{40.05 - 40.00}{0.10} = 0.50
\end{aligned}
$$

Verification: $C_{pk} = C_p (1 - k) = 2.153 \times 0.5 = 1.077$ ✓

**Interpretation:**

> The process **spread is excellent** (Cp = 2.15), but the **mean is shifted upward** by 50 % of half-tolerance. **Cpk = 1.08** indicates the process is only **marginally capable**. **Recommended action:** re-centre the grinding wheel to bring μ from 20.025 mm → 20.00 mm. After re-centring, Cpk would equal Cp = 2.15, making the process **highly capable (Six-Sigma territory).**

**(Mark split: σ estimate 1 m, Cp 1 m, Cpu / Cpl 2 m, Cpk 1 m, k + verification 1 m, interpretation 1 m = Total 7 m.)**

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the **Six-Sigma methodology** with a neat block diagram. List any **four advantages** of adopting Six-Sigma in a manufacturing organisation.

#### Model Solution:

**Definition:** Six-Sigma is a data-driven quality philosophy developed by Motorola (1986) and popularised by General Electric, which seeks to reduce process variation so that the natural process spread (12σ) fits inside the design tolerance with a margin of ±6σ on each side, after allowing for a 1.5σ long-term mean shift.

**Six-Sigma Block Diagram (DMAIC):**

```
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                                                              │
│   ┌────────────┐    ┌────────────┐    ┌────────────┐         │
│   │  D - Define │ -> │  M - Measure│ -> │  A - Analyse│        │
│   │  Problem   │    │  Capability │    │  Root Cause │        │
│   │  CTQ       │    │  Sigma      │    │  DOE        │        │
│   └────────────┘    └────────────┘    └────────────┘         │
│          ↑                                    ↓              │
│   ┌────────────┐    ┌────────────┐                            │
│   │  C - Control│ <- │  I - Improve│                            │
│   │  SPC Charts │    │  Pilot Run  │                            │
│   │  Mistake    │    │  Validate   │                            │
│   │  Proofing   │    │             │                            │
│   └────────────┘    └────────────┘                            │
│                                                              │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

**Four Advantages of Six-Sigma Adoption:**

1. **Drastic reduction in defect rate** (from ~66 800 DPMO at 3σ to **3.4 DPMO** at 6σ → 99.9997 % yield).
2. **Lower cost of poor quality (COPQ)** through prevention-based rather than detection-based quality control.
3. **Improved customer satisfaction** as Voice of the Customer (VOC) is directly translated into CTQ metrics.
4. **Strong ROI** — GE famously reported **USD 2 billion in savings** within 5 years of Six-Sigma rollout (1995–2000).
5. *(Any other valid advantage — e.g., culture change, data-driven decision-making, supplier integration.)*

**(Mark split: Six-Sigma definition 1 m, DMAIC block diagram 3 m, four advantages 3 m = Total 7 m.)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 model]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** A bottling plant fills 1-litre water bottles. Specification: **Volume = 1000 ± 10 mL** (LSL = 990, USL = 1010). A long-term study of 1 000 bottles gave:

- Mean μ = **1002 mL**
- Overall standard deviation σ_overall = **3.0 mL**

Calculate **Pp, Ppk, and the long-term Z-score Z_LT**. Comment on whether the process qualifies as a Six-Sigma process.

#### Model Solution:

**Step 1 — Compute Pp:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_p &= \frac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6 \sigma_{\text{overall}}} = \frac{1010 - 990}{6 \times 3.0} = \frac{20}{18.0} = 1.111
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Ppu and Ppl:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{pu} &= \frac{1010 - 1002}{3 \times 3.0} = \frac{8}{9.0} = 0.889 \\[6pt]
P_{pl} &= \frac{1002 - 990}{3 \times 3.0} = \frac{12}{9.0} = 1.333
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Ppk (worst-side):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{pk} = \min(0.889,\, 1.333) = 0.889
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Short-Term Z (Z_ST):**

The mean is closer to the USL (since μ > midpoint 1000). Distance to nearest spec = USL − μ = 8 mL.

$$
\begin{aligned}
Z_{\text{ST}} &= \frac{\text{USL} - \mu}{3\sigma} = \frac{1010 - 1002}{3 \times 3.0} = \frac{8}{9.0} = 0.889
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Apply 1.5σ Long-Term Shift:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Z_{\text{LT}} = Z_{\text{ST}} + 1.5 = 0.889 + 1.5 = 2.389
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Verdict on Six-Sigma Status:**

| Test | Result | Verdict |
|:-----|:------:|:-------:|
| Ppk ≥ 1.33? | 0.889 → NO | Fails |
| Z_LT ≥ 6.0? | 2.389 → NO | Fails badly |
| Process Sigma Level | ~2.4 σ | Far from Six-Sigma |

**Interpretation:** The bottling process is **NOT Six-Sigma**. It is a **2.4σ process** with very high defect rate. To reach Six-Sigma, σ must be reduced to (USL − μ)/(6 × Z_ST_long) = 8 / (6 × 4.5) = **0.296 mL** (a **10×** reduction in σ). **Corrective actions:** (i) Re-centre filling head to 1000 mL, (ii) Reduce variation through maintenance, (iii) Implement closed-loop auto-correction.

**(Mark split: Pp 1 m, Ppu/Ppl 2 m, Ppk 1 m, Z_ST 1 m, Z_LT 1 m, interpretation 1 m = Total 7 m.)**

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **Taguchi Loss Function** and derive the **Cpm index**. Show why Cpm penalises off-target operation more strictly than Cpk.

#### Model Solution:

**Taguchi's Quality Loss Function (QLF):**

> Genichi Taguchi argued that any deviation from the **target value T** — even within specifications — produces a **quadratic loss** to society (rework, warranty, customer dissatisfaction, brand erosion).

$$
\begin{aligned}
L(x) = k\,(x - T)^{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

where **k** is the quality loss coefficient, related to the cost of replacement at the specification limit:

$$
\begin{aligned}
k = \frac{A_0}{\Delta_0^{2}}, \quad \Delta_0 = \text{USL} - T = T - \text{LSL}, \quad A_0 = \text{loss at spec limit}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Expected Loss over many units (when μ is the mean):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E[L] = k\,\!\left[\sigma^{2} + (\mu - T)^{2}\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

The bracketed term $\sigma^{2} + (\mu - T)^{2}$ is the **Mean Squared Deviation from Target (MSDT)**, which combines variability AND off-targetedness.

**Derivation of Cpm:**

> Cpm normalises this MSDT against the tolerance. It is defined as the ratio of the tolerance width to 6 × √(MSDT):

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{pm} &= \frac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sqrt{\sigma^{2} + (\mu - T)^{2}}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Comparison of Cpk vs Cpm:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{pk} &= \frac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL} - 2\vert \mu - M \vert}{6\sigma} \quad \text{where } M = \frac{\text{USL} + \text{LSL}}{2} \\[6pt]
C_{pm} &= \frac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sqrt{\sigma^{2} + (\mu - T)^{2}}}
\end{aligned}
$$

- **Cpk** penalises only the **linear distance from midpoint** |μ − M|.
- **Cpm** penalises the **squared distance from target** (μ − T)², embedded inside a square root. For the same mean shift, Cpm always drops **more sharply** than Cpk.

**Numerical Illustration (using Example 3 from Section 3.5):**

When μ = 100.5, T = 100, σ = 1.2:

- Cpk = 1.250
- Cpm = 1.282 (slightly higher here because T and M coincide)

If instead T = 100 and M = 100.5 (asymmetric spec) with μ = 100.5:
- Cpk = 1.250
- Cpm drops because (μ − T)² is large.

**Engineering Significance:**

Cpm is the **preferred index** in Japanese automotive and electronics industries (Toyota, Honda, Sony) where minimising deviation from target is more important than merely staying within spec. It aligns with **Design for Six-Sigma (DFSS)** philosophies.

**(Mark split: QLF definition 2 m, QLF formula 1 m, Cpm derivation 2 m, comparison with Cpk 2 m = Total 7 m.)**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT] **Last-Minute KTU Revision Bulletins**

- [x] **Process Capability = VOP vs VOC** comparison.
- [x] **Cp** ignores centring → use only when μ = midpoint.
- [x] **Cpk = min(Cpu, Cpl)** → always compute both sides.
- [x] **Cp ≥ Cpk always** (equality when μ is centred).
- [x] **Cpk = Cp × (1 − |k|)** — quick centring check.
- [x] **k = 0** → centred, **k = ±1** → mean touches spec.
- [x] **σ_within = R̄ / d₂** (use n = 5 ⇒ d₂ = 2.326).
- [x] **σ_overall = s** (sample standard deviation from raw data).
- [x] **Cp and Cpk use σ_within**; **Pp and Ppk use σ_overall**.
- [x] **Six-Sigma** = Z_LT ≥ 6.0 ⇒ 3.4 DPMO.
- [x] **Z_LT = Z_ST + 1.5** (Motorola shift correction).
- [x] **DPMO = (Defects × 10⁶) / (Units × Opportunities)**.
- [x] **Cpm** is Taguchi's target-centred index using √(σ² + (μ − T)²).
- [x] **Capability Benchmarks:** < 1 = fail; 1.0–1.33 = marginal; 1.33–1.67 = capable; > 1.67 = world-class.
- [x] **Always verify statistical control** (X-bar / R chart) before computing any capability index.
- [x] **KTU 2024 typical weightage:** 14 marks Part B question + 3 marks Part A definition (≈ 17 marks, ~17 % of paper).

---

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET758 - QUALITY ENGINEERING AND MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU Syllabus-Standard Definition**
> **Acceptance Sampling** is a statistical quality control technique in which a **random sample** is drawn from a lot (or batch) of finished products, and the decision to **accept** or **reject** the entire lot is based on the inspection results of that sample. It is a *lot-by-lot* sentencing procedure applied at the **producer–consumer interface**, after production is complete but before the lot is integrated into the supply chain.

**Key Terminology (KTU Glossary Standard):**

| Term | Expansion | Meaning |
|------|-----------|---------|
| **Lot (N)** | Batch Size | The total number of items submitted for inspection together |
| **Sample (n)** | Sample Size | The number of items randomly drawn from the lot |
| **Acceptance Number (c)** | — | The maximum number of defectives allowed in the sample for lot acceptance |
| **Rejectable Quality Level (RQL/LTPD)** | Lot Tolerance Percent Defective | The worst quality the consumer is willing to tolerate |
| **Acceptable Quality Level (AQL)** | — | The maximum quality level acceptable as a process average |
| **α (Producer's Risk)** | Type I Error | Probability of rejecting a *good* lot |
| **β (Consumer's Risk)** | Type II Error | Probability of accepting a *bad* lot |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Coconut Grading Problem 🍛

Imagine a Kerala spice trader who buys **large gunny bags of cardamom** from farmers. He cannot open and inspect every single pod (that would be too slow and destructive). So he randomly picks a **handful of pods** (the *sample*) from random corners of the bag. If the handful contains very few black/damaged pods, he accepts the entire bag. If too many are damaged, he rejects the whole bag.

> [!NOTE]
> The trader is **not measuring the quality of every pod** — he is making a **decision about the whole lot** based on a **small random subset**. This is exactly what Acceptance Sampling does in a manufacturing line.

## 1.3 Why Use Acceptance Sampling? (Engineering Justification)

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU High-Yield Point**
> Acceptance Sampling is **preferred over 100% inspection** when:
> 1. **Destructive Testing** is involved (e.g., tensile strength of steel, bomb testing, fuse testing).
> 2. **Cost of 100% inspection** exceeds the cost of passing some defectives (e.g., screws, washers).
> 3. **Fatigue/Monotony** of inspectors leads to higher error rates during 100% inspection.
> 4. **Large batch sizes** make 100% inspection impractical.
> 5. The product consists of **multiple components** whose joint quality must be assessed.

## 1.4 When NOT to Use Acceptance Sampling

- When the cost of a defective item escaping is **catastrophic** (e.g., aircraft engines, pacemakers, nuclear fuel rods).
- When inspection is **very cheap** and the lot is **very small**.
- When **in-process control** is more effective.
