> 2. **Confusing SQM obstacles with TQM obstacles** — TQM is broader; SQM specifically links to *strategy*. Always anchor your answer in the *strategic* context.
> 3. **Ignoring Indian context** — KTU papers reward *regionally relevant* examples (MSME sector, Kerala's industrial clusters, Make-in-India quality challenges). Use them.
> 4. **Skipping the human/cultural dimension** — Students love to write about ISO/Six Sigma tools and forget the *soft* obstacles. Examiner deducts 2–3 marks.
> 5. **No logical structure** — Always use the *Cause → Symptom → Consequence → Remedy* chain. It is the examiner's marking grid.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **SQM = enterprise-wide, CEO-driven, long-term, strategy-aligned quality management.**
- ✅ **Obstacles fall under 5 pillars: Leadership, Culture, Structure, Communication, Resources (L-C-S-C-R).**
- ✅ **Top management commitment is the #1 obstacle** — *almost every KTU question expects this.*
- ✅ **The "knowing-doing gap" is the central theme** — knowing the strategy ≠ executing the strategy.
- ✅ **Technical tools (SPC, FMEA, Six Sigma) are enablers, not solutions** — culture and leadership make them work.
- ✅ **Always use the chain:** *Cause → Symptom → Consequence → Remedy* in 14-mark answers.
- ✅ **Mention the CoQ model:** If prevention costs are low and appraisal costs are high, leadership commitment is weak.
- ✅ **Kotter's 8-step change model** is the gold-standard remedy for resistance-related obstacles.
- ✅ **Real-world anchors** — Toyota (Gemba, Andon, Hoshin Kanri), Deming (14 Points), Juran (Trilogy, CoQ), Baldrige/EFQM (excellence models).
- ✅ **Indian/Kerala context examples score extra** — MSME clusters, coir/spice/electronics manufacturing quality issues.
- ✅ **Avoid vague phrases** like "lack of awareness" — name the *specific* obstacle, e.g., "absence of a documented competency matrix for Six Sigma deployment."
- ✅ **The 5-pillar diagnostic wheel** is a powerful diagram to use in answers — visually impressive to examiners.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cost of Quality
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET758 - QUALITY ENGINEERING AND MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Cost of Quality

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cost of Quality (COQ) Definition:** Cost of Quality is the sum of all costs incurred in the design, implementation, operation, and maintenance of a quality system, plus the costs associated with failures—both internal (detected before delivery) and external (detected after delivery to the customer). It is essentially the "price of non-conformance" plus the "price of conformance."

Mathematically, it is expressed as:

$$COQ = C_{Prevention} + C_{Appraisal} + C_{Internal\,Failure} + C_{External\,Failure}$$

Where each component represents a distinct category of expenditure related to ensuring or failing to ensure product/service quality.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** COQ is a financial measurement that allows management to quantify the impact of quality decisions on profitability. It is one of the most important tools in the *Quality Management System (QMS)* because it translates quality concepts into monetary terms that top management understands.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy – "The Car Maintenance Analogy":**
>
> Imagine you own a car. The **Cost of Quality** is everything you spend related to keeping the car running well and avoiding breakdowns:
>
> - **Prevention Costs** → Regular oil changes, tire rotations, and following the service schedule. You pay now to avoid big bills later.
> - **Appraisal Costs** → Taking the car for periodic inspections and diagnostic tests to check that everything is still working.
> - **Internal Failure Costs** → Your mechanic finds a worn-out brake pad during inspection and replaces it **before** you drive. The car never reached the customer (you) with the defect.
> - **External Failure Costs** → The brake pad failed while you were driving on the highway, causing an accident. Now you have tow truck fees, repair bills, insurance claims, and—worst of all—a loss of trust in the car brand.
>
> Just like a car, a company must spend money on **prevention and appraisal** to **drastically reduce** the much larger **failure costs** that occur when defective products reach customers.

## 1.3 The Philosophy of COQ

The fundamental philosophy behind COQ is rooted in the work of quality gurus like **Joseph M. Juran** and **Philip B. Crosby**, who argued that:

> **"Quality is free. It is not a gift, but it is free. What costs money are the unquality things—all the rework, waste, and repair."** — *Philip B. Crosby*

This means that every rupee spent on **prevention** saves many more rupees that would otherwise be lost to **failure costs**. Companies that fail to invest in quality pay—only they pay it through rework, warranty claims, lost customers, and damaged reputation.

## 1.4 Categories Overview (P-A-F Model)

The **P-A-F Model** (also called the **Pareto-based Quality Cost Model**) classifies all quality-related costs into four major categories:

| # | Category | Acronym | One-Line Meaning |
|---|----------|---------|------------------|
| 1 | Prevention Costs | **P** | Money spent to *prevent* defects from occurring |
| 2 | Appraisal Costs | **A** | Money spent to *detect* defects through inspection/testing |
| 3 | Internal Failure Costs | **IF** | Cost of defects found *before* reaching the customer |
| 4 | External Failure Costs | **EF** | Cost of defects found *after* reaching the customer |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** Prevention and Appraisal costs are **"Costs of Conformance"** (investment in doing things right), while Internal and External Failure costs are **"Costs of Non-Conformance"** (the penalty for doing things wrong).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Breakdown of the Four COQ Categories

### 2.1.1 Prevention Costs (P)

These are costs incurred to **prevent poor quality from occurring in the first place**. They represent proactive investments in quality.

**Examples:**
- Quality planning and administration
- New product review
- Quality training and education programs
- Process capability studies (e.g., Cpk analysis)
- Supplier capability surveys and certification
- Quality improvement projects (Kaizen, Six Sigma)
- Designing quality into the product (Design for Quality – DFQ)
- Field testing
- Statistical Process Control (SPC) implementation

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Prevention Matters:** Prevention is the **only category** where spending directly *reduces* the need for spending in the other three categories. A rupee spent on prevention typically saves **₹10 to ₹100** in failure costs.

### 2.1.2 Appraisal Costs (A)

These are costs incurred to **detect defects** through inspection, testing, and measurement activities. They are the costs of "looking for" quality problems.

**Examples:**
- Incoming material inspection
- In-process inspection and testing
- Final product testing and acceptance
- QC laboratory testing
- Calibration of test equipment
- Inspection of purchased materials
- Cost of inspectors and testing personnel
- Field testing of products
- Quality audits

### 2.1.3 Internal Failure Costs (IF)

These are costs resulting from defects discovered **before** the product reaches the customer. The defect is "caught in-house."

**Examples:**
- Scrap (defective material that cannot be reworked)
- Rework and repair
- Reinspection and retesting
- Material review and disposition
- Downtime due to quality problems
- Design changes to correct defects
- Software bug fixing during development
- Inventory write-offs due to defects

### 2.1.4 External Failure Costs (EF)

These are costs resulting from defects discovered **after** the product reaches the customer. These are the most damaging costs because they affect the customer directly.

**Examples:**
- Warranty claims and repair costs
- Product recall expenses
- Returned materials handling
- Field service personnel costs
- Customer complaints handling
- Product liability lawsuits
- Loss of customer goodwill and brand damage
- Lost sales and market share erosion
- Discounts/allowances to compensate for poor quality
- Customer acquisition costs to replace lost customers

## 2.2 The COQ Curve (Optimal Quality Cost Model)

A classic concept in quality economics is the **COQ Curve**, which plots total quality cost against the level of quality (or inversely, against the level of defects).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Economic Principle:** As quality investment increases, failure costs **decrease rapidly**, but prevention/appraisal costs **increase**. There exists an **optimal point (Q\*)** where total quality cost is **minimized**.

The mathematical model can be expressed as:

$$TC(Q) = P(Q) + A(Q) + F(Q)$$

Where:
- $TC(Q)$ = Total Cost of Quality at quality level $Q$
- $P(Q)$ = Prevention Costs as a function of $Q$
- $A(Q)$ = Appraisal Costs as a function of $Q$
- $F(Q)$ = Failure Costs as a function of $Q$ (both internal and external)

> [!NOTE]
> At very **low** quality levels: Failure costs dominate (high scrap, rework, warranty)
> At very **high** quality levels: Prevention costs dominate (over-engineering, excessive inspection)
> At **optimal Q\***: Total cost is minimized → this is the **economic quality level**

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Expression | Application |
|---|---|---|
| Total Cost of Quality | $COQ = P + A + IF + EF$ | Master equation for all COQ calculations |
| Total Quality Cost Function | $TC(Q) = P(Q) + A(Q) + F(Q)$ | Used to find optimal quality level |
| Optimum Quality Point | $\dfrac{d\,TC(Q)}{dQ} = 0$ | Calculus condition for minimum total cost |
| Failure Cost Trade-off | $\Delta F \approx -k \cdot \Delta P$ | Empirical: a unit increase in prevention causes a $k$-fold reduction in failures |
| Cost of Conformance (CoC) | $CoC = P + A$ | "Investment" in quality |
| Cost of Non-Conformance (CNCo) | $CNCo = IF + EF$ | "Penalty" for poor quality |
| Quality Cost Ratio (Typical) | $P : A : IF : EF = 5 : 25 : 35 : 35$ | Industry benchmark before improvement (varies widely) |
| Sales-to-COG Quality Cost % | $\dfrac{COQ}{\text{Sales}} \times 100$ | Common KPI: 2.5%–5% of sales is typical |
| Quality Cost per Unit | $QCPU = \dfrac{Total\,COQ}{Number\,of\,units}$ | Per-unit COQ for pricing decisions |
| Zero Defects Concept | $\lim_{Q \to 100\%} F(Q) \to 0$ | Crosby's philosophy: cost of poor quality is the only true cost |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **No vertical pipes used** in the table above to prevent markdown table breakage. All absolute values are expressed as inline notation if needed.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In engineering industries, COQ is used to:
- **Justify** quality improvement investments to top management
- **Identify** the categories where the company is "leaking money"
- **Track** improvement progress over time using trend charts
- **Benchmark** against industry standards (e.g., a typical manufacturer spends 2.5%–5% of sales on COQ; service companies may spend 15%–25%)
- **Set** departmental budgets for quality functions
- **Comply** with ISO 9001:2015 quality management system requirements (Clause 9.1.1 – Monitoring, measurement, analysis, and evaluation)

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Optimal Quality Level

This is a high-yield derivation that KTU examiners love to test. We will derive the condition for the **minimum total quality cost**.

### Step 1: Define the Total Cost Function

The total quality cost is the sum of conformance costs (prevention + appraisal) and non-conformance costs (internal + external failure):

$$TC(Q) = C_{Prevention}(Q) + C_{Appraisal}(Q) + C_{Failure}(Q)$$

### Step 2: Behavioral Assumptions for Each Cost Component

**Assumption A — Failure costs decrease with quality:**

As quality level $Q$ improves (i.e., defect rate decreases), failure costs fall. We model this as a monotonically decreasing function. A common form is exponential decay:

$$C_{Failure}(Q) = C_{F0} \cdot e^{-\alpha Q}$$

Where:
- $C_{F0}$ = failure cost at zero quality investment (baseline scrap/warranty cost)
- $\alpha$ = sensitivity coefficient (how fast failures drop with quality investment)
- $Q$ = quality investment level (in monetary units or % of sales)

**Assumption B — Prevention + Appraisal costs increase with quality:**

Conformance costs rise as the organization invests more in prevention and appraisal. A linear approximation:

$$C_{P}(Q) + C_{A}(Q) = \beta \cdot Q + \gamma$$

Where:
- $\beta$ = marginal cost of prevention per unit of investment
- $\gamma$ = fixed appraisal cost (mandatory baseline inspection)

### Step 3: Total Cost Function

Combining both:

$$TC(Q) = \beta \cdot Q + \gamma + C_{F0} \cdot e^{-\alpha Q}$$

### Step 4: Differentiate to Find the Minimum

To find the **optimal quality investment level $Q^{\*}$**, take the derivative with respect to $Q$ and set it equal to zero:

$$\frac{d\,TC(Q)}{dQ} = \beta - \alpha \cdot C_{F0} \cdot e^{-\alpha Q} = 0$$

### Step 5: Solve the First-Order Condition

Rearranging:

$$\alpha \cdot C_{F0} \cdot e^{-\alpha Q^{*}} = \beta$$

$$e^{-\alpha Q^{*}} = \frac{\beta}{\alpha \cdot C_{F0}}$$

Taking the natural logarithm of both sides:

$$-\alpha Q^{*} = \ln\!\left(\frac{\beta}{\alpha \cdot C_{F0}}\right)$$

$$Q^{*} = -\frac{1}{\alpha} \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{\beta}{\alpha \cdot C_{F0}}\right)$$

Or equivalently:

$$Q^{*} = \frac{1}{\alpha} \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{\alpha \cdot C_{F0}}{\beta}\right)$$

### Step 6: Second-Order Condition (Confirming it's a Minimum)

The second derivative is:

$$\frac{d^{2}TC(Q)}{dQ^{2}} = \alpha^{2} \cdot C_{F0} \cdot e^{-\alpha Q}$$

Since $\alpha^2 > 0$, $C_{F0} > 0$, and $e^{-\alpha Q} > 0$ for all real $Q$, we have:

$$\frac{d^{2}TC(Q)}{dQ^{2}} > 0 \quad \forall\,Q$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** The critical point $Q^{\*}$ is indeed a **minimum** of the total cost function. This confirms the existence of an economically optimal quality level.

### Step 7: Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A company estimates:
- Failure cost at zero investment: $C_{F0} = ₹10{,}00{,}000$
- Sensitivity coefficient: $\alpha = 0.5$ per lakh
- Marginal prevention cost: $\beta = ₹20{,}000$ per unit of $Q$

**Step 1: Calculate $\alpha \cdot C_{F0}$**

$$\alpha \cdot C_{F0} = 0.5 \times 10{,}00{,}000 = 5{,}00{,}000$$

**Step 2: Calculate the ratio $\dfrac{\beta}{\alpha \cdot C_{F0}}$**

$$\frac{\beta}{\alpha \cdot C_{F0}} = \frac{20{,}000}{5{,}00{,}000} = 0.04$$

**Step 3: Apply the optimality formula**

$$Q^{*} = -\frac{1}{0.5} \cdot \ln(0.04) = -2 \times (-3.2189) \approx 6.44$$

**Step 4: Compute Minimum Total Cost**

At $Q^{\*} = 6.44$:

$$e^{-\alpha Q^{*}} = e^{-0.5 \times 6.44} = e^{-3.22} \approx 0.0399$$

$$TC(Q^{*}) = 20{,}000 \times 6.44 + \gamma + 10{,}00{,}000 \times 0.0399$$

$$TC(Q^{*}) = 1{,}28{,}800 + \gamma + 39{,}900 = 1{,}68{,}700 + \gamma$$

> [!NOTE]
> The fixed appraisal cost $\gamma$ is added separately. Compare this total to the cost at $Q = 0$ (which is just $\gamma + ₹10,00,000$ in failure costs alone), and the savings become evident.

## 3.2 The P-A-F Trade-off Matrix (Analytical)

| Investment Strategy | Prevention ↑ | Appraisal ↑ | Internal Failure ↓ | External Failure ↓ | Total COQ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Status Quo** | Low | Low | High | High | Very High |
| **Inspection-Heavy** | Low | Very High | Medium | High | High |
| **Prevention-Focused** | High | Moderate | Low | Very Low | **Minimum (Optimal)** |
| **Zero Defects Push** | Very High | High | Near Zero | Near Zero | Rising again |
| **Neglect Quality** | Very Low | Very Low | High | Very High | Maximum |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The P-A-F model tells us:** Shifting resources from appraisal and failure-handling into **prevention** is the most economically efficient strategy. This is the philosophical basis of **Total Quality Management (TQM)**.

## 3.3 Hidden Costs of Quality (Extension)

Many quality costs are **hidden** and not captured in standard accounting systems. These include:

$$COQ_{Total} = COQ_{Visible} + COQ_{Hidden}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Hidden costs** (per Juran's research) can be **3 to 4 times** the visible COQ. They include:
> - Customer dissatisfaction (lost future sales)
> - Loss of reputation and brand equity
> - Employee frustration and turnover due to rework
> - Schedule slippage due to defect correction
> - Excessive inventory buildup to buffer against quality problems
> - Engineering time diverted to fix field complaints
> - Line stoppages and machine downtime

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master P-A-F Cost Flow Diagram (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1[Quality Related Costs] --> node2[Costs of Conformance]
    node1 --> node3[Costs of Non-Conformance]
    node2 --> node4[Prevention Costs P]
    node2 --> node5[Appraisal Costs A]
    node3 --> node6[Internal Failure IF]
    node3 --> node7[External Failure EF]
    node4 --> nodeA1[Quality Training]
    node4 --> nodeA2[Process Capability Studies]
    node4 --> nodeA3[Supplier Certification]
    node4 --> nodeA4[Quality Planning]
    node5 --> nodeB1[In-process Inspection]
    node5 --> nodeB2[Final Product Testing]
    node5 --> nodeB3[Equipment Calibration]
    node5 --> nodeB4[Quality Audits]
    node6 --> nodeC1[Scrap and Rework]
    node6 --> nodeC2[Reinspection Cost]
    node6 --> nodeC3[Material Review]
    node6 --> nodeC4[Downtime Costs]
    node7 --> nodeD1[Warranty Claims]
    node7 --> nodeD2[Product Recalls]
    node7 --> nodeD3[Customer Complaints]
    node7 --> nodeD4[Lost Goodwill]
    node4 --> nodeSum[Total COQ]
    node5 --> nodeSum
    node6 --> nodeSum
    node7 --> nodeSum
```

## 4.2 The COQ Curve – Block Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Low Quality Investment] --> nodeB[Very High Failure Costs]
    nodeA --> nodeC[Low Prevention Costs]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Total Cost is HIGH]
    nodeB --> nodeD
    nodeE[Medium Quality Investment] --> nodeF[Moderate Failure Costs]
    nodeE --> nodeG[Moderate Prevention Costs]
    nodeG --> nodeH[Total Cost is MINIMUM]
    nodeF --> nodeH
    nodeI[Very High Quality Investment] --> nodeJ[Near Zero Failure Costs]
    nodeI --> nodeK[Very High Prevention Costs]
    nodeK --> nodeL[Total Cost RISES again]
    nodeJ --> nodeL
    nodeD --> nodeM[Find Optimum Point Qstar]
    nodeH --> nodeM
    nodeL --> nodeM
```

## 4.3 COQ Implementation Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    step1[Step 1: Identify Quality Costs] --> step2[Step 2: Classify into P A IF EF]
    step2 --> step3[Step 3: Establish Cost Centers and Accounts]
    step3 --> step4[Step 4: Collect and Record Data]
    step4 --> step5[Step 5: Analyze Trends and Variances]
    step5 --> step6[Step 6: Report to Management]
    step6 --> step7[Step 7: Identify Improvement Opportunities]
    step7 --> step8[Step 8: Take Corrective Action]
    step8 --> step9[Step 9: Monitor and Review]
    step9 --> step1
```

## 4.4 Strategic Trade-off Diagram (Pareto Logic)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    root[COQ Strategic Decision]
    root --> branch1[Reactive Approach: Inspect More]
    root --> branch2[Proactive Approach: Prevent More]
    branch1 --> sub1[Increase Appraisal A]
    sub1 --> out1[Short-term Defect Detection Improved]
    out1 --> final1[Failure Costs Still High in Long Term]
    branch2 --> sub2[Invest in Prevention P]
    sub2 --> out2[Process Capability Improves]
    out2 --> final2[All Other Costs Drop Significantly]
    final1 --> result[NOT RECOMMENDED]
    final2 --> result2[RECOMMENDED BY TQM]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Cost of Quality. List its four main categories.
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]`

**Model Answer:**

Cost of Quality (COQ) is the total financial outlay associated with ensuring products and services meet quality requirements, encompassing all prevention, appraisal, and failure-related expenditures.

The four main categories of COQ are:

1. **Prevention Costs (P):** Costs incurred to prevent defects from occurring (e.g., training, quality planning).
2. **Appraisal Costs (A):** Costs incurred to detect defects (e.g., inspection, testing).
3. **Internal Failure Costs (IF):** Costs of defects found before reaching the customer (e.g., scrap, rework).
4. **External Failure Costs (EF):** Costs of defects found after reaching the customer (e.g., warranty, returns).

The relationship is: $COQ = P + A + IF + EF$

---

### Question 2: Distinguish between Prevention Costs and Appraisal Costs with examples.
`[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Supplementary Exam]`

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Prevention Costs | Appraisal Costs |
|---|---|---|
| **Purpose** | Stop defects from *occurring* | *Detect* defects that have already occurred |
| **Timing** | Before the manufacturing/operational process | During or after the process |
| **Nature** | Proactive, forward-looking | Reactive, detective |
| **Examples** | Quality training, process capability studies, supplier certification, design reviews | Incoming inspection, final testing, calibration, quality audits |
| **Cost Impact** | Reduces failure costs in long run | Does not reduce defects, only identifies them |
| **Mindset** | Build quality *in* | Inspect quality *out* |

---

## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Choice A: Question A (14 Marks)

**Question:** Discuss in detail the four categories of Cost of Quality. Explain with a suitable diagram how the total cost of quality varies with the level of quality investment, and identify the optimum point. `[14 Marks]`

**Mark Distribution Hint:** Part (a) Categories – 7 marks | Part (b) Curve and optimum – 7 marks

#### Part (a) — Detailed Explanation of the Four Categories (7 Marks)

The Cost of Quality (COQ) classifies all quality-related expenditures into four categories:

**1. Prevention Costs (P):** These are the costs of activities specifically designed to prevent poor quality from occurring. Examples include:
- Quality planning and administration
- New product and process design reviews
- Employee quality training programs
- Statistical Process Control (SPC) implementation
- Supplier evaluation and certification
- Process capability studies (Cp, Cpk analysis)
- Quality improvement projects (Six Sigma, Kaizen)

These are **proactive investments** that build quality into the product.

**2. Appraisal Costs (A):** These are the costs of activities designed to detect defects after they have occurred but before the product reaches the customer. Examples include:
- Incoming raw material inspection
- In-process and final product testing
- Quality control laboratory testing
- Equipment calibration and maintenance
- Internal quality audits
- Cost of inspectors and testing personnel

Appraisal costs are **detection-based** and do not prevent defects; they only identify them.

**3. Internal Failure Costs (IF):** These are costs resulting from defects discovered *before* delivery to the customer. Examples include:
- Scrap (irrecoverable defective material)
- Rework and repair operations
- Reinspection and retesting of reworked products
- Material review board decisions
- Production line stoppages due to defects
- Design engineering changes to fix defects
- Inventory write-offs

**4. External Failure Costs (EF):** These are costs resulting from defects discovered *after* the product has reached the customer. Examples include:
- Warranty claim processing and repairs
- Product recall campaigns
- Handling of returned materials
- Field service technician costs
- Customer complaint management
- Product liability lawsuits
- Loss of customer goodwill and brand damage
- Lost future sales and market share

External failure costs are the **most damaging** category because they directly impact revenue and reputation.

The total Cost of Quality is therefore:

$$COQ = P + A + IF + EF$$

> [!NOTE]
> The first two categories (P, A) are called **"Costs of Conformance"** (CoC) — money spent to do things right. The latter two (IF, EF) are **"Costs of Non-Conformance"** (CNCo) — money lost because things went wrong.

#### Part (b) — COQ Curve and Optimum Quality Level (7 Marks)

The **COQ Curve** is a fundamental quality economics model that plots the four cost categories against the level of quality investment.

**Behavior of Each Curve:**

As quality investment (or quality level) increases:
- **Prevention Costs (P)** rise steadily — more training, more design effort
- **Appraisal Costs (A)** may rise initially, then stabilize at a level needed for compliance
- **Internal Failure Costs (IF)** fall sharply
- **External Failure Costs (EF)** fall sharply (the most dramatic drop)

**The Total Cost Curve:**

The total cost is the sum of all four:

$$TC(Q) = P(Q) + A(Q) + IF(Q) + EF(Q)$$

This total cost curve is **U-shaped**:
- At **low quality levels** (left side): Failure costs dominate, so total cost is very high
- At **moderate-to-high quality levels**: Prevention costs rise but failure costs fall more rapidly → total cost decreases
- At the **optimum point Q\***: The trade-off between rising prevention costs and falling failure costs reaches balance
- At **very high quality levels** (right side): Prevention costs become excessive (over-engineering), and total cost starts rising again

**The Optimum Point Q\*:** This is the level of quality investment that **minimizes the total cost of quality**. It is found mathematically by:

$$\frac{d\,TC(Q)}{dQ} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad Q^{*} = \frac{1}{\alpha} \ln\!\left(\frac{\alpha C_{F0}}{\beta}\right)$$

At $Q^{\*}$:
- Marginal savings in failure costs = marginal cost of additional prevention
- The organization achieves the **"economic quality level"**
- Beyond this point, additional prevention spending yields diminishing returns

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Crosby's View vs Juran's View:** Philip Crosby argues that the optimum point is at 100% quality (zero defects), because every defect still carries a hidden cost. Juran argues for an economic optimum (Q\*) where the total cost is minimized. Modern TQM leans toward Crosby's philosophy, using the COQ data primarily to **identify and eliminate failure costs** rather than to find a mathematical minimum.

---

### Choice B: Question B (14 Marks)

**Question:** Explain the process of implementing a Cost of Quality system in an organization. What are the typical cost elements under each category? Discuss the role of COQ in strategic decision-making. `[14 Marks]`

**Mark Distribution Hint:** Part (a) Implementation steps – 7 marks | Part (b) Cost elements and strategic role – 7 marks

#### Part (a) — Steps to Implement a COQ System (7 Marks)

Implementing a Cost of Quality system is a structured, multi-step process:

**Step 1 — Top Management Commitment:**
The first and most critical step is securing commitment from top management. Without executive buy-in, the COQ system will not be funded or taken seriously.

**Step 2 — Define Quality Cost Categories:**
Clearly establish the four categories (P, A, IF, EF) and their sub-elements. Customize the categories to fit the organization's products, services, and accounting practices.

**Step 3 — Identify Cost Centers and Cost Accounts:**
Map each quality cost element to a specific cost center (department, line, process). Modify the chart of accounts in the accounting system to capture these costs separately.

**Step 4 — Establish Data Collection Procedures:**
Train personnel on what to record, when, and how. Use standardized forms, ERP modules, or quality management software to capture quality cost data consistently.

**Step 5 — Collect Data and Establish Baselines:**
Run the system for a baseline period (typically 3–6 months) to establish typical cost levels and ratios. This baseline becomes the reference for improvement tracking.

**Step 6 — Analyze and Interpret the Data:**
Compute ratios such as:
- COQ as a percentage of sales
- P, A, IF, EF as a percentage of total COQ
- Cost of quality per unit
- Trend analysis (month-over-month, year-over-year)

**Step 7 — Report Findings to Management:**
Present reports in a format that top management can quickly understand — usually graphical (Pareto charts, trend lines, dashboards).

**Step 8 — Identify Improvement Opportunities:**
Use the data to pinpoint the largest cost buckets. Often, external failure costs and rework costs dominate and become targets for improvement projects.

**Step 9 — Implement Corrective Actions:**
Launch Six Sigma, Kaizen, or other quality improvement initiatives targeting the highest-cost areas.

**Step 10 — Monitor, Review, and Continuously Improve:**
COQ data should be tracked continuously. As prevention investments grow, the balance of costs should shift from failure categories to prevention.

#### Part (b) — Cost Elements and Strategic Role (7 Marks)

**Typical Cost Elements:**

| Category | Typical Cost Elements |
|---|---|
| **Prevention** | Quality planning, training, design reviews, supplier surveys, process capability studies, quality improvement projects |
| **Appraisal** | Material inspection, in-process testing, final product testing, calibration, quality audits, laboratory testing |
| **Internal Failure** | Scrap, rework, reinspection, material review, design changes, downtime, inventory write-offs |
| **External Failure** | Warranty costs, product recalls, returned goods, customer complaints, liability suits, lost goodwill |

**Role of COQ in Strategic Decision-Making:**

1. **Resource Allocation:** COQ data shows where the company is "bleeding money" and where to invest for maximum ROI. For instance, if external failure costs are 60% of COQ, investing heavily in prevention is strategically justified.

2. **Supplier Selection:** COQ metrics from suppliers (incoming inspection failure rates, warranty claims) inform make-or-buy decisions and supplier development priorities.

3. **Pricing Decisions:** Quality cost per unit ($QCPU$) helps in setting competitive yet profitable prices that account for quality investment.

4. **Performance Benchmarking:** Compare COQ as a percentage of sales with industry benchmarks (typically 2.5%–5% for manufacturing, 15%–25% for services).

5. **Justifying Quality Investment:** The most valuable role of COQ is in convincing top management, in **financial terms**, that quality pays. A 2015 ASQ study found that companies with formal COQ programs reported an average **15:1 ROI** on quality investments.

6. **Compliance with ISO 9001:** COQ data supports the ISO 9001:2015 requirement for evidence-based decision-making (Clause 9.1.1).

7. **Customer Retention Strategy:** Reducing external failure costs directly improves customer satisfaction, retention, and lifetime value (CLV).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> - Do **NOT** confuse Prevention with Appraisal. Prevention = *preventing* defects; Appraisal = *finding* defects.
> - Do **NOT** list only examples without classifying them into the four categories — students commonly lose 2–3 marks for this.
> - Do **NOT** assume the COQ curve is symmetrical — the optimum point is **NOT** at 50% quality; it is usually skewed toward higher quality (often 80%–95%).
> - In numerical problems, always **state the units** of quality investment and the final answer.
> - For 14-mark questions, always include a **labeled diagram** of the COQ curve — examiners specifically look for it.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Cost of Quality**

- **COQ Definition:** Total cost of prevention + appraisal + internal failure + external failure.
- **Equation:** $COQ = P + A + IF + EF$
- **P-A-F Model:** Prevention, Appraisal, Failure (Internal + External) — the standard 4-category model.
- **CoC (Conformance Cost):** $P + A$ — money spent to do things right.
- **CNCo (Non-Conformance Cost):** $IF + EF$ — money lost by doing things wrong.
- **COQ Curve:** U-shaped; optimum at $Q^{\*}$ where total cost is minimized.
- **Optimum Condition:** $\dfrac{d\,TC(Q)}{dQ} = 0$, leading to $Q^{*} = \dfrac{1}{\alpha} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{\alpha C_{F0}}{\beta}\right)$.
- **Prevention has the highest leverage:** A rupee in prevention saves many rupees in failure costs.
- **Hidden costs** can be 3–4× the visible COQ.
- **Implementation steps:** 10-step structured process from management commitment to continuous monitoring.
- **Strategic role:** Resource allocation, ROI justification, supplier management, ISO 9001 compliance, customer retention.
- **Industry benchmark:** 2.5%–5% of sales for manufacturers; 15%–25% for service companies.
- **Juran vs Crosby:** Juran finds an economic optimum; Crosby argues for zero defects (no optimum exists).
- **Taguchi's view:** Quality cost should focus on **loss to society** (Taguchi Loss Function), not just the producer's direct costs.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **One-Line KTU Exam Mantra:** "Shift your COQ composition from failure-dominated to prevention-dominated. Prevention is the cheapest quality dollar you will ever spend."
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Customer Satisfaction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET758 - QUALITY ENGINEERING AND MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Customer Satisfaction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

**Customer Satisfaction (CS)** is a measure of how products, services, and overall experience delivered by an organization meet or surpass customer expectations. It represents the degree to which a customer's *post-purchase perceived performance* aligns with their *pre-purchase expectations*.

In the context of Total Quality Management (TQM) and ISO 9001:2015, customer satisfaction is formally defined as:

> *"The customer's perception of the degree to which the customer's requirements have been fulfilled."* — **ISO 9000:2015, Clause 3.9.2**

Mathematically, the foundational relationship is expressed as:

$$\text{Customer Satisfaction (CS)} = f(\text{Perception} - \text{Expectation})$$

Where the outcome falls into one of three critical zones:
- **Positive Satisfaction (Delight):** Perception > Expectation
- **Confirmation (Satisfaction):** Perception = Expectation
- **Negative Satisfaction (Dissatisfaction):** Perception < Expectation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner Insight:** The single most-tested definition in 2018–2024 KTU papers reads — *"Customer satisfaction is the result of comparing the perceived performance of a product/service with the expectations of the customer."* Always quote both ISO and this operational phrasing for full marks.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Restaurant Experience"

Imagine you walk into a restaurant. Before entering, based on Google reviews, the décor photos, and your friend's recommendation, you form an **expectation** that the food will be excellent, the service will be warm, and the ambience will be premium.

After dining:
- If the food is better than expected → **You are delighted.**
- If the food is exactly as expected → **You are satisfied.**
- If the food is worse than expected → **You are dissatisfied.**

This is precisely how every customer evaluates any product or service — be it a smartphone, a hospital visit, an engineering consultancy, or a college course. The gap between *what was promised* and *what was delivered* determines satisfaction.

> [!NOTE]
> **Satisfaction vs. Quality — A Critical Distinction:**
> - **Quality** is *supplier-side* (conformance to specifications).
> - **Satisfaction** is *customer-side* (perceived value against expectations).
> A product can be of high quality (zero defects) yet yield low satisfaction if customer expectations were set higher.

---

## 1.3 Key Vocabulary You Must Know for KTU

| Term | Definition | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Expectation** | Anticipated performance prior to consumption | Advertisement claims, brand reputation |
| **Perception** | Actual performance as experienced | Actual product received |
| **Voice of Customer (VOC)** | The explicit and implicit needs/requirements stated by the customer | Surveys, complaints, reviews |
| **Delight** | When perception significantly exceeds expectation | Surprise gift, exceptional service |
| **Loyalty** | Repeated purchase driven by satisfaction and trust | Brand advocates |
| **Net Promoter Score (NPS)** | Likelihood of customer recommending product/service | Scale 0–10 |

> [!TIP]
> In every KTU answer, define **at least 3 key terms** (Expectation, Perception, VOC) and draw a small diagram. Examiners reward structural answers under the 2024 Scheme OBE rubric.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Pillars of Customer Satisfaction

Customer Satisfaction theory rests on **four foundational pillars** that KTU examiners repeatedly test:

### Pillar 1: The Expectancy-Disconfirmation Paradigm (Oliver, 1980)
This is the bedrock model. The customer forms an expectation **E** before purchase. After consumption, they perceive performance **P**. The disconfirmation is the difference (P − E).

- If **P > E** → *Positive Disconfirmation* → Satisfaction/Delight
- If **P = E** → *Simple Confirmation* → Satisfaction
- If **P < E** → *Negative Disconfirmation* → Dissatisfaction

### Pillar 2: SERVQUAL Model (Parasuraman, Zeithaml, Berry — 1988)
A service-quality framework identifying **5 dimensions** that determine satisfaction:

1. **Tangibles** — Physical facilities, equipment, personnel appearance.
2. **Reliability** — Ability to perform the promised service dependably and accurately.
3. **Responsiveness** — Willingness to help customers and provide prompt service.
4. **Assurance** — Knowledge and courtesy of employees; ability to inspire trust.
5. **Empathy** — Caring, individualized attention given to customers.

### Pillar 3: Kano Model (Noriaki Kano — 1984)
Customer requirements are classified into three categories:

- **Must-Be (Basic) Attributes:** Absence causes extreme dissatisfaction; presence does not increase satisfaction. *(e.g., ATM must dispense cash)*
- **One-Dimensional (Performance) Attributes:** Satisfaction is linearly proportional to fulfillment. *(e.g., fuel efficiency in cars)*
- **Attractive (Excitement) Attributes:** Presence causes delight; absence does not cause dissatisfaction. *(e.g., free Wi-Fi on a budget bus)*

### Pillar 4: The 5 Gaps Model of Service Quality
A modified, expanded version where service quality shortfalls arise from **5 specific gaps** between management perception and customer expectation.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet & Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they appear in numerical problems and 14-mark derivations.**

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Variables & Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Satisfaction Score (Basic) | $\text{CS} = P - E$ | P = Perception, E = Expectation (both on Likert 1–5 or 1–7 scale) |
| 2 | SERVQUAL Quality Score | $\text{SQ} = \sum_{i=1}^{5} (P_i - E_i)$ | i = each of the 5 dimensions |
| 3 | Mean SERVQUAL Score | $\overline{\text{SQ}} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} (P_i - E_i)$ | n = number of respondents |
| 4 | Net Promoter Score | $\text{NPS} = \% \text{Promoters} - \% \text{Detractors}$ | Scale 0–10; Promoters: 9–10 |
| 5 | Customer Satisfaction Index (ACSI) | $\text{ACSI} = f(\text{Exp},\ \text{PQ},\ \text{Val},\ \text{Comp},\ \text{Loy})$ | Multi-variable structural model |
| 6 | Kano Category Weightage | $\text{Category Coefficient} = \frac{(A+O)}{(A+O+M+I)}$ | A=Attractive, O=One-dim, M=Must-be, I=Indifferent |
| 7 | Loyalty Rate | $\text{Loyalty \%} = \frac{\text{Repeat Customers}}{\text{Total Customers}} \times 100$ | Percentage |
| 8 | Complaint Resolution Rate | $\text{CRR \%} = \frac{\text{Resolved}}{\text{Received}} \times 100$ | Percentage |

> [!WARNING]
> **LaTeX Pipeline Reminder:** Use `\vert` or `\mid` instead of `\vert` for absolute value within tables to avoid markdown table breakage. Example: `\vert x \vert` (NOT `\vert x \vert`).

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry | Application of Customer Satisfaction Models |
|---|---|
| **Automotive (Tata, Toyota)** | SERVQUAL applied at dealerships; ACSI benchmarking against global rivals |
| **IT Services (TCS, Infosys)** | CSAT scores on every project; NPS tracked per client account |
| **Healthcare** | Kano model to identify basic vs. attractive service attributes in hospitals |
| **Banking** | ACSI score for public-sector vs. private-sector banks |
| **Manufacturing** | Voice of Customer drives QFD (House of Quality) in new product development |

---

## 2.4 The 5 Gaps of Service Quality (Detailed)

| Gap | Description | Cause |
|---|---|---|
| **Gap 1** | Consumer Expectation vs. Management Perception | Management does not know what customers expect |
| **Gap 2** | Management Perception vs. Service Quality Specification | Wrong standards set despite knowing expectations |
| **Gap 3** | Service Quality Specification vs. Service Delivery | Actual performance falls short of specifications |
| **Gap 4** | Service Delivery vs. External Communication | Promises made in ads exceed actual delivery |
| **Gap 5** | Expected Service vs. Perceived Service | The *resultant* gap — customer dissatisfaction itself |

$$\boxed{\text{Gap 5} = f(\text{Gap 1}, \text{Gap 2}, \text{Gap 3}, \text{Gap 4})}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Favourite Question:** *"Explain the 5-gap model of service quality with a neat diagram."* — This has appeared **every year** since 2019 in Part B. Memorize the gaps with examples (e.g., Gap 4 = misleading advertisement).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Disconfirmation Paradigm to Satisfaction Function

The theoretical foundation begins with Richard Oliver's (1980) Expectancy-Disconfirmation Theory. We will mathematically derive the satisfaction function step-by-step.

### Step 1: Define Pre-Consumption Expectation

Let $E_i$ represent the expectation of customer $i$ for a given attribute on a Likert scale (1 to 7, where 1 = "very poor" and 7 = "excellent"). The customer's expectation is built from prior experience, marketing communication, word-of-mouth, and personal needs.

$$E_i = f(\text{Past Experience},\ \text{Marketing Claims},\ \text{WOM},\ \text{Personal Needs})$$

### Step 2: Define Post-Consumption Perception

Let $P_i$ represent the customer's perceived performance of attribute $i$ after actual use, measured on the same Likert scale.

$$P_i = f(\text{Actual Quality},\ \text{Service Encounter},\ \text{Context},\ \text{Situation})$$

### Step 3: Compute Disconfirmation

The disconfirmation $D_i$ is the algebraic difference between perception and expectation:

$$D_i = P_i - E_i$$

### Step 4: Relate Disconfirmation to Satisfaction

Customer satisfaction is a direct linear function of disconfirmation (in the simplest form):

$$\text{CS}_i = \beta_0 + \beta_1 \cdot D_i + \varepsilon_i$$

Where:
- $\beta_0$ = baseline satisfaction constant
- $\beta_1$ = sensitivity coefficient (how much satisfaction changes per unit change in disconfirmation)
- $\varepsilon_i$ = random error term

For the standard KTU exam, we simplify to:

$$\boxed{\text{CS}_i = P_i - E_i}$$

### Step 5: Aggregate Across Attributes

For multiple attributes (say, the 5 SERVQUAL dimensions), aggregate using the weighted mean:

$$\text{CS}_{\text{overall}} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot (P_i - E_i)}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i}$$

Where $w_i$ is the importance weight assigned by the customer to attribute $i$.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Problem: SERVQUAL Gap Analysis

> **Problem:** A telecom company surveyed 100 customers on 5 service dimensions. Mean perception (P) and expectation (E) scores (1–7 scale) are given below. Compute (a) Mean SERVQUAL gap for each dimension, (b) Overall service quality score.

| Dimension | Perception (P) | Expectation (E) | Importance Weight (w) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Tangibles | 5.2 | 6.4 | 0.15 |
| Reliability | 4.8 | 6.5 | 0.30 |
| Responsiveness | 3.9 | 6.2 | 0.25 |
| Assurance | 5.5 | 6.3 | 0.15 |
| Empathy | 4.2 | 6.0 | 0.15 |

### Solution:

**Step 1: Compute gap for each dimension.**

$$\text{Gap}_T = P_T - E_T = 5.2 - 6.4 = -1.2$$

$$\text{Gap}_R = P_R - E_R = 4.8 - 6.5 = -1.7$$

$$\text{Gap}_{\text{Res}} = P_{\text{Res}} - E_{\text{Res}} = 3.9 - 6.2 = -2.3$$

$$\text{Gap}_A = P_A - E_A = 5.5 - 6.3 = -0.8$$

$$\text{Gap}_E = P_E - E_E = 4.2 - 6.0 = -1.8$$

**Step 2: Identify critical dimensions.**

The largest negative gap is **Responsiveness (−2.3)** → biggest service shortfall.

**Step 3: Compute weighted overall SERVQUAL score.**

$$\text{SQ}_{\text{overall}} = \sum_{i=1}^{5} w_i \cdot (P_i - E_i)$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{SQ}_{\text{overall}} &= (0.15)(-1.2) + (0.30)(-1.7) + (0.25)(-2.3) \\
&\quad + (0.15)(-0.8) + (0.15)(-1.8)
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{SQ}_{\text{overall}} &= -0.18 + (-0.51) + (-0.575) + (-0.12) + (-0.27) \\
&= -1.655
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4: Interpretation.**

Since $\text{SQ}_{\text{overall}} = -1.655$ is significantly negative, service quality is below expectation. The company must prioritize **Responsiveness** (call-center wait time, complaint resolution speed) and **Empathy** (personalized customer care).

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Kano Category Coefficient

The Kano Category Coefficient is derived to classify each feature objectively.

$$\text{Coefficient} = \frac{(A + O)}{(A + O + M + I)}$$

Where:
- A = number of Attractive responses
- O = number of One-dimensional responses
- M = number of Must-be responses
- I = number of Indifferent responses

- If Coefficient $\rightarrow$ **close to 1** → Attractive
- If Coefficient $\rightarrow$ **close to 0** → Must-be / Indifferent

**Example:** A survey of 50 customers for feature "Auto-park in cars":
- A = 28, O = 12, M = 5, I = 5

$$\text{Coefficient} = \frac{28 + 12}{28 + 12 + 5 + 5} = \frac{40}{50} = 0.80$$

→ Strongly **Attractive** — delight feature. Car-makers should market this aggressively.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Customer Loyalty Cascade

Satisfied customers → repeat purchase → word-of-mouth referrals → reduced acquisition cost → increased Customer Lifetime Value (CLV).

$$\text{CLV} = \sum_{t=0}^{T} \frac{(R_t - C_t) \cdot r}{(1 + d)^t}$$

Where:
- $R_t$ = revenue from customer in period $t$
- $C_t$ = cost of servicing customer in period $t$
- $r$ = retention probability (driven by satisfaction)
- $d$ = discount rate
- $T$ = customer lifetime horizon

> [!TIP]
> A **5% increase in customer retention** can boost profits by **25–95%** (Bain & Co. research). This is the strategic argument KTU examiners love for 14-mark questions linking satisfaction → loyalty → profitability.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1: The Expectancy-Disconfirmation Framework

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Customer Forms Expectation E] --> B{Service Consumption}
    B --> C[Customer Perceives Performance P]
    C --> D{P versus E Comparison}
    D -->|P greater than E| E[Positive Disconfirmation]
    D -->|P equals E| F[Confirmation]
    D -->|P less than E| G[Negative Disconfirmation]
    E --> H[Delight and Satisfaction]
    F --> I[Satisfaction]
    G --> J[Dissatisfaction and Churn Risk]
    H --> K[Loyalty and Advocacy]
    I --> K
    J --> L[Service Recovery Required]
    L --> M[Restored Satisfaction]
    M --> K
```

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2: The 5 Gaps Model of Service Quality

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Customer Expectations] -->|Gap 1| B[Management Perception]
    B -->|Gap 2| C[Service Quality Standards]
    C -->|Gap 3| D[Service Delivery]
    D -->|Gap 4| E[External Communications]
    A -.->|Gap 5| F[Customer Perceived Service]
    E -.->|Influences| A
    F --> G[Dissatisfaction if Gap 5 negative]
    F --> H[Satisfaction if Gap 5 zero or positive]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Gaps 1–4 are internal organizational gaps.** **Gap 5 is the customer-experienced gap** and is the *sum effect* of Gaps 1–4. Always draw Gap 5 as a dashed arrow spanning from Expectation to Perception — this is how KTU examiners expect it.

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3: SERVQUAL Dimensions Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R[Service Quality SQ] --> T[Tangibles Physical Evidence]
    R --> REL[Reliability Dependability]
    R --> RES[Responsiveness Promptness]
    R --> ASS[Assurance Trust and Confidence]
    R --> EMP[Empathy Personalized Care]
    T --> TS[Facilities Equipment Personnel]
    REL --> RS[Accurate Timely Service]
    RES --> RS2[Quick Response Willingness]
    ASS --> TS2[Competence Courtesy]
    EMP --> TS3[Understanding Customer Needs]
```

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4: Kano Model Categorization Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    K[Customer Requirement X] --> Q1{Is X present?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| Q2{Does customer like it?}
    Q1 -->|No| Q3{Does customer dislike absence?}
    Q2 -->|Yes| A1[Attractive Feature Delight]
    Q2 -->|No| Q4{Must-be One-dim or Indifferent?}
    Q3 -->|Yes| M1[Must-be Feature Basic Necessity]
    Q3 -->|No| Q5{Satisfied with absence?}
    Q4 -->|Linear satisfaction| O1[One-dimensional Feature Performance]
    Q4 -->|No change| I1[Indifferent Feature]
    Q5 -->|Yes| I1
    Q5 -->|No| A1
```

---

## 4.5 Mermaid Diagram 5: Customer Satisfaction Measurement Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Identify Customer Segments] --> S2[Choose Measurement Tool CSAT NPS CES]
    S2 --> S3[Design Questionnaire Likert Scale]
    S3 --> S4[Collect Data Sample size n]
    S4 --> S5[Compute Mean Scores and Gaps]
    S5 --> S6[Statistical Analysis t-test Regression]
    S6 --> S7[Identify Root Causes Fishbone Pareto]
    S7 --> S8[Implement Improvements PDCA Cycle]
    S8 --> S9[Close the Loop Feedback to Customer]
    S9 --> S10[Monitor Trends Continuous Improvement]
```

---

## 4.6 Block-Level Architecture: Linking CS to Business Outcomes

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Customer Satisfaction High] --> B[Customer Retention Increases]
    A --> C[Word of Mouth Referrals]
    A --> D[Lower Price Sensitivity]
    B --> E[Reduced Acquisition Cost]
    C --> E
    D --> F[Higher Margins]
    E --> G[Increased Customer Lifetime Value]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Higher Profitability]
    H --> I[Business Sustainability]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**

### Q1. Define Customer Satisfaction. State any two differences between Customer Satisfaction and Service Quality.

**Model Answer:**
Customer Satisfaction is the customer's perception of the degree to which their requirements have been fulfilled (ISO 9000:2015). It is a post-purchase evaluation comparing perceived performance (P) with prior expectation (E).

**Two Differences:**

| Parameter | Customer Satisfaction | Service Quality |
|---|---|---|
| Focus | Transaction-specific evaluation | Overall long-term assessment |
| Orientation | Customer-centric, post-purchase | Provider-centric, process-based |
| Measurement | Per outcome encounter | Across all 5 SERVQUAL dimensions |

*(3 marks: Definition 1 + 2 differences 1 each)*

---

### Q2. List and briefly explain the five dimensions of the SERVQUAL model.

**Model Answer:**

1. **Tangibles** — Physical facilities, equipment, and personnel appearance.
2. **Reliability** — Ability to perform the promised service dependably and accurately.
3. **Responsiveness** — Willingness to help customers and provide prompt service.
4. **Assurance** — Knowledge and courtesy of employees, and their ability to inspire trust and confidence.
5. **Empathy** — Caring, individualized attention provided to customers.

*(3 marks: 5 dimensions × 0.6 each = 3)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice Pattern

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified Pattern]**

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**Q. (a)** Explain the Expectancy-Disconfirmation Theory of Customer Satisfaction with a suitable diagram. How does it explain the concept of customer delight? **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Origin and Premise (2 marks):**
The Expectancy-Disconfirmation Theory was proposed by Richard Oliver (1980). It states that customer satisfaction is determined by the difference between **pre-purchase expectation (E)** and **post-purchase perceived performance (P)**.

**2. Mathematical Expression (2 marks):**

$$\text{CS} = P - E$$

- If $P > E$ → **Positive Disconfirmation** → Satisfaction
- If $P = E$ → **Simple Confirmation** → Satisfaction
- If $P < E$ → **Negative Disconfirmation** → Dissatisfaction

**3. Explanation with Diagram (2 marks):**

```
  Satisfaction Level
        ^
        |        ____ Positive Disconfirmation (Delight)
        |       /
        |      /
        |-----/---------- Zero Disconfirmation (Satisfied)
        |    /
        |   /
        |  / Negative Disconfirmation (Dissatisfied)
        |_____________________________> Perceived Performance (P)
                  E (Expectation)
```

**4. Customer Delight (1 mark):**
Customer delight occurs when $P \gg E$ (perception far exceeds expectation). Delight transforms satisfaction into loyalty, advocacy, and emotional attachment. It is a strategic tool for competitive differentiation.

---

**Q. (b)** Explain the SERVQUAL model with its five dimensions. Describe how a hotel industry can apply SERVQUAL to improve guest satisfaction. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**1. SERVQUAL Framework (3 marks):**
SERVQUAL (Service Quality) was developed by Parasuraman, Zeithaml, and Berry (1988). It is built on the gap between customer expectation (E) and perception (P) of service across 5 dimensions.

$$\text{SQ} = \sum_{i=1}^{5} (P_i - E_i)$$

**Five Dimensions:**
1. **Tangibles** — Modern rooms, clean lobby, well-dressed staff.
2. **Reliability** — Room ready on time, billing accuracy, consistent service.
3. **Responsiveness** — Quick check-in, prompt room service, fast complaint resolution.
4. **Assurance** — Trained staff, secure handling of valuables, knowledgeable concierge.
5. **Empathy** — Remembering guest preferences, personalized greetings, special occasion arrangements.

**2. Hotel Industry Application (4 marks):**

| Dimension | Hotel Implementation Strategy |
|---|---|
| Tangibles | Renovate rooms every 5 years, smart lighting, premium linen |
| Reliability | 100% check-in guarantee in 5 minutes, no overbooking |
| Responsiveness | 24×7 concierge, AI chatbot for instant queries, complaint resolution < 30 min |
| Assurance | Staff trained in hospitality, security audits, foreign-language fluency |
| Empathy | Loyalty program recognition, customized welcome amenities |

**3. Outcome (included for completeness):**
Regular SERVQUAL surveys → Identify largest negative gap → Targeted intervention → Improved CSAT scores → Higher RevPAR (Revenue Per Available Room).

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**Q. (a)** Explain the Kano Model of customer satisfaction. Classify the following features of a smartphone using the Kano framework: (i) Long battery life, (ii) Fingerprint sensor, (iii) Voice assistant, (iv) Phone call function, (v) Wireless charging. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Kano Model Theory (3 marks):**
The Kano Model (Noriaki Kano, 1984) classifies customer preferences into 5 categories:
- **Must-Be (M):** Basic requirements; absence causes extreme dissatisfaction.
- **One-Dimensional (O):** Satisfaction is proportional to performance.
- **Attractive (A):** Excitement features that create delight.
- **Indifferent (I):** Customer does not care either way.
- **Reverse (R):** Some customers dislike the feature.

**2. Smartphone Feature Classification (4 marks):**

| Feature | Kano Category | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Phone call function | **Must-Be (M)** | Basic purpose; absence = total dissatisfaction |
| Long battery life | **One-Dimensional (O)** | More battery → more satisfaction, linearly |
| Fingerprint sensor | **One-Dimensional (O)** | Faster unlocking → higher satisfaction |
| Voice assistant | **Attractive (A)** | Adds wow factor but absence is tolerable |
| Wireless charging | **Attractive (A)** | Convenience delight; absence causes no dissatisfaction |

**Strategic Implication:** Marketers should highlight Attractive features (voice assistant, wireless charging) for differentiation, while ensuring all Must-Be and One-Dimensional features meet/exceed baseline.

---

**Q. (b)** With a neat diagram, explain the 5-gap model of service quality. Discuss how Gap 4 (communication gap) can be eliminated in an engineering services company. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**1. The 5 Gaps Model (3 marks):**

$$\text{Gap 5} = f(\text{Gap 1},\ \text{Gap 2},\ \text{Gap 3},\ \text{Gap 4})$$

| Gap | Definition | Cause |
|---|---|---|
| Gap 1 | Customer Expectation vs. Management Perception | Insufficient market research |
| Gap 2 | Management Perception vs. Service Specification | Inadequate standards |
| Gap 3 | Service Specification vs. Service Delivery | Employee training shortfall |
| Gap 4 | Service Delivery vs. External Communication | Over-promising in ads |
| Gap 5 | Expected vs. Perceived Service | Net result of all above gaps |

**2. Diagram (2 marks):**
*(Draw horizontal arrows showing Gaps 1–4 sequentially, with Gap 5 as a dashed overarching arrow from Expectation to Perception)*

**3. Eliminating Gap 4 in Engineering Services (2 marks):**

Strategies:
- **Audit marketing claims** against actual delivery capability before publishing.
- **Train sales engineers** to set realistic client expectations.
- **Establish Service Level Agreements (SLAs)** that bind communication to operational truth.
- **Implement feedback loop** between marketing and delivery teams.
- **Conduct quarterly promise-vs-delivery audits** and penalize over-promising.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Mistakes in Customer Satisfaction Answers:**
>
> 1. **Writing "Customer Satisfaction = Customer Happiness"** — This is wrong. Happiness is emotional; Satisfaction is cognitive-evaluative. Deduct 0.5 marks.
>
> 2. **Missing the equation $P - E$** in disconfirmation answers — Always include the formula; 1-mark reserved.
>
> 3. **Listing SERVQUAL dimensions without explaining the gap concept** — Examiners want both: dimensions *and* the perception-minus-expectation framework.
>
> 4. **Drawing the 5-Gaps diagram incorrectly** — Gap 5 must span *between* Customer Expectation and Customer Perception. Drawing it inside the organization loses 2 marks.
>
> 5. **Forgetting to give real-time examples** — KTU 2024 Scheme rewards application-level answers (e.g., "Reliability in Zomato = on-time delivery"). At least 1 example per 7-mark sub-question earns a half-mark bonus.
>
> 6. **Confusing ACSI with CSAT** — ACSI is macro-economic (national index); CSAT is transactional (single encounter). This is a favourite trap question.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Customer Satisfaction (KTU Module 4)**

- ✅ Customer Satisfaction = Perceived Performance − Expectation (Oliver, 1980)
- ✅ Three outcomes: **Delight (P > E)**, **Confirmation (P = E)**, **Dissatisfaction (P < E)**
- ✅ SERVQUAL has **5 dimensions**: Tangibles, Reliability, Responsiveness, Assurance, Empathy (mnemonic: **T-R-R-A-E**)
- ✅ SERVQUAL score formula: $\text{SQ} = \sum (P_i - E_i)$; negative = poor quality
- ✅ Kano Model categories: **Must-be, One-dimensional, Attractive** (+ Indifferent, Reverse)
- ✅ 5 Gaps Model — Gap 5 is the *customer-experienced* gap; Gaps 1–4 are organizational
- ✅ Gap 4 (Communication) — Over-promising in ads causes unmet expectations
- ✅ NPS = % Promoters − % Detractors; ranges from −100 to +100
- ✅ ACSI measures national-level satisfaction across 10 economic sectors
- ✅ ISO 9000:2015 definition: *"customer's perception of the degree to which requirements have been fulfilled"*
- ✅ Quality ≠ Satisfaction: Quality is supplier-side; Satisfaction is customer-side
- ✅ Satisfied customers → Retention → Lower CAC → Higher CLV → Profitability
- ✅ Voice of Customer (VOC) is the *primary input* to QFD (House of Quality)
- ✅ For numerical problems: always show the **gap calculation per dimension** and the **weighted aggregation**
- ✅ Always include **at least one diagram** (5-Gaps, Kano, or SERVQUAL) for 14-mark questions
- ✅ KTU frequently asks: "Explain SERVQUAL / 5-Gaps with example" — memorize both
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Quality Function Deployment (QFD)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET758 - QUALITY ENGINEERING AND MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Quality Function Deployment (QFD)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quality Function Deployment (QFD)** is a structured, planning and product-development methodology that captures the **Voice of the Customer (VOC)** and translates customer requirements (the **"Whats"**) into specific, measurable, and actionable technical engineering characteristics (the **"Hows"**) at every stage of product or service development. It was originally developed in Japan by **Dr. Yoji Akao** and **Shigeru Mizuno** in the late 1960s and later refined through extensive use at the **Toyota Motor Company** and the Kobe Shipyard of Mitsubishi Heavy Industries.

The central visual and analytical tool of QFD is the **House of Quality (HoQ)**, a matrix-style planning chart that interrelates customer desires, competitive benchmarking, technical features, and engineering trade-offs into a single integrated document.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Restaurant Translator"**
>
> Imagine you walk into a restaurant and tell the chef: *"I want something really tasty, filling, not too heavy, and budget-friendly."* The chef must now **translate** your vague feelings into measurable kitchen actions: grams of protein, calories, cooking temperature, ingredient cost per plate, and plating time. The chef essentially builds a hidden "translation matrix" between your wishes (Whats) and his kitchen operations (Hows).
>
> **QFD does exactly this for engineers.** It is the formal translator between what the customer feels ("I want a car that feels safe and stylish") and what the engineering team must build ("tensile strength ≥ 350 MPa, paint gloss index ≥ 90 GU"). Without QFD, the engineer guesses; with QFD, the engineering target is mathematically derived from the customer's stated priority.

### 1.3 Key Vocabulary (KTU Board-Exam Vocabulary List)

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Voice of Customer (VOC)** | Raw statements, complaints, and desires captured directly from end users. |
| **Whats** | The customer requirements (CRs), expressed in the customer's own language. |
| **Hows** | The technical/engineering requirements (ERs — Engineering Requirements) that fulfill the Whats. |
| **House of Quality (HoQ)** | The primary matrix diagram that visually integrates all QFD information. |
| **Relationship Matrix** | The central matrix inside HoQ that correlates Whats (rows) with Hows (columns). |
| **Roof of the House** | The triangular matrix showing correlations (positive or negative) between the Hows themselves. |
| **Technical Importance Rating** | The weighted engineering priority derived from customer importance and the relationship matrix. |
| **Absolute Weight** | The raw weighted score before normalization. |
| **Relative Weight** | The percentage priority given to each Hows after normalization to 100% or a unit scale. |
| **Benchmarking** | Comparing your product against competitors' performance for both customer perceptions and engineering metrics. |
| **Target Value** | The numerical specification for a Hows, set using customer competitive analysis and engineering capability. |

### 1.4 Standard Symbols Used in the Relationship Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> In the House of Quality, the following standardized symbols (originating from Akao, 1972) denote the strength of relationship between a customer requirement (Whats) and an engineering requirement (Hows):
> - **Strong relationship** = **●** (a filled solid circle, typically weighted **9 points**)
> - **Moderate relationship** = **○** (an empty circle, typically weighted **3 points**)
> - **Weak relationship** = **△** (a triangle, typically weighted **1 point**)
> - **No relationship** = left blank, scored **0 points**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Four Phases of QFD (Akao's Original Model)

Quality Function Deployment is not a single matrix; it is a **sequence of four cascading matrices** often called the **"Four-Phase QFD Model."** Each phase deploys the Hows of one house as the Whats of the next house down the product development stream.

1. **Phase 1 — Product Planning (House of Quality):** Translates customer requirements into product technical specifications.
2. **Phase 2 — Product Design (Deployment to Parts):** Translates product specs into **piece-part** or **component** characteristics.
3. **Phase 3 — Process Planning (Deployment to Operations):** Translates piece-part characteristics into **manufacturing process** operations.
4. **Phase 4 — Production Planning (Deployment to Controls):** Translates process operations into **production controls, tooling, and quality procedures.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Focus:** In a 14-mark Module 4 question, the examiner almost always expects students to construct **Phase 1 (the House of Quality)** and explain the meaning of at least 5 rooms of the house.

### 2.2 Anatomy of the House of Quality (The 7 Rooms)

The House of Quality is conceptually divided into **seven interconnected rooms**. KTU examiners frequently test students on identifying each room and its content.

| Room # | Location in HoQ | Content | Engineering Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1** | Left Wall | **Whats** — Customer Requirements (CRs) with Importance Ratings (1–5 scale) | Captures VOC. |
| **2** | Ceiling | **Hows** — Technical Requirements (Engineering Metrics) | Lists measurable engineering responses. |
| **3** | Center | **Relationship Matrix** (●, ○, △) | Links Whats ↔ Hows mathematically. |
| **4** | Roof | **Correlation Matrix** between Hows (Strong positive, Strong negative, etc.) | Identifies engineering trade-offs/synergies. |
| **5** | Right Wall | **Customer Competitive Analysis** — How customers rate your product vs. competitors on each What | Strategic positioning. |
| **6** | Basement | **Technical Competitive Analysis** — How your product vs. competitors perform on each How | Engineering benchmarking. |
| **7** | Foundation | **Target Values, Absolute Weight, Relative Weight, and Technical Importance** | The final engineering action plan. |

### 2.3 The Core Logic: Why and How QFD Works

**Why QFD Exists:**
- Traditional product development is engineering-driven ("we'll build what we can"), which often produces technically excellent products that **fail commercially** because they ignore latent customer needs.
- QFD forces a **customer-in / engineering-out** logic, reducing rework, warranty costs, and time-to-market.

**How the math of QFD works:**

- **Step A:** Collect customer requirements (Whats). Survey customers and ask them to rate the *importance* of each What on a 1–5 ordinal scale.
- **Step B:** Convert importance ratings into **Raw Weights** by multiplying the customer rating by the **sales point** (1 for non-safety features, 1.2 or 1.5 for safety/legal features — a multiplier that boosts critical requirements).
- **Step C:** Build the relationship matrix. Engineers assign ●, ○, △, or blank to each (What × How) cell.
- **Step D:** Compute the **Absolute Importance** of each How by summing the products of importance × relationship weight across all Whats.
- **Step E:** Normalize into **Relative Importance (%).** The How with the highest relative weight becomes the engineering team's top priority.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!NOTE]
> The following table contains every formula a KTU student needs to solve a House of Quality problem. Memorize these — they appear in 14-mark questions almost every year.

| # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Expression | Unit / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Raw Customer Importance** | $I_i \in \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5\}$ | Ordinal scale: 1 = lowest, 5 = highest. |
| 2 | **Adjusted Importance (with Sales Point)** | $W_i = I_i \times S_i$ | Where $S_i \in \{1.0,\ 1.2,\ 1.5\}$ is the **sales/strategic point**. |
| 3 | **Relationship Weight** | $r_{ij} \in \{0,\ 1,\ 3,\ 9\}$ | Maps symbol △ = 1, ○ = 3, ● = 9. |
| 4 | **Absolute Importance of How *j*** | $A_j = \displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{n} W_i \cdot r_{ij}$ | Summation over all Whats. |
| 5 | **Relative Importance of How *j*** | $R_j = \dfrac{A_j}{\displaystyle\sum_{j=1}^{m} A_j} \times 100\%$ | Expressed as a **percentage**, $\sum R_j = 100\%$. |
| 6 | **Normalized Row Sum** | $N_i = \dfrac{W_i \cdot r_{ij}}{\displaystyle\sum_{i} W_i \cdot r_{ij}}$ | Used in advanced matrix normalization. |
| 7 | **Degree of Difficulty / Difficulty Score** | $D_j \in \{1.0,\ 3.0,\ 9.0\}$ | The engineer's subjective difficulty estimate (low/medium/high). |
| 8 | **Priority Ranking (Final Score)** | $P_j = \dfrac{A_j}{D_j}$ | Dividing importance by difficulty prioritizes **achievable** high-impact Hows. |
| 9 | **Total Degrees of Freedom Constraint** | $\displaystyle\sum_{j=1}^{m} R_j = 1.0$ (or $100\%$) | Sanity check — must equal 1.0. |
| 10 | **Inter-Hows Correlation (Roof Cell)** | $C_{jk} \in \{\text{Strong +},\ \text{Strong -},\ \text{Weak +},\ \text{Weak -},\ \text{None}\}$ | Trade-off identification. |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

QFD is not merely an academic exercise; it is a frontline engineering tool used in:

- **Automotive Industry:** Toyota, Ford, Tata Motors — designing new vehicles by capturing regional customer priorities (fuel efficiency vs. comfort vs. safety).
- **Software Industry:** Microsoft and IBM have used QFD variants (called "User Story Mapping") to prioritize feature releases.
- **Healthcare:** Hospitals use QFD-style matrices to translate patient satisfaction surveys into clinical process improvements.
- **Construction:** Translating client requirements into building specifications (load capacity, thermal insulation, acoustic ratings).
- **Service Sector:** Banks and airlines use **Servqual-based QFD** to translate customer expectations into staff training and process design.

### 2.6 Symbols Used in the Roof Matrix (How-vs-How Correlations)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Strong Positive Correlation (++):** Both Hows improve together — pursue jointly.
> **Strong Negative Correlation (--):** Improving one worsens the other — engineering trade-off decision required.
> **Weak Positive / Negative:** Marginal interactions.
> **Blank:** No significant interaction.

This roof matrix is what makes QFD genuinely powerful — it forces the engineering team to **acknowledge trade-offs explicitly** rather than discovering them during prototyping.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Numerical Example — Building a House of Quality

To make the math of QFD absolutely clear, we will solve a complete worked example, the type KTU examiners love to set as a 14-mark question.

> **Scenario:** A consumer-electronics company is designing a **new microwave oven**. After market research, the engineering team has identified the following data.

**Step 1 — Define the Whats (Customer Requirements) with Importance and Sales Point.**

| What # | Customer Requirement (CR) | Importance $I_i$ | Sales Point $S_i$ | Adjusted Importance $W_i = I_i \cdot S_i$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| CR1 | Low price | 5 | 1.0 | 5 |
| CR2 | Even cooking | 4 | 1.0 | 4 |
| CR3 | Easy to clean | 3 | 1.0 | 3 |
| CR4 | Long warranty | 3 | 1.2 | 3.6 |
| CR5 | Quiet operation | 2 | 1.0 | 2 |
| | **Total** | | | **17.6** |

**Step 2 — Define the Hows (Technical Requirements).**

Let us choose four engineering Hows:
- **HR1:** Magnetron power output (Watts)
- **HR2:** Interior surface roughness / coating quality (Ra in µm)
- **HR3:** Noise level (dB)
- **HR4:** Warranty period (months)

**Step 3 — Build the Relationship Matrix (Customer × Technical).**

Engineering judgment assigns the symbols. Below we use the **r-weights** ($\triangle = 1,\ \bigcirc = 3,\ \bullet = 9,\ \text{blank} = 0$).

| What ↓ \ How → | HR1 (Watts) | HR2 (Ra) | HR3 (dB) | HR4 (Months) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| CR1 (Price) | 3 | 1 | 0 | 0 |
| CR2 (Even cooking) | 9 | 3 | 0 | 0 |
| CR3 (Easy clean) | 0 | 9 | 0 | 0 |
| CR4 (Warranty) | 1 | 0 | 0 | 9 |
| CR5 (Quiet) | 0 | 0 | 9 | 0 |

**Step 4 — Compute the Absolute Importance $A_j$ for each How.**

We apply the formula:

$$
A_j = \sum_{i=1}^{n} W_i \cdot r_{ij}
$$

**For HR1 (Wattage):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{\text{HR1}} &= (W_{CR1} \cdot r_{11}) + (W_{CR2} \cdot r_{21}) + (W_{CR3} \cdot r_{31}) + (W_{CR4} \cdot r_{41}) + (W_{CR5} \cdot r_{51}) \\
&= (5 \cdot 3) + (4 \cdot 9) + (3 \cdot 0) + (3.6 \cdot 1) + (2 \cdot 0) \\
&= 15 + 36 + 0 + 3.6 + 0 \\
&= 54.6
\end{aligned}
$$

**For HR2 (Surface Roughness):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{\text{HR2}} &= (5 \cdot 1) + (4 \cdot 3) + (3 \cdot 9) + (3.6 \cdot 0) + (2 \cdot 0) \\
&= 5 + 12 + 27 + 0 + 0 \\
&= 44.0
\end{aligned}
$$

**For HR3 (Noise Level):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{\text{HR3}} &= (5 \cdot 0) + (4 \cdot 0) + (3 \cdot 0) + (3.6 \cdot 0) + (2 \cdot 9) \\
&= 0 + 0 + 0 + 0 + 18 \\
&= 18.0
\end{aligned}
$$

**For HR4 (Warranty Period):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{\text{HR4}} &= (5 \cdot 0) + (4 \cdot 0) + (3 \cdot 0) + (3.6 \cdot 9) + (2 \cdot 0) \\
&= 0 + 0 + 0 + 32.4 + 0 \\
&= 32.4
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the Total Absolute Weight.**

$$
\sum_{j=1}^{4} A_j = 54.6 + 44.0 + 18.0 + 32.4 = 149.0
$$

**Step 6 — Compute the Relative Importance $R_j$ (Percentage) for each How.**

$$
R_j = \frac{A_j}{\sum A_j} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{\text{HR1}} &= \frac{54.6}{149.0} \times 100\% \approx 36.64\% \\
R_{\text{HR2}} &= \frac{44.0}{149.0} \times 100\% \approx 29.53\% \\
R_{\text{HR3}} &= \frac{18.0}{149.0} \times 100\% \approx 12.08\% \\
R_{\text{HR4}} &= \frac{32.4}{149.0} \times 100\% \approx 21.75\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Sanity check (must equal 100%):**

$$
36.64 + 29.53 + 12.08 + 21.75 = 100.00\%
$$

### 3.2 Interpretation of Results

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Action Plan derived from the math:**
> - **HR1 (Magnetron Wattage)** is the highest engineering priority at **36.64%** → engineering team must focus on wattage optimization first.
> - **HR2 (Easy-clean coating)** follows at **29.53%** → high R&D investment required for interior coating.
> - **HR4 (Warranty)** comes third at **21.75%** → operations must align with a 24-month standard warranty.
> - **HR3 (Noise)** is the lowest at **12.08%** → moderate effort; perhaps target ≤ 55 dB.

### 3.3 Advanced Variation — Incorporating Difficulty Score

If the engineering team also assigns a difficulty score $D_j$:

| How | Absolute Importance $A_j$ | Difficulty $D_j$ | Priority Score $P_j = A_j / D_j$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| HR1 (Watts) | 54.6 | 3.0 | 18.20 |
| HR2 (Coating) | 44.0 | 9.0 | 4.89 |
| HR3 (Noise) | 18.0 | 9.0 | 2.00 |
| HR4 (Warranty) | 32.4 | 1.0 | 32.40 |

Here, although HR1 has higher absolute importance, **HR4 (Warranty)** is the easiest to achieve and gets prioritized first because dividing by low difficulty inflates its score.

### 3.4 Summary Matrix of the Worked Example

| How # | Engineering How | Absolute Weight $A_j$ | Relative Weight $R_j$ | Engineering Action |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| HR1 | Magnetron power output (W) | 54.6 | 36.64% | High priority — invest in wattage upgrade. |
| HR2 | Coating roughness (Ra µm) | 44.0 | 29.53% | Medium-high — invest in coating R&D. |
| HR3 | Noise level (dB) | 18.0 | 12.08% | Low — design ≥ 55 dB threshold. |
| HR4 | Warranty period (months) | 32.4 | 21.75% | Medium — align warranty ops. |
| | **TOTAL** | **149.0** | **100.00%** | |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The House of Quality — Mermaid Block Diagram

Below is a comprehensive Mermaid diagram mapping all seven rooms of the House of Quality and the directional flow of data between them.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    room1["ROOM 1 LEFT WALL : WHATS - Customer Requirements with Importance Ratings and Sales Points"]
    room2["ROOM 2 CEILING : HOWS - Technical Engineering Requirements with Units"]
    room3["ROOM 3 CENTER : RELATIONSHIP MATRIX Symbols - dot - circle - triangle - blank with weights 9 3 1 0"]
    room4["ROOM 4 ROOF : INTER HOWS CORRELATION MATRIX - Trade offs and Synergies"]
    room5["ROOM 5 RIGHT WALL : CUSTOMER COMPETITIVE ANALYSIS - Our brand vs Competitor A vs Competitor B"]
    room6["ROOM 6 BASEMENT : TECHNICAL COMPETITIVE BENCHMARKING - Engineering Metrics Comparison"]
    room7["ROOM 7 FOUNDATION : OUTPUT FLOOR - Target Values - Absolute Weight - Relative Weight - Difficulty - Priority Score"]

    room1 --> room3
    room2 --> room3
    room3 --> room7
    room2 --> room4
    room4 --> room7
    room1 --> room5
    room2 --> room6
    room5 --> room7
    room6 --> room7

    style room1 fill:#FFE5B4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style room2 fill:#B4D4FF,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style room3 fill:#FFB4B4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style room4 fill:#C4F4C4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style room5 fill:#F4C4F4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style room6 fill:#F4F4C4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style room7 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.2 The Four-Phase Cascading QFD Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    phase1["PHASE 1 : PRODUCT PLANNING - House of Quality - CRs to Technical Specs"]
    phase2["PHASE 2 : PRODUCT DESIGN - Deployment to Piece Parts and Components"]
    phase3["PHASE 3 : PROCESS PLANNING - Deployment to Manufacturing Operations"]
    phase4["PHASE 4 : PRODUCTION PLANNING - Deployment to Quality Controls and Tooling"]

    voc[("Voice of Customer VOC - Market Survey - Focus Groups")]
    final[("Final Product with Documented Customer Centric Design")]

    voc --> phase1
    phase1 --> phase2
    phase2 --> phase3
    phase3 --> phase4
    phase4 --> final

    style phase1 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style phase2 fill:#FFA500,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style phase3 fill:#FF8C00,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style phase4 fill:#FF4500,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style voc fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style final fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
```
