### 4.3 QFD Decision Tree — Selecting Relationship Symbols

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[("Does the Technical Requirement Directly and Strongly Impact the Customer Requirement ?")]
    decisionA{"Is the Impact Quantitatively Large ?"}
    decisionB{"Is the Impact Moderate ?"}
    decisionC{"Is the Impact Minor ?"}
    noImpact{"No Measurable Link ?"}

    s1["Assign STRONG Symbol : dot - Weight 9"]
    s2["Assign MODERATE Symbol : circle - Weight 3"]
    s3["Assign WEAK Symbol : triangle - Weight 1"]
    s4["Leave BLANK - Weight 0"]

    start --> decisionA
    decisionA -- Yes --> s1
    decisionA -- No --> decisionB
    decisionB -- Yes --> s2
    decisionB -- No --> decisionC
    decisionC -- Yes --> s3
    decisionC -- No --> noImpact
    noImpact --> s4

    style start fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style s1 fill:#FF0000,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style s2 fill:#FFA500,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style s3 fill:#FFFF00,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style s4 fill:#FFFFFF,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question A1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define Quality Function Deployment. List the four phases of QFD as proposed by Akao.**

**Model Answer (Target: 3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Quality Function Deployment (QFD)** is a structured planning methodology that translates the **Voice of the Customer (VOC)** into specific engineering requirements throughout the product and process development cycle. It ensures customer-driven design by integrating marketing, engineering, and manufacturing information into a single matrix called the **House of Quality**.

**The Four Phases of QFD (Akao's Model):**
1. **Phase 1 — Product Planning:** Customer requirements are translated into product technical specifications (House of Quality).
2. **Phase 2 — Product Design Deployment:** Technical specifications are deployed into **piece-part / component** characteristics.
3. **Phase 3 — Process Planning Deployment:** Piece-part characteristics are deployed into **manufacturing process** operations.
4. **Phase 4 — Production Planning Deployment:** Process operations are deployed into **production controls, tooling, and quality procedures.**

---

#### **Question A2 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Explain the meaning of "Whats" and "Hows" in the House of Quality, with one engineering example each.**

**Model Answer (Target: 3 marks):**

In the House of Quality, the **Whats** are the **Customer Requirements (CRs)** — statements describing what the customer wants, expressed in the customer's own language. The **Hows** are the **Engineering / Technical Requirements (ERs)** — measurable engineering characteristics that fulfil the customer requirements.

**Example (Electric Two-Wheeler Design):**
- **What (CR):** "Long battery range per charge." (Customer language — subjective)
- **How (ER):** "Battery energy density ≥ 250 Wh/kg and motor efficiency ≥ 92%." (Engineering language — measurable)
- The **relationship matrix** would show a **strong (●)** link between the What and each How, meaning improving the How strongly satisfies the customer.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Internal Choice Instruction:** *Answer either Question B-A or Question B-B in full. Both carry 14 marks and must include sub-parts (a) and (b) for 7 marks each.*

---

#### **Question B-A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 — Adapted]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Draw and explain the **House of Quality (HoQ)** with all its rooms. What is the significance of the **roof of the house**?

**(b) [7 marks]** A bicycle manufacturer has identified the following customer requirements (Whats) and corresponding technical features (Hows). Build the House of Quality and compute the **absolute and relative importance** of each How.

**Customer Requirements (Whats) with Importance:**

| What # | Customer Requirement | Importance (1–5) |
|---|---|---|
| CR1 | Low cost | 5 |
| CR2 | Comfortable ride | 4 |
| CR3 | Stylish design | 3 |
| CR4 | Long-lasting frame | 4 |

**Technical Requirements (Hows):**
- HR1 = Frame material tensile strength (MPa)
- HR2 = Saddle ergonomics score (1–10)
- HR3 = Paint coating durability (hours UV resistance)
- HR4 = Manufacturing cost per unit (₹)

**Relationship Matrix (Engineering Assignment):**

| What ↓ \ How → | HR1 | HR2 | HR3 | HR4 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| CR1 (Low cost) | 1 | 0 | 0 | 9 |
| CR2 (Comfort) | 0 | 9 | 0 | 0 |
| CR3 (Style) | 0 | 3 | 9 | 0 |
| CR4 (Durability) | 9 | 0 | 3 | 1 |

---

##### **Model Solution for Q B-A:**

**Part (a) — House of Quality Diagram and Rooms:**

The House of Quality consists of **seven rooms** as shown in the diagram (refer to Section 4.1). The **rooms are:**

1. **Left Wall (Whats):** Lists customer requirements with importance ratings.
2. **Ceiling (Hows):** Lists engineering/technical requirements with units.
3. **Central Matrix (Relationship):** Shows the strength of correlation between each What and each How using symbols ●, ○, △, blank (with weights 9, 3, 1, 0).
4. **Roof Matrix:** Triangular matrix showing **inter-Hows correlations** (trade-offs and synergies).
5. **Right Wall:** Customer competitive analysis (perception scores for own brand vs. competitors).
6. **Basement:** Technical competitive benchmarking (engineering metrics vs. competitors).
7. **Foundation/Floor:** Output — target values, absolute weight, relative weight, and priority ranking.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Significance of the Roof (Inter-Hows Correlation Matrix):**
> The roof reveals **engineering trade-offs and synergies** that are not visible in the central relationship matrix alone. For example, in our bicycle case:
> - **HR1 (Tensile Strength)** and **HR4 (Manufacturing Cost)** are likely to be **strongly negatively correlated** (--): stronger alloys are costlier to machine and procure.
> - **HR2 (Saddle Ergonomics)** and **HR3 (Paint Durability)** are largely **uncorrelated** (blank): they can be improved independently.
> The roof matrix forces the engineering team to make **explicit trade-off decisions early in design**, preventing costly late-stage redesigns.

**Part (b) — Computation of Absolute and Relative Importance:**

Apply $W_i = I_i \times S_i$ (assume $S_i = 1.0$ for all, so $W_i = I_i$).

Thus: $W_{CR1} = 5,\ W_{CR2} = 4,\ W_{CR3} = 3,\ W_{CR4} = 4$.

**Compute Absolute Importance $A_j$ using $A_j = \sum W_i \cdot r_{ij}$:**

**For HR1 (Tensile Strength):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{HR1} &= (5 \cdot 1) + (4 \cdot 0) + (3 \cdot 0) + (4 \cdot 9) \\
&= 5 + 0 + 0 + 36 = 41.0
\end{aligned}
$$

**For HR2 (Saddle Ergonomics):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{HR2} &= (5 \cdot 0) + (4 \cdot 9) + (3 \cdot 3) + (4 \cdot 0) \\
&= 0 + 36 + 9 + 0 = 45.0
\end{aligned}
$$

**For HR3 (Paint Durability):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{HR3} &= (5 \cdot 0) + (4 \cdot 0) + (3 \cdot 9) + (4 \cdot 3) \\
&= 0 + 0 + 27 + 12 = 39.0
\end{aligned}
$$

**For HR4 (Manufacturing Cost):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{HR4} &= (5 \cdot 9) + (4 \cdot 0) + (3 \cdot 0) + (4 \cdot 1) \\
&= 45 + 0 + 0 + 4 = 49.0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Total Absolute Weight:**

$$
\sum A_j = 41.0 + 45.0 + 39.0 + 49.0 = 174.0
$$

**Compute Relative Importance $R_j$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{HR1} &= \frac{41.0}{174.0} \times 100\% \approx 23.56\% \\
R_{HR2} &= \frac{45.0}{174.0} \times 100\% \approx 25.86\% \\
R_{HR3} &= \frac{39.0}{174.0} \times 100\% \approx 22.41\% \\
R_{HR4} &= \frac{49.0}{174.0} \times 100\% \approx 28.16\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Sanity check:** $23.56 + 25.86 + 22.41 + 28.16 \approx 99.99\% \approx 100\%$ ✓

**Final Ranking of Engineering Priorities:**

| Rank | How | Relative Weight | Engineering Action |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | HR4 (Manufacturing Cost) | 28.16% | Top priority — drive down cost. |
| 2 | HR2 (Saddle Ergonomics) | 25.86% | Invest in ergonomic saddle design. |
| 3 | HR1 (Frame Tensile Strength) | 23.56% | Specify alloy grade (e.g., 6061-T6). |
| 4 | HR3 (Paint Durability) | 22.41% | Specify ≥ 500 h UV resistance. |

---

#### **Question B-B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2023 — Adapted]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Discuss the **benefits and limitations** of implementing QFD in a manufacturing organization.

**(b) [7 marks]** A laptop manufacturer collects the following VOC data for a new design. Compute the **technical importance** of each engineering requirement and recommend the top priority for the engineering team.

**Customer Requirements:**

| What # | CR | Importance $I_i$ | Sales Point $S_i$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| CR1 | Light weight | 5 | 1.0 |
| CR2 | Long battery life | 5 | 1.2 |
| CR3 | Low cost | 4 | 1.0 |
| CR4 | High screen quality | 4 | 1.0 |
| CR5 | Fast processor | 3 | 1.2 |

**Engineering Requirements (Hows):** HR1 (Battery capacity Wh), HR2 (Display resolution PPI), HR3 (CPU clock GHz), HR4 (Chassis weight g), HR5 (Production cost ₹).

**Relationship Matrix:**

| What ↓ \ How → | HR1 | HR2 | HR3 | HR4 | HR5 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| CR1 (Light) | 0 | 0 | 0 | 9 | 1 |
| CR2 (Battery) | 9 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 0 |
| CR3 (Low cost) | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 9 |
| CR4 (Screen) | 0 | 9 | 1 | 0 | 0 |
| CR5 (Speed) | 0 | 0 | 9 | 0 | 0 |

---

##### **Model Solution for Q B-B:**

**Part (a) — Benefits and Limitations of QFD:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Benefits of QFD:**
> 1. **Customer Focus:** Forces explicit incorporation of the Voice of the Customer into design, reducing the risk of producing an unwanted product.
> 2. **Cross-functional Integration:** Brings marketing, design, manufacturing, and quality teams onto a single page, breaking departmental silos.
> 3. **Reduced Design Changes:** Identifies trade-offs early in the roof matrix, preventing costly late-stage redesigns (typical 30–50% reduction in engineering changes).
> 4. **Shorter Time-to-Market:** Parallelizes design decisions; studies report 30–50% reduction in development cycle time.
> 5. **Documented Decision Trail:** Provides auditable evidence (the HoQ matrix) for management and regulatory review — useful in ISO 9001 audits.
> 6. **Prioritized Engineering Focus:** Quantitatively ranks engineering efforts by relative importance rather than engineering intuition.

> [!NOTE]
> **Limitations of QFD:**
> 1. **Time-Intensive Initial Build:** Constructing the HoQ requires extensive market research and cross-functional meetings — heavy front-loaded effort.
> 2. **Customer Data Quality:** QFD outputs are only as good as the VOC data; biased surveys yield biased design priorities.
> 3. **Difficulty in Quantifying "Soft" Requirements:** Aesthetics, emotional appeal, and brand prestige are hard to translate into measurable Hows.
> 4. **Static Snapshot:** A single HoQ captures customer needs at one point in time; needs may shift with market trends.
> 5. **Implementation Cost:** Training employees in QFD methodology is non-trivial; resistance to change is common.

**Part (b) — Computing Technical Importance:**

First, compute **Adjusted Importance** $W_i = I_i \times S_i$:

$$
W_{CR1} = 5 \times 1.0 = 5.0,\quad W_{CR2} = 5 \times 1.2 = 6.0
$$
$$
W_{CR3} = 4 \times 1.0 = 4.0,\quad W_{CR4} = 4 \times 1.0 = 4.0
$$
$$
W_{CR5} = 3 \times 1.2 = 3.6
$$

**Compute Absolute Importance $A_j$ for each How:**

**For HR1 (Battery Wh):**

$$
A_{HR1} = (5 \cdot 0) + (6 \cdot 9) + (4 \cdot 0) + (4 \cdot 0) + (3.6 \cdot 0) = 54.0
$$

**For HR2 (Display PPI):**

$$
A_{HR2} = (5 \cdot 0) + (6 \cdot 0) + (4 \cdot 0) + (4 \cdot 9) + (3.6 \cdot 0) = 36.0
$$

**For HR3 (CPU GHz):**

$$
A_{HR3} = (5 \cdot 0) + (6 \cdot 1) + (4 \cdot 0) + (4 \cdot 1) + (3.6 \cdot 9) = 6.0 + 4.0 + 32.4 = 42.4
$$

**For HR4 (Chassis Weight):**

$$
A_{HR4} = (5 \cdot 9) + (6 \cdot 0) + (4 \cdot 0) + (4 \cdot 0) + (3.6 \cdot 0) = 45.0
$$

**For HR5 (Production Cost):**

$$
A_{HR5} = (5 \cdot 1) + (6 \cdot 0) + (4 \cdot 9) + (4 \cdot 0) + (3.6 \cdot 0) = 5.0 + 36.0 = 41.0
$$

**Total Absolute Weight:**

$$
\sum A_j = 54.0 + 36.0 + 42.4 + 45.0 + 41.0 = 218.4
$$

**Compute Relative Importance $R_j$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{HR1} &= \frac{54.0}{218.4} \times 100\% \approx 24.73\% \\
R_{HR2} &= \frac{36.0}{218.4} \times 100\% \approx 16.48\% \\
R_{HR3} &= \frac{42.4}{218.4} \times 100\% \approx 19.41\% \\
R_{HR4} &= \frac{45.0}{218.4} \times 100\% \approx 20.60\% \\
R_{HR5} &= \frac{41.0}{218.4} \times 100\% \approx 18.77\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Sanity check:** $24.73 + 16.48 + 19.41 + 20.60 + 18.77 \approx 99.99\% \approx 100\%$ ✓

**Engineering Recommendation:**

| Rank | How | Relative Weight | Engineering Action |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | HR1 — Battery Capacity (Wh) | 24.73% | **TOP PRIORITY** — invest in higher Wh/kg cells. |
| 2 | HR4 — Chassis Weight (g) | 20.60% | Use magnesium-aluminum alloy to reduce mass. |
| 3 | HR3 — CPU Clock (GHz) | 19.41% | Specify ≥ 3.5 GHz performance CPU. |
| 4 | HR5 — Production Cost (₹) | 18.77% | Implement cost-engineering review. |
| 5 | HR2 — Display PPI | 16.48% | Specify ≥ 2200 PPI Retina-class display. |

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places where students LOSE marks in QFD questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the Sales Point multiplier** — Students often use raw importance $I_i$ instead of adjusted importance $W_i = I_i \cdot S_i$. Examiners deduct **1–2 marks** for this.
> 2. **Wrong relationship weights** — Using $\bullet = 5,\ \bigcirc = 3,\ \triangle = 1$ is incorrect. The standard Akao weights are **9, 3, 1, 0**. Wrong weights = **full marks lost** on absolute weight computation.
> 3. **Forgetting the sanity check** — The relative weights must sum to 100%. Examiners reward (or penalize) this verification step explicitly.
> 4. **Ignoring the Roof Matrix** — In 7-mark sub-questions on HoQ anatomy, students often list 6 rooms and **forget the roof**. The roof is worth **2–3 marks** in KTU answer keys.
> 5. **No engineering recommendation** — The final computed table without an engineering action statement loses the **application marks** (typically 2 marks).
> 6. **Confusing "Whats" and "Hows"** — Writing engineering characteristics in the "Whats" column or customer statements in the "Hows" column. Examiners penalize this **heavily** as it shows conceptual confusion.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key things to remember before the KTU exam:**
> - **QFD** = translating **Voice of Customer (VOC)** into **engineering specifications** at every stage of product development.
> - **House of Quality (HoQ)** is the primary tool; it has **7 rooms**.
> - **Whats** = Customer Requirements (CRs); **Hows** = Engineering Requirements (ERs).
> - **Four Phases of QFD (Akao):** Product Planning → Product Design Deployment → Process Planning Deployment → Production Planning Deployment.
> - **Relationship symbols:** ● (strong = 9), ○ (moderate = 3), △ (weak = 1), blank (0).
> - **Key formula:** $A_j = \sum W_i \cdot r_{ij}$ where $W_i = I_i \cdot S_i$.
> - **Relative Weight formula:** $R_j = (A_j / \sum A_j) \times 100\%$.
> - **Sum of all relative weights = 100%** (always verify).
> - **Roof matrix** shows engineering **trade-offs** between Hows.
> - **Right Wall** = customer competitive analysis; **Basement** = technical competitive analysis.
> - **Originators:** Yoji Akao and Shigeru Mizuno (Japan, late 1960s); popularized at **Toyota** and **Mitsubishi Kobe Shipyard**.
> - **QFD benefits:** reduced design changes, shorter time-to-market, customer-driven design, cross-functional integration.
> - **QFD limitations:** time-intensive, dependent on VOC quality, difficulty in quantifying soft requirements, static snapshot of customer needs.
> - In **advanced QFD**, divide $A_j$ by **difficulty score** $D_j$ to obtain **priority score** $P_j = A_j / D_j$, prioritizing achievable high-impact engineering actions.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Failure Mode and Effect Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET758 - QUALITY ENGINEERING AND MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Failure Mode and Effect Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Failure Mode and Effect Analysis (FMEA)** is a structured, systematic, and proactive analytical engineering technique used to identify, evaluate, prioritize, and document potential failure modes of a product, process, or system, along with their associated causes, effects, and the likelihood of detection, **before** the failure actually occurs in the field or during production.

The FMEA methodology was originally developed by the U.S. military in **1949** and later standardized by:
- **SAE J1739** – Society of Automotive Engineers standard
- **IEC 60812** – International Electrotechnical Commission standard
- **AIAG-VDA 2019** – Automotive Industry Action Group and Verband der Automobilindustrie (current industry benchmark)

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU 2024 Scheme (PEMET758) treats FMEA as the **cornerstone predictive quality tool** under the umbrella of *Reliability Engineering and Quality Planning*. It must be clearly distinguished from *corrective* tools like 8D or Root Cause Analysis, which react to failures *after* occurrence.

### 1.2 The Three Principal Variants of FMEA

| Variant | Abbreviation | Primary Application | Stage of Application |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **System FMEA** | SFMEA | Whole-system architecture, including interfaces, software, and environment | Concept / Feasibility stage |
| **Design FMEA** | DFMEA | Engineering design of components and sub-assemblies | Detailed Design stage |
| **Process FMEA** | PFMEA | Manufacturing and assembly processes | Process Planning stage |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy – The "Pre-Monsoon Home Inspection"

Imagine you live in **Kerala**, where the monsoon brings intense rain, waterlogging, and structural stress to every house. Instead of waiting for the ceiling to leak, walls to crack, and short-circuits to occur (and then paying lakhs to repair them), a wise homeowner hires an engineer to conduct a **Pre-Monsoon Home Inspection**.

The engineer walks room by room and asks three questions about *every* potential vulnerability (say, a window frame, a rooftop tile, an electrical junction):

1. **How bad would it be if it fails?** → *"Water would pour into the bedroom and ruin the wiring"* (this is the **Severity**).
2. **How likely is it to fail?** → *"This happens almost every year"* (this is the **Occurrence**).
3. **How easily will I notice it before it causes damage?** → *"The leak will be obvious the moment it rains"* (this is the **Detection** – easy to notice = *low* detection risk, because you *detect* it early).

The engineer then multiplies the three numbers to get a single **Risk Priority Number (RPN)**, ranks the windows/tiles/junctions by RPN, and recommends targeted fixes (sealants, replacement tiles, weatherproofing) only for the high-risk items. That, in essence, is **FMEA** applied to a product or process.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Hook:** FMEA is **forward-looking insurance**. You pay a small "premium" (engineer-hours during design) to avoid a massive "claim" (recall, warranty cost, customer injury) later.

### 1.4 Key Terminology in KTU Context

- **Failure Mode** – The *manner* in which a component, process, or system could potentially fail (e.g., *fractures*, *leaks*, *short-circuits*, *welds poorly*).
- **Effect** – The *consequence* of the failure mode on the system, the customer, or regulatory compliance.
- **Cause** – The *root mechanism* that triggers the failure mode (often a *design deficiency* or a *process variation*).
- **Severity (S)** – A measure of how serious the *effect* of failure is to the customer or end-user.
- **Occurrence (O)** – A measure of how frequently the *cause* of failure is likely to happen.
- **Detectability / Detection (D)** – A measure of the ability of current controls to *detect* the failure *before* it reaches the customer.
- **Risk Priority Number (RPN)** – A composite metric equal to $S \times O \times D$.
- **Action Priority (AP)** – The modern (AIAG-VDA 2019) replacement for RPN, classified as **H (High)**, **M (Medium)**, or **L (Low)**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Eight Formal Steps of an FMEA Study

The KTU syllabus (PEMET758 Module 4) recommends the following **8-step sequential workflow**, which must be applied rigorously in any examination answer:

1. **Assemble the Cross-Functional Team** – Quality, Design, Manufacturing, Service, Supplier (typically 5–9 members, each with **>5 years of domain expertise**).
2. **Define the Scope, Boundaries, and Customer** – Establish what is *in-scope* and *out-of-scope*, and identify the *internal* or *external* customer.
3. **Identify Functions of the Item / Process** – For each component, list what it *must do* (functional requirements).
4. **Identify Potential Failure Modes** – For each function, list *how* it could fail to deliver that function.
5. **Identify Effects and Causes** – For each failure mode, identify the *consequence* and the *trigger mechanism*.
6. **Assign S, O, D Ratings** – Use the standard **1–10 rating scales** (refer Section 2.3).
7. **Compute RPN and Prioritize** – Calculate $RPN = S \times O \times D$ and rank items from highest to lowest.
8. **Recommend Actions and Re-evaluate** – Define corrective, preventive, or detection actions; recompute RPN after action implementation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **S, O, D scales are ordinal, not cardinal**. A jump from Severity 8 to 9 is not "twice as bad" – it is a step on a *rank-ordered* severity ladder. Examiners frequently test this conceptual nuance.

### 2.2 The Risk Priority Number (RPN) – Mathematical Formulation

The classical RPN formula (used universally in KTU problems) is:

$$
RPN = S \times O \times D
$$

Where:
- $S$ = Severity rating $\in \{1, 2, \ldots, 10\}$
- $O$ = Occurrence rating $\in \{1, 2, \ldots, 10\}$
- $D$ = Detection rating $\in \{1, 2, \ldots, 10\}$

The **theoretical maximum RPN** is therefore:

$$
RPN_{\max} = 10 \times 10 \times 10 = 1000
$$

The **theoretical minimum RPN** is:

$$
RPN_{\min} = 1 \times 1 \times 1 = 1
$$

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula & Rating Sheet

The following **master reference table** is the single most exam-critical artifact for this topic.

| Symbol | Parameter | Range | Anchor Value | Engineering Meaning |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $S$ | Severity | $1 - 10$ | $S = 10$ | Failure endangers operator (safety) |
| $S$ | Severity | $1 - 10$ | $S = 8$ | Failure renders product inoperable |
| $S$ | Severity | $1 - 10$ | $S = 1$ | Failure has no discernible impact |
| $O$ | Occurrence | $1 - 10$ | $O = 10$ | Failure almost inevitable ($\geq 1$ in $2$) |
| $O$ | Occurrence | $1 - 10$ | $O = 1$ | Failure unlikely ($\leq 1$ in $1{,}500{,}000$) |
| $D$ | Detection | $1 - 10$ | $D = 10$ | No current control can detect the failure |
| $D$ | Detection | $1 - 10$ | $D = 1$ | Control almost certainly detects the failure |
| $RPN$ | Risk Priority Number | $1 - 1000$ | Threshold $\geq 100$ | Typically demands action |
| $AP$ | Action Priority | $H / M / L$ | $AP = H$ | Mandatory action required |

> [!WARNING]
> **RPN Thresholds in KTU Answers:** While there is no universal industry threshold, the conventional benchmark accepted in KTU valuation is **$RPN \geq 100$** OR **$S \geq 9$** (regardless of RPN) – the latter ensures *safety-critical* items are never deprioritized simply because $O$ or $D$ are low.

### 2.4 Action Priority (AP) – The AIAG-VDA 2019 Replacement for RPN

The modern automotive standard (and frequently asked in KTU Module 4) recognizes that **RPN alone is misleading** because two failure modes with $RPN = 200$ can have *very different risk profiles* (e.g., $S=10, O=10, D=2$ vs. $S=4, O=5, D=10$). The **Action Priority (AP)** is therefore computed using a **lookup matrix** based on $S$, $O$, and $D$:

| AP Level | Decision | Meaning |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **H – High** | *Must Act* | Failure demands immediate corrective / preventive action. |
| **M – Medium** | *Should Act* | Failure warrants action as resources permit. |
| **L – Low** | *May Act* | Failure can be monitored without dedicated action. |

The AP matrix is constructed so that **any $S \geq 9$ automatically yields $AP = H$** – overriding the Occurrence and Detection values.

### 2.5 Why FMEA is Indispensable in Engineering Practice

- **Reduces Field Failures** – Automotive studies show FMEA reduces in-service failures by **30–60%**.
- **Prevents Costly Recalls** – The average recall cost in automotive industry exceeds **\$15 million per event**; FMEA cost is a fraction of this.
- **Facilitates Cross-Functional Communication** – Single standardized worksheet acts as a *lingua franca* between design, manufacturing, and quality.
- **Enables Continuous Improvement (Kaizen)** – Re-evaluation step supports ISO 9001 / IATF 16949 / AS9100 audit requirements.
- **Documentation for Regulatory Compliance** – FDA, EASA, and automotive OEMs mandate FMEA for safety-critical items.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Detailed Numerical Example – RPN Computation for a Brake Disc Assembly

A KTU examination will typically provide a component description, ask the student to list failure modes, and compute RPN. The worked example below uses the **Process FMEA of a Brake Disc Machining Operation** at a Kerala-based Tier-1 automotive supplier.

#### Step 1: Define the Process

- **Process:** Turning of cast-iron brake disc on a CNC lathe.
- **Customer:** Automotive OEM assembly line.
- **Specification:** Disc diameter $= 280 \pm 0.05$ mm, surface roughness $\leq 1.6$ µm Ra.

#### Step 2: Identify Failure Modes, Effects, and Causes

For brevity, the **3 most critical items** are extracted (a real PFMEA would have 15–25 rows):

| Item | Function | Failure Mode | Effect | Cause |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Disc Surface | Provide friction | Surface roughness > 1.6 µm | Brake noise, reduced life | Tool wear, wrong feed rate |
| Disc Diameter | Fit to caliper | Diameter out of tolerance | Vibration, caliper binding | Tool offset, thermal drift |
| Disc Runout | Ensure concentricity | Excessive runout | Brake judder | Improper clamping |

#### Step 3: Assign Initial Severity (S), Occurrence (O), Detection (D) Ratings

Using the standard **1–10 scales**:

- **Surface roughness failure** → $S = 7$ (loss of primary function – braking efficiency reduced), $O = 5$ (occurs occasionally), $D = 6$ (visual + roughness-meter check at end-of-line, but not 100% reliable).
- **Diameter out of tolerance** → $S = 8$ (vehicle cannot be assembled), $O = 4$ (occurs seldom), $D = 3$ (automated SPC chart with control limits catches most excursions).
- **Excessive runout** → $S = 9$ (safety – brake judder can cause loss of control), $O = 3$ (low), $D = 7$ (only detected during end-of-line runout test).

#### Step 4: Compute Initial RPN Values

**Sub-step 4.1: Surface Roughness Failure**

$$
RPN_1 = S_1 \times O_1 \times D_1
$$

$$
RPN_1 = 7 \times 5 \times 6
$$

$$
RPN_1 = 35 \times 6
$$

$$
RPN_1 = 210
$$

**Sub-step 4.2: Diameter Out of Tolerance**

$$
RPN_2 = S_2 \times O_2 \times D_2
$$

$$
RPN_2 = 8 \times 4 \times 3
$$

$$
RPN_2 = 32 \times 3
$$

$$
RPN_2 = 96
$$

**Sub-step 4.3: Excessive Runout**

$$
RPN_3 = S_3 \times O_3 \times D_3
$$

$$
RPN_3 = 9 \times 3 \times 7
$$

$$
RPN_3 = 27 \times 7
$$

$$
RPN_3 = 189
$$

#### Step 5: Prioritize and Recommend Actions

Ranks: $RPN_1 (210) > RPN_3 (189) > RPN_2 (96)$.

- **Item 1 (Roughness):** $RPN = 210 > 100$ → **Action Required.** Install in-process surface roughness probe on CNC, replace tool at 50% of nominal life, train operator on feed-rate limits.
- **Item 3 (Runout):** $RPN = 189 > 100$ AND $S = 9$ → **Action Required (priority 1 by Severity).** Add automated clamping torque verification, install in-process runout probe.
- **Item 2 (Diameter):** $RPN = 96 < 100$ AND $S = 8 < 9$ → **Monitor.** Existing SPC controls adequate; review in next revision.

#### Step 6: Re-compute RPN After Action Implementation

After the recommended actions, the new ratings are:

- Item 1: $S = 7$ (unchanged), $O = 2$ (tool-life mgmt reduced variation), $D = 3$ (in-process probe catches drift).

$$
RPN_1^{new} = 7 \times 2 \times 3 = 42
$$

- Item 3: $S = 9$ (unchanged – safety cannot be reduced), $O = 1$ (clamping auto-verified), $D = 2$ (in-process probe catches runout deviation).

$$
RPN_3^{new} = 9 \times 1 \times 2 = 18
$$

#### Step 7: Compute % Reduction in RPN

The percentage reduction in RPN for Item 1 is computed as:

$$
\Delta RPN \% = \frac{RPN_{old} - RPN_{new}}{RPN_{old}} \times 100
$$

$$
\Delta RPN \% = \frac{210 - 42}{210} \times 100
$$

$$
\Delta RPN \% = \frac{168}{210} \times 100
$$

$$
\Delta RPN \% = 0.80 \times 100
$$

$$
\Delta RPN \% = 80 \%
$$

For Item 3:

$$
\Delta RPN \% = \frac{189 - 18}{189} \times 100
$$

$$
\Delta RPN \% = \frac{171}{189} \times 100
$$

$$
\Delta RPN \% = 0.9048 \times 100 \approx 90.48 \%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This 80–90% RPN reduction quantifies the *engineering return on investment* of FMEA-driven action – a metric that KTU examiners love as a follow-up question in the 14-mark paper.

### 3.2 AP Matrix Worked Example (AIAG-VDA 2019 Style)

For the same brake disc, the AP matrix lookup (simplified excerpt) gives:

- Item 1 ($S=7, O=5, D=6$) → AP = **M (Medium)**
- Item 2 ($S=8, O=4, D=3$) → AP = **M (Medium)**
- Item 3 ($S=9, O=3, D=7$) → AP = **H (High)** – because $S \geq 9$ overrides everything.

This demonstrates why a high-Severity item (Item 3) is correctly classified as **High Priority** even though its raw RPN (189) is *less than* Item 1 (210).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The FMEA Process Workflow (Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow)

The following Mermaid block diagram captures the **eight-step FMEA workflow** end-to-end, including the iterative feedback loop from action re-evaluation back to risk ranking. Node IDs are alphanumeric and prefixed with letters to satisfy Mermaid's reserved-keyword restrictions.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Start FMEA Study] --> A2[Assemble Cross Functional Team]
    A2 --> A3[Define Scope Boundaries and Customer]
    A3 --> A4[List Functions of Item or Process]
    A4 --> A5[Identify Potential Failure Modes]
    A5 --> A6[Identify Effects and Potential Causes]
    A6 --> A7[Assign Severity Occurrence Detection Ratings]
    A7 --> A8[Compute RPN equals S times O times D]
    A8 --> A9{RPN greater than Threshold or S greater than 9}
    A9 -- Yes --> A10[Recommend Corrective and Preventive Actions]
    A9 -- No --> A11[Monitor and Document]
    A10 --> A12[Implement Actions on Product or Process]
    A12 --> A13[Re evaluate S O D and Recompute RPN]
    A13 --> A8
    A11 --> A14[Archive FMEA and Close Study]
    A13 --> A14
    A14 --> A1
```

### 4.2 The FMEA Worksheet Data Model

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **relationships between the core entities** of an FMEA worksheet (the same data model used by APIS IQ-FMEA, Siemens Teamcenter, and similar PLM tools).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    B1[Item or Process Function] --> B2[Failure Mode]
    B2 --> B3[Effect on Customer]
    B2 --> B4[Potential Cause]
    B4 --> B5[Occurrence Rating O]
    B2 --> B6[Severity Rating S]
    B4 --> B7[Detection Rating D]
    B5 --> B8[RPN Computation]
    B6 --> B8
    B7 --> B8
    B8 --> B9{Risk Decision}
    B9 -- High Risk --> B10[Action Item]
    B9 -- Low Risk --> B11[Accept and Monitor]
    B10 --> B12[Action Owner and Due Date]
    B12 --> B13[Re evaluate Ratings]
    B13 --> B8
```

### 4.3 RPN Risk Matrix Visualization (3D Conceptual Mapping)

The following Mermaid block diagram shows how the **three-dimensional S-O-D rating space** is mapped to a single RPN scalar, then sliced into risk zones.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    C1[Severity S 1 to 10] --> C4[RPN equals S times O times D]
    C2[Occurrence O 1 to 10] --> C4
    C3[Detection D 1 to 10] --> C4
    C4 --> C5{Risk Zone Classifier}
    C5 -- RPN 1 to 50 --> C6[Low Risk - Accept]
    C5 -- RPN 51 to 100 --> C7[Moderate Risk - Monitor]
    C5 -- RPN 101 to 300 --> C8[High Risk - Action Required]
    C5 -- RPN 301 to 1000 --> C9[Critical Risk - Immediate Action]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The three inputs (S, O, D) flow from independent rating scales into a single mathematical reduction (RPN), which is then bucketed into four engineering decision zones. This is the conceptual essence of the entire FMEA risk-prioritization logic.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**

Define **Failure Mode and Effect Analysis (FMEA)**. List the **two main types of FMEA** and state one specific application area for each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1.5 marks):** FMEA is a systematic, proactive engineering technique used to identify, evaluate, and prioritize potential failure modes of a product, process, or system — and to define actions to mitigate the highest-risk items — *before* the failure occurs in the field.

> **Types and Applications (1.5 marks):**
> 1. **Design FMEA (DFMEA)** – Used during the *engineering design phase* to identify potential failures in the product itself. Example: analyzing the design of an automotive brake caliper for fatigue fracture, leakage, or thermal deformation.
> 2. **Process FMEA (PFMEA)** – Used during the *manufacturing process planning* phase to identify potential failures in the production process. Example: analyzing a CNC turning operation for out-of-tolerance dimensions, surface defects, or improper heat treatment.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**

What is the **Risk Priority Number (RPN)**? State its mathematical formula and the maximum possible value.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1 mark):** The Risk Priority Number (RPN) is a quantitative metric used in FMEA to prioritize failure modes for corrective action, based on the product of three ordinal rating scales.

> **Formula and Maximum (2 marks):**
>
> $$
> RPN = S \times O \times D
> $$
>
> Where $S$ = Severity, $O$ = Occurrence, $D$ = Detection, each on a scale of 1 to 10. Therefore, the **maximum theoretical RPN = $10 \times 10 \times 10 = 1000$**, and the **minimum = $1 \times 1 \times 1 = 1$**.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each – Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) – [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**

> **(a)** With the help of a suitable block diagram, describe the **step-by-step procedure for conducting a Process FMEA (PFMEA)** for a CNC turning operation producing a steel shaft. Mention the role of the cross-functional team and the RPN computation step in detail. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** For the following four potential failure modes identified in the above PFMEA, compute the RPN and prioritize them for action. State, with justification, which failure mode demands **immediate corrective action**. **(7 marks)**

| Failure Mode | Severity (S) | Occurrence (O) | Detection (D) |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| F1: Diameter out of tolerance | 8 | 5 | 4 |
| F2: Surface roughness high | 6 | 7 | 5 |
| F3: Cracks in shaft | 9 | 2 | 8 |
| F4: Burr at end | 3 | 6 | 3 |

---

**Model Answer:**

**Part (a) – 7 marks:**

**Step 1 – Assembling the Cross-Functional Team (1 mark):**
A PFMEA team typically comprises **5–9 members** representing Quality (team leader), Design, Manufacturing, Tooling, Supplier Quality, and Service. Each member must have **>5 years of domain expertise** to ensure realistic failure-mode identification. The team's role is to bring *multiple perspectives* on the same process – a single engineer can never anticipate all failure modes alone.

**Step 2 – Define Scope, Boundaries, and Customer (0.5 mark):**
For the CNC turning of the steel shaft, the *in-scope* items are: machine tool, tooling, fixturing, operator skill, and incoming material. The *customer* is the next assembly station that receives the shaft.

**Step 3 – Identify Functions (0.5 mark):**
The shaft must (i) achieve the design diameter with $\pm 0.05$ mm tolerance, (ii) achieve surface roughness $R_a \leq 1.6$ µm, (iii) be free from cracks, and (iv) be burr-free at both ends.

**Step 4 – Identify Failure Modes (1 mark):**
For each function, list all the ways it can fail. Example: function (i) can fail via "diameter oversize," "diameter undersize," "out-of-roundness," or "taper."

**Step 5 – Identify Effects and Causes (0.5 mark):**
For each failure mode, list the *effect* (consequence to the customer) and the *cause* (mechanism producing the failure). Example: "Diameter oversize" → Effect: "shaft does not fit in bearing housing"; Cause: "tool thermal drift during long cycle."

**Step 6 – Assign S, O, D Ratings (1 mark):**
Use the standard 1–10 scales. Severity reflects customer impact, Occurrence reflects failure-cause frequency, Detection reflects the ability of current controls (e.g., SPC charts, in-process gauges) to *catch* the failure *before* the customer receives the part.

**Step 7 – Compute RPN and Prioritize (1 mark):**
Apply the formula $RPN = S \times O \times D$. Rank failure modes from highest to lowest RPN. Set a threshold (typically $RPN \geq 100$ OR $S \geq 9$) above which action is mandatory.

**Step 8 – Recommend Actions and Re-evaluate (1 mark):**
Define specific actions, assign owners and due dates, implement the actions, and recompute RPN. Document the *before* and *after* RPN to demonstrate the effectiveness of the action.

**Step 9 – Iterate (0.5 mark):**
FMEA is a *living document*. Re-examine the FMEA whenever there is a design change, process change, supplier change, or recurrence of a field failure.

**Part (b) – 7 marks:**

**Computation of RPN for each failure mode:**

**F1: Diameter out of tolerance**

$$
RPN_{F1} = S \times O \times D = 8 \times 5 \times 4 = 160
$$

**F2: Surface roughness high**

$$
RPN_{F2} = 6 \times 7 \times 5 = 210
$$

**F3: Cracks in shaft**

$$
RPN_{F3} = 9 \times 2 \times 8 = 144
$$

**F4: Burr at end**

$$
RPN_{F4} = 3 \times 6 \times 3 = 54
$$

**Prioritization (ranked from highest to lowest RPN):**

| Rank | Failure Mode | RPN |
| :---: | :--- | :---: |
| 1 | F2 – Surface roughness high | 210 |
| 2 | F1 – Diameter out of tolerance | 160 |
| 3 | F3 – Cracks in shaft | 144 |
| 4 | F4 – Burr at end | 54 |

**Decision on Immediate Action (2 marks):**

Although **F2** has the highest RPN (210), the failure mode that demands **immediate corrective action is F3 (Cracks in shaft)** because its **Severity = 9**, which is **safety-critical** (a cracked shaft under load can lead to catastrophic mechanical failure and loss of life). Per the FMEA rule that **$S \geq 9$ overrides RPN comparison**, F3 must be actioned *first*, before F2 or F1.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students frequently write *"F2 with RPN 210 needs immediate action"* and lose 1.5–2 marks. The examiner expects the student to recognize the **Severity-Override Rule**: $S \geq 9$ is a *hard floor* that automatically elevates a failure mode to top priority, irrespective of RPN ranking.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) – Alternative Choice**

> **(a)** Differentiate between **Design FMEA (DFMEA)** and **Process FMEA (PFMEA)** in terms of purpose, scope, timing, and primary customer. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Explain the limitations of the classical RPN approach and describe how the **Action Priority (AP)** method (AIAG-VDA 2019) addresses these limitations. Illustrate with a numerical example showing two failure modes with identical RPN but different AP. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Answer:**

**Part (a) – 7 marks:**

| Dimension | DFMEA (1.75 mark) | PFMEA (1.75 mark) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Purpose** | Identify failure modes introduced by the *product design* itself | Identify failure modes introduced by the *manufacturing process* |
| **Scope** | Components, sub-assemblies, interfaces, software, materials | Machine tools, fixturing, tooling, operator actions, inspection methods |
| **Timing in Product Lifecycle** | Conducted during *Concept* and *Detailed Design* stages (B-sample / C-sample phase) | Conducted during *Process Planning* stage (pre-PPAP / pre-production) |
| **Primary Customer** | End-user of the product (driver, patient, passenger) | Internal next-station customer (assembly line, sub-supplier) |
| **Failure Causes** | Design deficiencies – geometry, material selection, tolerance stack-up, electrical overstress | Process variations – tool wear, machine calibration, operator error, environmental drift |
| **Detection Controls** | Design verification (FEA, testing, DFMEA reviews) | Process controls (SPC, gauges, poka-yoke, mistake-proofing) |
| **Owner of Worksheet** | Design Engineering | Manufacturing / Process Engineering |

**Key contrast (2 marks):** DFMEA is *design-centric* and precedes PFMEA. PFMEA *assumes* the design is frozen and focuses on *how* the design could be ruined by process variation. A design change after PFMEA invalidates the entire PFMEA worksheet.

**Part (b) – 7 marks:**

**Limitations of Classical RPN (3 marks):**

1. **RPN obscures risk asymmetry** – Two failure modes with $RPN = 200$ can have radically different risk profiles: e.g., $A: (S=10, O=10, D=2)$ vs. $B: (S=4, O=5, D=10)$. Classical RPN treats them as *equal*, but Mode A is *catastrophic* (Severity 10) while Mode B is *minor* (Severity 4).
2. **RPN is sensitive to arbitrary scale choice** – The 1–10 scales are ordinal, but RPN multiplication implies cardinal arithmetic – a methodological inconsistency.
3. **RPN thresholds are arbitrary** – The conventional "RPN $\geq 100$" threshold is not derived from first principles; it is a convention.
4. **RPN ignores Severity-Only Items** – A failure mode with $S = 10$ and very low $O$ may have a small RPN but must be actioned regardless.

**How AP Addresses These Limitations (2 marks):**
The AIAG-VDA 2019 Action Priority method uses a **predefined lookup matrix** of (S, O, D) → (H, M, L) classifications. The matrix is *non-linear* and *severity-biased*: any cell with $S \geq 9$ is automatically classified as **H**, regardless of O and D. This eliminates the RPN-arbitrariness problem and ensures safety-critical items are always prioritized.

**Numerical Example (2 marks):**

Consider two failure modes:

- **Mode A:** $S = 10, O = 5, D = 4$ → $RPN = 10 \times 5 \times 4 = 200$
- **Mode B:** $S = 4, O = 10, D = 5$ → $RPN = 4 \times 10 \times 5 = 200$

**Classical RPN verdict:** *Both are equal – same priority.*

**AP verdict (using AIAG-VDA matrix):**
- Mode A: $S = 10$ → **AP = H (High) – must act immediately.**
- Mode B: $S = 4, O = 10, D = 5$ → **AP = M (Medium) – act as resources permit.**

**Inference:** AP correctly elevates the safety-critical Mode A above the merely-frequent Mode B – a discrimination the classical RPN cannot make.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** When answering the AP question, students often *only* describe RPN limitations and forget to *numerically demonstrate* the AP advantage. The 2-mark numerical illustration is the differentiator between a 5-mark and 7-mark answer. Always end with a *concrete* $(S, O, D)$ pair that exposes the RPN paradox.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist – FMEA**

- **FMEA** = proactive, systematic, *forward-looking* quality tool used *before* failure occurs.
- **Three Variants:** SFMEA, DFMEA, PFMEA – each used at a different lifecycle stage.
- **Core Formula (memorize verbatim):** $RPN = S \times O \times D$ with $S, O, D \in [1, 10]$.
- **RPN range:** $1 \leq RPN \leq 1000$.
- **Conventional Action Threshold:** $RPN \geq 100$ **OR** $S \geq 9$ (severity override).
- **Scales are ordinal**, not cardinal – do not interpret $S = 8$ as "twice as bad as $S = 4$".
- **AIAG-VDA 2019 modern approach:** Replace RPN with **Action Priority (AP)** classified as **H / M / L** using a lookup matrix.
- **$S = 10$** = safety endangering operator; **$O = 10$** = failure almost inevitable; **$D = 10$** = no current control can detect the failure.
- **FMEA team:** 5–9 cross-functional experts, each with **>5 years of domain experience**.
- **FMEA is a *living document*** – must be updated on every design / process / supplier change.
- **Cost of poor FMEA:** recalls, warranty claims, safety incidents; **Cost of good FMEA:** engineer-hours during design (a fraction of the above).
- **Standards:** SAE J1739, IEC 60812, AIAG-VDA 2019, IATF 16949 Element 8.3.5.1.
- **For PFMEA answers:** emphasize *process-specific* failure causes (tool wear, calibration drift, operator error).
- **For DFMEA answers:** emphasize *design-specific* failure causes (geometry, material, tolerance stack-up, electrical overstress).
- **Always conclude a 14-mark answer** with a justification of *priority ranking* and a brief *action plan*.
- **Examiner loves** % RPN reduction calculations – learn the formula $\Delta RPN \% = \frac{RPN_{old} - RPN_{new}}{RPN_{old}} \times 100$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Analysis of Variance (ANOVA)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET758 - QUALITY ENGINEERING AND MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Analysis of Variance (ANOVA)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

**Analysis of Variance (ANOVA)** is a parametric statistical hypothesis-testing procedure developed by **Sir Ronald Aylmer Fisher** in 1920. It is used to determine whether the **means of three or more independent groups** are statistically significantly different from one another by partitioning the **Total Variability** observed in a dataset into components attributable to different sources of variation.

Mathematically, ANOVA tests the following pair of hypotheses:

$$H_0 : \mu_1 = \mu_2 = \mu_3 = \dots = \mu_k$$

$$H_1 : \text{At least one } \mu_i \text{ is different from the others}$$

Where $\mu_i$ denotes the population mean of the $i$-th group and $k$ is the total number of groups (treatments).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why ANOVA and not multiple t-tests?**
> Conducting multiple pairwise t-tests inflates the **Type I Error Rate** (probability of false positive). ANOVA controls this by testing all group means simultaneously under a single unified null hypothesis at a pre-specified significance level $\alpha$.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Classroom Test Score" Intuition

Imagine a Principal evaluating **three different teaching methods** (Method A, B, C) on student test scores. The Principal wants to know: *"Do these methods actually produce different learning outcomes, or is the observed variation just random noise?"*

- **Variation BETWEEN methods** = Are the class averages far apart? (e.g., A averages 85, B averages 70, C averages 60 → Big difference)
- **Variation WITHIN methods** = Even within the same method, students score differently due to individual ability, mood, etc. (random noise)

**ANOVA's Logical Core**: If the *Between-group* variation is **substantially larger** than the *Within-group* variation, then the teaching method genuinely matters. If both variations are comparable, the differences are just random noise.

This comparison is quantified using the **F-statistic** (named after Fisher):

$$F = \frac{\text{Variation BETWEEN Groups (Explained)}}{\text{Variation WITHIN Groups (Unexplained)}}$$

A large F-ratio ⇒ groups are genuinely different; a small F-ratio ⇒ observed differences are likely due to chance.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight for Quality Engineering**: In Six Sigma and process optimization, ANOVA answers the question: *"Which process input (X) is actually driving the variation in the output (Y)?"* — making it indispensable for **Design of Experiments (DOE)** and **Cause-and-Effect Analysis**.

---

## 1.3 Key Terminology and Symbols

| Symbol | Meaning |
| :--- | :--- |
| $k$ | Number of groups / treatments / levels |
| $n_i$ | Number of observations in the $i$-th group |
| $N$ | Total observations $= \sum_{i=1}^{k} n_i$ |
| $X_{ij}$ | The $j$-th observation in the $i$-th group |
| $\bar{X}_{i\cdot}$ | Sample mean of the $i$-th group |
| $\bar{X}_{..}$ | Grand mean (mean of all observations) |
| $SS_T$ | Total Sum of Squares |
| $SS_B$ (or $SS_{Tr}$) | Sum of Squares Between treatments |
| $SS_W$ (or $SS_E$) | Sum of Squares Within (Error) |

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic**: $SS_T = SS_B + SS_W$ (Total = Between + Within). This is the **Fundamental ANOVA Identity** that examiners love to test.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Underlying Logic — Variance Partitioning

The fundamental theorem of ANOVA states that the **total observed variability** in a dataset can be **additively decomposed** into two independent components:

$$SS_{Total} = SS_{Between} + SS_{Within}$$

**Step-by-step reasoning:**

1. **Grand Mean Calculation**: First, compute the overall average of all observations:
   $\bar{X}_{..} = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{k}\sum_{j=1}^{n_i} X_{ij}}{N}$

2. **Between-Group Variation**: Measures how much the **group means deviate** from the grand mean. Captures the effect of the treatment/factor.

3. **Within-Group Variation**: Measures how much **individual observations deviate** from their own group's mean. Captures random error/residual noise.

4. **F-Ratio Construction**: If the treatment has **no real effect**, both variances should be roughly equal estimates of the underlying population variance. A significantly larger $SS_B$ relative to $SS_W$ indicates a real treatment effect.

5. **Decision Rule**: Compare the calculated F-ratio with the critical value $F_{\alpha, (k-1), (N-k)}$. Reject $H_0$ if $F_{calc} > F_{crit}$.

---

## 2.2 Assumptions of One-Way ANOVA (KRUSKAL-WALLIS alternative if violated)

For the F-test to be valid, the following assumptions must hold:

1. **Normality**: Each group is drawn from a normally distributed population.
2. **Homogeneity of Variance (Homoscedasticity)**: All groups share a common population variance $\sigma^2$.
3. **Independence**: All observations are statistically independent.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Trap**: ANOVA does NOT require equal sample sizes ($n_i$ may differ). The test remains valid; only the formula structure is marginally affected.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates **every formula** required to solve KTU ESE problems on ANOVA. Memorize it thoroughly.

| Formula Number | Equation | Description |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| (1) | $SS_T = \sum_{i=1}^{k}\sum_{j=1}^{n_i} (X_{ij} - \bar{X}_{..})^2$ | Total Sum of Squares |
| (2) | $SS_B = \sum_{i=1}^{k} n_i (\bar{X}_{i\cdot} - \bar{X}_{..})^2$ | Between-group SS (Treatment) |
| (3) | $SS_W = \sum_{i=1}^{k}\sum_{j=1}^{n_i} (X_{ij} - \bar{X}_{i\cdot})^2$ | Within-group SS (Error) |
| (4) | $SS_T = SS_B + SS_W$ | Fundamental Partition Identity |
| (5) | $MS_B = \dfrac{SS_B}{k-1}$ | Mean Square Between |
| (6) | $MS_W = \dfrac{SS_W}{N-k}$ | Mean Square Within |
| (7) | $F_{calc} = \dfrac{MS_B}{MS_W}$ | F-test Statistic |
| (8) | $\eta^2 = \dfrac{SS_B}{SS_T}$ | Effect Size (Strength of Treatment) |
| (9) | $CV = \dfrac{\sqrt{MS_W}}{\bar{X}_{..}} \times 100\%$ | Coefficient of Variation |

### Degrees of Freedom (Critical for F-Table Entry)

| Source | Degrees of Freedom |
| :--- | :--- |
| Between (Treatment) | $k - 1$ |
| Within (Error) | $N - k$ |
| **Total** | $N - 1$ |

> [!TIP]
> **Sanity Check Rule**: $(k-1) + (N-k) = N-1$. If this identity fails, you have made a counting error.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In **Quality Engineering**, ANOVA is the workhorse of:

- **Design of Experiments (DOE)**: Determining which factor(s) most significantly affect a process output (yield, strength, hardness).
- **Multi-Vendor Comparison**: Comparing defect rates across 4 different suppliers to identify if supplier choice is a significant driver of quality.
- **Shift Analysis**: Comparing mean output quality across morning, evening, and night shifts.
- **Machinery Performance**: Comparing mean production across multiple CNC machines.
- **Six Sigma Green Belt Projects**: Validating root causes identified during the Analyze phase.

---

## 2.5 The Standard ANOVA Table (KTU Exam Format)

This is the **canonical output format** expected in KTU answers:

| Source of Variation | Sum of Squares | Degrees of Freedom | Mean Square | F-ratio |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Between (Treatment) | $SS_B$ | $k-1$ | $MS_B$ | $F = MS_B \slash MS_W$ |
| Within (Error) | $SS_W$ | $N-k$ | $MS_W$ | — |
| **Total** | $SS_T$ | $N-1$ | — | — |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Mathematical Derivation of the Fundamental Identity

We begin with the total variation, expressed as the squared deviation of each observation from the grand mean:

$$SS_T = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \sum_{j=1}^{n_i} (X_{ij} - \bar{X}_{..})^2$$

We now insert a clever algebraic maneuver — adding and subtracting the group mean $\bar{X}_{i\cdot}$:

$$SS_T = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \sum_{j=1}^{n_i} \left[(X_{ij} - \bar{X}_{i\cdot}) + (\bar{X}_{i\cdot} - \bar{X}_{..})\right]^2$$

Expanding the square using $(a+b)^2 = a^2 + 2ab + b^2$:

$$\begin{aligned}
SS_T &= \sum_{i=1}^{k} \sum_{j=1}^{n_i} (X_{ij} - \bar{X}_{i\cdot})^2 \\
&\quad + 2 \sum_{i=1}^{k} \sum_{j=1}^{n_i} (X_{ij} - \bar{X}_{i\cdot})(\bar{X}_{i\cdot} - \bar{X}_{..}) \\
&\quad + \sum_{i=1}^{k} \sum_{j=1}^{n_i} (\bar{X}_{i\cdot} - \bar{X}_{..})^2
\end{aligned}$$

**Examining the middle cross-term:**

$$\sum_{j=1}^{n_i} (X_{ij} - \bar{X}_{i\cdot}) = n_i \bar{X}_{i\cdot} - n_i \bar{X}_{i\cdot} = 0$$

Because the sum of deviations from the mean is identically zero, the cross-term vanishes entirely. We are left with:

$$SS_T = \underbrace{\sum_{i=1}^{k} n_i (\bar{X}_{i\cdot} - \bar{X}_{..})^2}_{SS_B} + \underbrace{\sum_{i=1}^{k} \sum_{j=1}^{n_i} (X_{ij} - \bar{X}_{i\cdot})^2}_{SS_W}$$

This **rigorously proves** the fundamental ANOVA identity: $SS_T = SS_B + SS_W$.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Full ANOVA Computation

**Problem Statement**: A quality engineer wants to compare the tensile strength (in MPa) of steel rods produced by 4 different manufacturing methods. Five rods are tested per method. Test at $\alpha = 0.05$ whether the mean tensile strengths differ significantly.

**Raw Data:**

| Method A | Method B | Method C | Method D |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 23 | 30 | 26 | 21 |
| 25 | 32 | 28 | 23 |
| 27 | 28 | 25 | 22 |
| 22 | 31 | 27 | 20 |
| 24 | 29 | 29 | 24 |

**Step 1: Identify parameters**

- $k = 4$ (methods)
- $n_i = 5$ for each $i$
- $N = 20$ total observations
- $\alpha = 0.05$

**Step 2: Compute group means**

$$\bar{X}_{A} = \frac{23 + 25 + 27 + 22 + 24}{5} = \frac{121}{5} = 24.2$$

$$\bar{X}_{B} = \frac{30 + 32 + 28 + 31 + 29}{5} = \frac{150}{5} = 30.0$$

$$\bar{X}_{C} = \frac{26 + 28 + 25 + 27 + 29}{5} = \frac{135}{5} = 27.0$$

$$\bar{X}_{D} = \frac{21 + 23 + 22 + 20 + 24}{5} = \frac{110}{5} = 22.0$$

**Step 3: Compute the grand mean**

$$\bar{X}_{..} = \frac{121 + 150 + 135 + 110}{20} = \frac{516}{20} = 25.8$$

**Step 4: Compute $SS_B$ (Between-Group Sum of Squares)**

Using formula (2): $SS_B = \sum_{i=1}^{k} n_i (\bar{X}_{i\cdot} - \bar{X}_{..})^2$

$$\begin{aligned}
SS_B &= 5(24.2 - 25.8)^2 + 5(30.0 - 25.8)^2 + 5(27.0 - 25.8)^2 + 5(22.0 - 25.8)^2 \\
&= 5(-1.6)^2 + 5(4.2)^2 + 5(1.2)^2 + 5(-3.8)^2 \\
&= 5(2.56) + 5(17.64) + 5(1.44) + 5(14.44) \\
&= 12.80 + 88.20 + 7.20 + 72.20 \\
&= 180.40
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5: Compute $SS_W$ (Within-Group Sum of Squares)**

We compute each group's contribution separately.

*Method A*: $(23 - 24.2)^2 + (25 - 24.2)^2 + (27 - 24.2)^2 + (22 - 24.2)^2 + (24 - 24.2)^2$

$$= 1.44 + 0.64 + 7.84 + 4.84 + 0.04 = 14.80$$

*Method B*: $(30 - 30)^2 + (32 - 30)^2 + (28 - 30)^2 + (31 - 30)^2 + (29 - 30)^2$

$$= 0 + 4 + 4 + 1 + 1 = 10.00$$

*Method C*: $(26 - 27)^2 + (28 - 27)^2 + (25 - 27)^2 + (27 - 27)^2 + (29 - 27)^2$

$$= 1 + 1 + 4 + 0 + 4 = 10.00$$

*Method D*: $(21 - 22)^2 + (23 - 22)^2 + (22 - 22)^2 + (20 - 22)^2 + (24 - 22)^2$

$$= 1 + 1 + 0 + 4 + 4 = 10.00$$

Summing across groups:

$$SS_W = 14.80 + 10.00 + 10.00 + 10.00 = 44.80$$

**Step 6: Verify the Fundamental Identity**

$$SS_T = SS_B + SS_W = 180.40 + 44.80 = 225.20$$

*(Independent computation of $SS_T$ from raw data confirms this value.)*

**Step 7: Compute Degrees of Freedom**

$$df_{Between} = k - 1 = 4 - 1 = 3$$

$$df_{Within} = N - k = 20 - 4 = 16$$

$$df_{Total} = N - 1 = 20 - 1 = 19$$

**Verification**: $3 + 16 = 19 = df_{Total}$ ✓

**Step 8: Compute Mean Squares**

$$MS_B = \frac{SS_B}{df_{Between}} = \frac{180.40}{3} = 60.1333$$

$$MS_W = \frac{SS_W}{df_{Within}} = \frac{44.80}{16} = 2.8000$$

**Step 9: Compute F-Statistic**

$$F_{calc} = \frac{MS_B}{MS_W} = \frac{60.1333}{2.8000} = 21.4762$$

**Step 10: Critical Value and Decision**

From the F-distribution table at $\alpha = 0.05$ with $(3, 16)$ degrees of freedom:

$$F_{crit}(0.05, 3, 16) = 3.24$$

**Decision Rule**: Since $F_{calc} = 21.48 > F_{crit} = 3.24$, we **reject $H_0$**.

**Engineering Conclusion**: There is statistically significant evidence at the 5% level that the mean tensile strengths produced by the four manufacturing methods are **not all equal**. At least one method produces a fundamentally different strength profile.

---

## 3.3 Final ANOVA Summary Table

| Source of Variation | Sum of Squares | df | Mean Square | F-ratio | F-critical |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Between (Methods) | 180.40 | 3 | 60.13 | **21.48** | 3.24 |
| Within (Error) | 44.80 | 16 | 2.80 | — | — |
| **Total** | **225.20** | **19** | — | — | — |

---

## 3.4 Effect Size and Strength Quantification

Beyond mere statistical significance, the engineer should quantify **how strong** the treatment effect is:

$$\eta^2 = \frac{SS_B}{SS_T} = \frac{180.40}{225.20} = 0.8011 \approx 80.11\%$$

**Interpretation**: Approximately **80.11%** of the total observed variation in tensile strength is attributable to the choice of manufacturing method. This is a **very large effect size** — the manufacturing method is overwhelmingly the dominant source of variation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In KTU answers, always report both the F-test decision AND the effect size interpretation. Examiners allocate marks for the *practical* engineering insight, not just the mechanical computation.

---

## 3.5 Post-Hoc Testing (Conceptual Note)

When $H_0$ is rejected, ANOVA tells us *that* a difference exists but not *where*. **Post-hoc tests** (Tukey's HSD, Bonferroni, Scheffé) are required for pairwise comparisons. KTU typically only requires stating this limitation and naming the appropriate test.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conceptual Flowchart — The ANOVA Decision Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Define Research Question] --> B[Collect k Groups of Data]
    B --> C{Verify ANOVA Assumptions}
    C -->|Normality OK| D[Shapiro-Wilk Test]
    C -->|Homoscedasticity OK| E[Levene's Test]
    C -->|Independence OK| F[Random Sampling Design]
    D --> G[All Assumptions Met]
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H[Calculate Group Means and Grand Mean]
    H --> I[Compute SS_B, SS_W, SS_T]
    I --> J[Calculate df for Each Source]
    J --> K[Compute MS_B and MS_W]
    K --> L[Calculate F_calc = MS_B / MS_W]
    L --> M[Look up F_crit from F-Table]
    M --> N{Compare F_calc vs F_crit}
    N -->|F_calc > F_crit| O[Reject H0: Significant Difference Exists]
    N -->|F_calc ≤ F_crit| P[Fail to Reject H0: No Significant Difference]
    O --> Q[Run Post-Hoc Tests for Pairwise Comparison]
    P --> R[Conclude Groups are Statistically Similar]
    Q --> S[End: Engineering Decision]
    R --> S
```

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Variance Partitioning Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Total Observed Variation in Dataset] --> B[Partitioning Process]
    B --> C[SS Between Groups]
    B --> D[SS Within Groups]
    C --> E[Explained Variation - Due to Treatment Factor]
    D --> F[Unexplained Variation - Random Error Noise]
    E --> G[Numerator of F-Ratio]
    F --> H[Denominator of F-Ratio]
    G --> I[F Statistic Computation]
    H --> I
    I --> J[Hypothesis Testing Engine]
    J --> K[Decision: Reject or Retain H0]
```

---

## 4.3 ANOVA Table Construction Flow

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Raw Data Xij] --> B[Compute Group Sums]
    A --> C[Compute Grand Sum]
    B --> D[Group Means Xi bar]
    C --> E[Grand Mean X double bar]
    D --> F[SS Between]
    D --> G[SS Within]
    E --> F
    A --> G
    F --> H[Mean Square Between]
    G --> I[Mean Square Within]
    H --> J[F Ratio]
    I --> J
    J --> K[Final ANOVA Table]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**State and explain the fundamental assumptions underlying the one-way ANOVA procedure.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The three core assumptions of one-way ANOVA are:

1. **Normality**: The observations within each group must be drawn from a normally distributed population. This is typically verified using the Shapiro-Wilk test or by inspecting a normal Q-Q plot.

2. **Homogeneity of Variance (Homoscedasticity)**: All $k$ groups must share a common population variance $\sigma^2$. This is tested using Levene's test or Bartlett's test. Violation leads to use of Welch's ANOVA instead.

3. **Independence**: Each observation must be statistically independent of all others. This is ensured by appropriate random sampling and randomized experimental design.

When these assumptions are violated, non-parametric alternatives such as the **Kruskal-Wallis H test** are recommended.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between $SS_B$ and $SS_W$ in the context of ANOVA. Why is their ratio important?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | $SS_B$ (Between-Group Sum of Squares) | $SS_W$ (Within-Group Sum of Squares) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Measures | Variation among group means | Variation within each group |
| Source | Treatment effect (explained) | Random error (unexplained) |
| Formula | $\sum n_i (\bar{X}_{i\cdot} - \bar{X}_{..})^2$ | $\sum \sum (X_{ij} - \bar{X}_{i\cdot})^2$ |
| df | $k - 1$ | $N - k$ |

**Importance of the Ratio**: The ratio $F = MS_B / MS_W$ compares the explained variation (due to treatment) against the unexplained variation (random noise). A significantly large F-ratio indicates the treatment has a genuine effect; a value near unity indicates the treatment effect is indistinguishable from random noise.

---

## PART B — Extended Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Internal Choice Rule (KTU ESE)**: Students answer **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** A study compares the effect of **three catalysts** on reaction yield (in grams). Five trials are conducted per catalyst. The data are summarized below:

- Catalyst 1: $\bar{X}_1 = 45.2$, $SS_1 = 124.0$
- Catalyst 2: $\bar{X}_2 = 52.8$, $SS_2 = 98.5$
- Catalyst 3: $\bar{X}_3 = 48.6$, $SS_3 = 110.5$
- Grand Mean: $\bar{X}_{..} = 48.87$, $N = 15$, $k = 3$

Construct the complete ANOVA table and test at $\alpha = 0.05$ whether the catalysts differ significantly in mean yield. **[7 marks]**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Compute $SS_B$** using formula (2):

$$SS_B = \sum n_i (\bar{X}_{i\cdot} - \bar{X}_{..})^2$$

$$\begin{aligned}
SS_B &= 5(45.2 - 48.87)^2 + 5(52.8 - 48.87)^2 + 5(48.6 - 48.87)^2 \\
&= 5(-3.67)^2 + 5(3.93)^2 + 5(-0.27)^2 \\
&= 5(13.4689) + 5(15.4449) + 5(0.0729) \\
&= 67.3445 + 77.2245 + 0.3645 \\
&= 144.9335
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2: Compute $SS_W$** by summing the group-level sum of squares:

$$SS_W = SS_1 + SS_2 + SS_3 = 124.0 + 98.5 + 110.5 = 333.0$$

**Step 3: Compute $SS_T$** using identity (4):

$$SS_T = SS_B + SS_W = 144.93 + 333.00 = 477.93$$

**Step 4: Compute degrees of freedom:**

$$df_B = k - 1 = 3 - 1 = 2, \quad df_W = N - k = 15 - 3 = 12$$

**Step 5: Compute Mean Squares:**

$$MS_B = \frac{144.93}{2} = 72.467, \quad MS_W = \frac{333.00}{12} = 27.750$$

**Step 6: Compute F-statistic:**

$$F_{calc} = \frac{MS_B}{MS_W} = \frac{72.467}{27.750} = 2.612$$

**Step 7: Critical Value:** $F_{0.05, 2, 12} = 3.89$

**Step 8: ANOVA Table:**

| Source | SS | df | MS | F-ratio | F-critical |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Between | 144.93 | 2 | 72.47 | 2.61 | 3.89 |
| Within | 333.00 | 12 | 27.75 | — | — |
| **Total** | **477.93** | **14** | — | — | — |

**Step 9: Decision:** Since $F_{calc} = 2.61 < F_{crit} = 3.89$, we **fail to reject $H_0$**. There is insufficient evidence at the 5% level to conclude that the catalysts differ in mean reaction yield.

---

**(b)** Explain the concept of **partitioning of total variability** in ANOVA. Demonstrate the identity $SS_T = SS_B + SS_W$ mathematically. **[7 marks]**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

The total variability in a dataset arises from two fundamentally distinct sources: the systematic effect of the treatment/factor being studied, and the inherent random noise affecting every observation. ANOVA's central insight is that these two sources can be **separated and quantified independently** through a process called **partitioning of total variability**.

We start with the squared deviation of each observation $X_{ij}$ from the grand mean $\bar{X}_{..}$, sum these over all observations, and algebraically decompose it:

$$SS_T = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \sum_{j=1}^{n_i} (X_{ij} - \bar{X}_{..})^2$$

Adding and subtracting the group mean $\bar{X}_{i\cdot}$ inside the squared term:

$$SS_T = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \sum_{j=1}^{n_i} \left[(X_{ij} - \bar{X}_{i\cdot}) + (\bar{X}_{i\cdot} - \bar{X}_{..})\right]^2$$

Expanding using $(a+b)^2 = a^2 + 2ab + b^2$:

$$\begin{aligned}
SS_T &= \sum_{i=1}^{k} \sum_{j=1}^{n_i} (X_{ij} - \bar{X}_{i\cdot})^2 \\
&\quad + 2 \sum_{i=1}^{k} (\bar{X}_{i\cdot} - \bar{X}_{..}) \underbrace{\sum_{j=1}^{n_i} (X_{ij} - \bar{X}_{i\cdot})}_{= \, 0 \text{ by definition of mean}} \\
&\quad + \sum_{i=1}^{k} \sum_{j=1}^{n_i} (\bar{X}_{i\cdot} - \bar{X}_{..})^2
\end{aligned}$$

The cross-term vanishes (sum of deviations from mean is zero). Simplifying the third term using $\sum_{j=1}^{n_i} 1 = n_i$:

$$\begin{aligned}
SS_T &= \underbrace{\sum_{i=1}^{k} \sum_{j=1}^{n_i} (X_{ij} - \bar{X}_{i\cdot})^2}_{SS_W \, \text{(Within)}} + \underbrace{\sum_{i=1}^{k} n_i (\bar{X}_{i\cdot} - \bar{X}_{..})^2}_{SS_B \, \text{(Between)}}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{\therefore SS_T = SS_B + SS_W} \quad \blacksquare$$

**Engineering Interpretation**: This identity allows the quality engineer to attribute a precise **percentage** of total process variation to the controllable factor (treatment) versus residual noise, directly enabling **Pareto-style prioritization** of root causes.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** A manufacturing company uses **four machines** to produce identical components. To investigate whether the machines differ in mean output quality (measured as surface roughness in $\mu m$, lower is better), 5 components are sampled from each machine. The sample means and variances are:

| Machine | Sample Mean | Sample Variance |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| M1 | 12.4 | 4.2 |
| M2 | 14.8 | 5.1 |
| M3 | 11.6 | 3.8 |
| M4 | 13.2 | 4.5 |
| Grand Mean | 13.0 | — |

Test at $\alpha = 0.01$ whether there is a significant difference in mean surface roughness among the machines. **[7 marks]**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Parameters**

$k = 4$ machines, $n_i = 5$, $N = 20$, $\alpha = 0.01$

**Step 2: Compute $SS_B$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
SS_B &= 5(12.4 - 13.0)^2 + 5(14.8 - 13.0)^2 + 5(11.6 - 13.0)^2 + 5(13.2 - 13.0)^2 \\
&= 5(-0.6)^2 + 5(1.8)^2 + 5(-1.4)^2 + 5(0.2)^2 \\
&= 5(0.36) + 5(3.24) + 5(1.96) + 5(0.04) \\
&= 1.80 + 16.20 + 9.80 + 0.20 \\
&= 28.00
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Compute $SS_W$** using $SS_i = (n_i - 1) s_i^2$ for each group:

$$\begin{aligned}
SS_W &= (5-1)(4.2) + (5-1)(5.1) + (5-1)(3.8) + (5-1)(4.5) \\
&= 4(4.2) + 4(5.1) + 4(3.8) + 4(4.5) \\
&= 16.80 + 20.40 + 15.20 + 18.00 \\
&= 70.40
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4: Total Sum of Squares**

$$SS_T = SS_B + SS_W = 28.00 + 70.40 = 98.40$$

**Step 5: Degrees of Freedom**

$$df_B = k - 1 = 3, \quad df_W = N - k = 16$$

**Step 6: Mean Squares**

$$MS_B = \frac{28.00}{3} = 9.333, \quad MS_W = \frac{70.40}{16} = 4.400$$

**Step 7: F-Statistic**

$$F_{calc} = \frac{9.333}{4.400} = 2.121$$

**Step 8: Critical Value** at $\alpha = 0.01$: $F_{0.01, 3, 16} = 5.29$

**Step 9: Decision:** Since $F_{calc} = 2.12 < F_{crit} = 5.29$, we **fail to reject $H_0$** at the 1% significance level. There is insufficient evidence to conclude that the machines differ significantly in mean surface roughness.

**Engineering Recommendation**: Since the machines appear statistically equivalent in output quality, selection among them may be based on other criteria such as cost, energy efficiency, or maintenance overhead rather than quality alone.

---

**(b)** Discuss the **assumptions of ANOVA**. What non-parametric alternative is used when these assumptions are violated? **[7 marks]**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

The validity of the F-test in ANOVA rests on three foundational assumptions:

**1. Normality of Residuals**: Each group's observations must be drawn from a normally distributed population. This applies to the residuals (deviations from group means), not the raw data directly.

*Verification Methods*:
- **Shapiro-Wilk Test** (best for small samples, $n < 50$)
- **Anderson-Darling Test**
- Normal Q-Q plots (graphical inspection)
- Kolmogorov-Smirnov test

**2. Homogeneity of Variance (Homoscedasticity)**: All $k$ populations must share a common variance $\sigma^2$. This is critical because the F-ratio pools all within-group variances into a single $MS_W$ estimate.

*Verification Methods*:
- **Levene's Test** (robust to non-normality)
- **Bartlett's Test** (sensitive to non-normality)
- **Hartley's F-max Test** (simple but restrictive)

**3. Independence of Observations**: Each measurement must be statistically independent. Violations occur with time-series data, clustered samples, or repeated measures.

*Verification Methods*:
- Runs Test
- Durbin-Watson Statistic (for ordered data)

**Non-Parametric Alternative — Kruskal-Wallis H Test**:

When one or more assumptions are violated (especially normality), the **Kruskal-Wallis H test** is the recommended non-parametric equivalent of one-way ANOVA. Its procedure:

1. Pool all $N$ observations from all groups together.
2. Rank them from 1 (smallest) to $N$ (largest), handling ties by averaging ranks.
3. Sum the ranks within each group: $R_i$.
4. Compute the H-statistic:

$$H = \frac{12}{N(N+1)} \sum_{i=1}^{k} \frac{R_i^2}{n_i} - 3(N+1)$$

5. For large samples, $H$ follows approximately a $\chi^2$ distribution with $k-1$ degrees of freedom. Reject $H_0$ if $H > \chi^2_{\alpha, k-1}$.

**Engineering Significance**: In real-world quality data (e.g., defect counts, failure times), normality is often violated. Knowing the Kruskal-Wallis alternative equips the engineer to perform robust group comparisons without distributional assumptions.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Missing the ANOVA Table Format**: Students often compute $SS_B$, $SS_W$, and $F$ correctly but **forget to present them in the standard ANOVA table format**. Examiners explicitly allocate 2 marks for the table layout.
>
> 2. **Degrees of Freedom Error**: Confusing $df_{Between}$ with $df_{Total}$. Remember: $df_{Between} = k - 1$, $df_{Within} = N - k$, $df_{Total} = N - 1$.
>
> 3. **F-Table Lookup Mistakes**: Students read the F-table incorrectly (rows and columns swapped). Critical: $df_1$ (numerator) corresponds to Between-Treatment; $df_2$ (denominator) corresponds to Within-Error.
>
> 4. **No Decision Statement**: Simply computing $F_{calc}$ without explicitly stating the comparison $F_{calc} \gtrless F_{crit}$ and the decision "Reject $H_0$" or "Fail to reject $H_0$" loses 1–2 marks.
>
> 5. **No Engineering Interpretation**: Computing the statistics correctly but failing to translate them into a **practical quality-engineering conclusion** loses the application-oriented marks.
>
> 6. **Partition Identity Confusion**: Students sometimes calculate $SS_T$ independently using deviations from the grand mean and obtain a slightly different value due to rounding, then panic. Use the identity $SS_T = SS_B + SS_W$ as a verification tool.
>
> 7. **Effect Size Omission**: Modern KTU examiners (under NEP 2020 / OBE framework) value reporting $\eta^2$ to communicate the *strength* of the effect, not just *whether* it exists.

---

## TOPIC RECAP — Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following 12 points constitute the absolute minimum competency required to score full marks on ANOVA in any KTU examination.**

- [ ] ANOVA tests equality of **three or more group means** simultaneously under a single null hypothesis $H_0: \mu_1 = \mu_2 = \dots = \mu_k$.
- [ ] The **fundamental identity** is $SS_T = SS_B + SS_W$ — Total = Between + Within.
- [ ] $SS_B$ quantifies variation **among group means** (treatment effect).
- [ ] $SS_W$ quantifies variation **within groups** (random error).
- [ ] Degrees of freedom: $df_B = k-1$, $df_W = N-k$, $df_T = N-1$.
- [ ] Mean Squares are obtained by dividing each $SS$ by its respective $df$.
- [ ] The **F-statistic** is $F = MS_B / MS_W$.
- [ ] Under $H_0$, $F$ follows an F-distribution with $(k-1, N-k)$ degrees of freedom.
- [ ] Decision Rule: Reject $H_0$ if $F_{calc} > F_{\alpha, k-1, N-k}$.
- [ ] Three core assumptions: **Normality, Homoscedasticity, Independence**.
- [ ] Non-parametric alternative when assumptions fail: **Kruskal-Wallis H test**.
- [ ] **Effect size** $\eta^2 = SS_B / SS_T$ quantifies the strength of the treatment effect.

> [!TIP]
> **One-Sentence Mnemonic for ANOVA Logic**: *"If the gap between groups is much bigger than the noise inside groups, the treatment is doing something real."* — This captures the essence of the F-test in plain language and helps during oral examinations and viva voce.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

> - **Module:** Module 4

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Design of Experiments (DOE)** is a systematic, statistical methodology used to determine the causal relationship between a set of controllable input variables (**factors**) and one or more measurable output variables (**responses**). It involves the planned, structured execution of experiments using principles of **randomization**, **replication**, and **blocking** to extract maximum information with minimum resource consumption.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Exact Terminology)**
> DOE is a planned approach to experimentation wherein the factors affecting a process are deliberately varied, the resulting changes in the response are observed, and the data is analyzed statistically to draw objective and valid conclusions.

## 1.2 Key Terminology (KTU Board-Favourite List)

| Term | Meaning | Industrial Example |
|------|---------|-------------------|
| **Factor** | An independent controllable variable | Temperature, Pressure, Speed |
| **Level** | A specific setting/value of a factor | 80°C, 100°C, 120°C |
| **Response** | The dependent output variable measured | Yield %, Tensile Strength |
| **Treatment** | A specific combination of factor levels | (Temp=100°C, Pressure=5 bar) |
| **Replication** | Repeating an experiment under identical conditions | Running the same trial 3 times |
| **Randomization** | Allocating treatments to runs in random order | To nullify bias from external noise |
| **Blocking** | Grouping similar experimental units | Running day-shift and night-shift separately |
| **Experimental Error** | The inherent variability in the response | Pure chance fluctuation |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Masterchef Kitchen" Analogy**
> Imagine you are a chef trying to perfect a recipe for a cake. You suspect three things matter: oven temperature, baking time, and sugar quantity. If you try changing everything at once ("Stir-Fry Method"), you will never know which change made the cake better or worse. **DOE is the scientific cooking method** — you change one ingredient at a time (or in a planned combination), record the taste score (response), and statistically prove which ingredient *truly* matters. The oven *factor* has three *levels* (150°C, 175°C, 200°C), and you randomly decide which cake gets baked at which temperature to avoid bias.

## 1.4 Foundational Principles of DOE (The Three Pillars)

1. **Randomization**: The order of experimental runs is randomized. This protects against hidden variables (e.g., humidity drift, machine warm-up) biasing the result.
2. **Replication**: Running the experiment multiple times at the same factor levels. This allows estimation of **experimental error** and improves the precision of the mean.
3. **Blocking**: Used to account for known sources of nuisance variability. Units are grouped into homogeneous blocks, and treatments are compared *within* each block.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for the Three Pillars: R-R-B (Read-Rule-Book)**
> **R**andomization, **R**eplication, **B**locking — KTU frequently asks these as 3-mark direct questions.
